shell: The hard and stony covering of certain fruits and of certain animals; as the shell of a nut; the shell of an oyster or lobster. the shells of animals are crustaceous or testaceous; crustaceous as that of the lobster, and testaceous as that of the oyster and clam.
The outer coat of an egg.
The outer part of a house unfinished. We say of a building that wants the interior timbers or finishing, that it is a mere shell.
An instrument of music, like testudo in Latin; the first lyre being make, it is said, by drawing strings over a tortoise shell.
Oute ror superficial part; as the shell of religion.
shelled: Deprived of the shell; also, separated from the ear; as, shelled corn or maiz.
shell-fish: An aquatic animal whose covering consists of a shell, crustaceous or testaceous; as lobsters, crabs, oysters, clams
shelling: Taking off the shell; casting the external hard covering; separating from hte husk and falling.
Separating from the ear, as maiz.
shell-work: Work composed of shells, or adorned with them.
shelly: Abounding with shells; as the shelly shore.
Consisting of shells. Lobsters disengage themselves from their shelly prisons.
shelter: That which covers or defends from injury or annoyance. A house is a shelter from rain and other inclemencies of the weather; the foliage of a tree is a shelter from the rays of the sun. The healing plant shall aid, From storms a shelter, and from heat a shade. Pope.
The state of being covered and protected; protection; security, Who into shelter takes their tender bloom. Young.
He that defends or guards from danger.

To cover from violence, injury, annoyance or attack; as a valley sheltered from the north wind by a mountain. Those ruins shelter’d once his sacred head. Dryden . We besought the deep shelter to us. Milton.
To defend; to protect from danger; to secure or render safe; to harbor. What endless shall you gain, to save and shelter Troy’s unhappy train?
sheltered: Covered from injury or annoyance; defended; protected.
sheltering: Covering from injury or annoyance; protecting.
shelterless: Destitute of shelter or protection; without home or refuge. Now sad and shelterless perhaps she lies. Rowel .
sheltie: A small but strong horse in Scotland; so called from Shetland, where it is produced.
shelve: shelv. To place on a shelf or on shelves.
shelving: or a. Inclining; sloping; having declivity. With rocks and shelving arches vaulted round.
shelvy: Full of rocks or sand banks; shallow; as a shelvy shore.
shemitic: Pertaining to Shem, the son of Noah. The Shemetic languages are Chaldee, Syriac, Arabic, Hebrew, Samaria, Ethiopic and Old Phoenician.
shend: To injure, mar or spoil. Obs. That much I fear my body willbe shent.
To blamerevile, degrade. The famous name of knighthood foully shend. Spenser.
To overpower or surpass. She pass’d the rest as Cynthia doth shend . The lesser stars.
shepherd: Aman employed in tending, feeding and gaurding sheep in the pasture.

The pastor of a parish, church or congregation; a minister of the gospel who superintends a church or parish, and gived instruction in spiritual things. God and Christ are in Scripture dinominated Shepherds, as they lead, protect and govern their people, and provide for their wilfare.
shepherdess: A woman that tends sheep; hence, a rural lass. She put herself into the garb of a sheperdess.
shepherdish: Resembling a shepherd; suiting a shepherd; pastoral; rustic.
sherbet: A drink composed of water, lemon juice and sugar, sometimes with perfumed cakes dissolved in it, with an infusion of some drops of rose water. Another kind made with violets, honey, juice of rasins
sherd: A pragment; usually written shard, which see.
sherif: An officer in each county, to whom is entrusted the execution of the laws. In England, sherifs are appointed by the king. In the United States, sherifs are elected by legislature or by the citizens, or appointed and commissioned by the executive of the state. The office of sherif in England is judicial and ministerial. In the United States it is mostly or wholly ministerial. The sherif, by himself or his deputies, executes civil and criminal process throughout the county, has charge of the jail and prisoners, attends courts and keeps the peace.
sherifalty: The office or jurisdiction of sherif.
sherifdom: The office or jurisdiction of sherif.
sherifship: The office or jurisdiction of sherif.
sherifwick: The office or jurisdiction of sherif.
sherriffe: The title of a descendant of Mohammed by Hassan Ibn Ali.
sherry: A species of wine; so called from Xeres in Spain, where it is made. Shew, Shewed, Shewn.
shew-bread: Among the Jews, bread of exhibition; the loaves of bread which the priest of the week placed before the Lord, on the golden table in the sanctuary. They were shaped like a brick, were ten palms long and five wide, weighing about eight pounds each. They were made of fine flour unleavened, and changed every sabbath. The loaves were twelve in number, and represented the twelve tribes of Israel. They were to be eaten by the priest only.
shibboleth: A word which was made the criterion by which to distinguish the Ephraimites not being able to pronounce the letter sh, pronounced the word sibboleth. See Judges 12:6. Hence,
The criterion of a party; or that which distinguishes one party from another; and usually some peculiarity in things of little importance.
shide: A piece split off; a cleft; a piece; a billet of wood; a splinter.
sheild: A broad piece of defensive armor; a buckler; used in war for the protection of the body. The shields of the ancients were of different shapes and sizes, triangular, square, ovalmade of lether or wood covered with lether, and borne on the left arm. This species of armor was a good defence angainst arrows, darts, spearsbut would not protect against bullets.

Defense; shelter; protection; or the person that defends or protects; as a chief, the ornament and the shield of the nation. Fear not, Abram; I am they shield and thy exceedind great reward. Genesis 15:1.
In heraldry, the escutcheon or field on which are placed the bearings in coats of arms.
shield: To cover, as with a shield; to cover from dasnger; to defend; to protect; to secure form assault or injury. To see the son the vanquish’d father shield. Hear one that comes to shield his injur’d honor. Smith.
To ward off; to defend against; as clothes shield one from the cold.
shielded: Covered, as with a shield; defended; protected.
shielding: Covering, as with a shield; defending from attack or injury; protected.
shift: To move; to change place or position. Vegetables are not able to shift and seek nutriment.
To change its direction; to vary; as, the wind shifted from south to west.
To change; to give place to other things.
To change clothes particularly the under garment or chemise.

To resort to expedients for a livelihood, or for accomplishing a purpose; to move from one thing to another, and to seize one expedient when anohter fails. Men in distress will look to themselves, and leave their companions to shift as well as they can. L’Estrange.
shifted: Changed from one place or position to another.
shifter: One that shifts; the person that plays tricks or practices artifice.
In ships, a person employed to assist the ship’s cook in washing, steeping and shifting the salt provisions.
shifting: Changing place or position; reporting from one expedient to another.
shiftless: Destitute of expedients, or not resorting to successful expedients; wanting means to act or live; as a shiftless fellow.
shill: To put under cover; to sheal.
shilling: An English silver coin equal to twelve pence, or the twentieth part of a pound. The English shilling, or shilling sterling, is equivilent to nearly 22 cents, hundredths, money of the United States. Our ancestors introduced the name with the coin into this country, but by depreciation the value of the shilling shrunk in New England and Virginia one fourth, or to a fraction less than 17 cents, in New York to 12 1/2 cents, in Pennsylvania, New Jersey and Maryland to about 11 cents.

An English silver coin equal to twelve pence, or the twentieth part of a pound. The English shilling, or shilling sterling, is equivilent to nearly 22 cents, 22 hundredths, money of the United States. Our ancestors introduced the name with the coin into this country, but by depreciation the value of the shilling shrunk in New England and Virginia one fourth, or to a fraction less than 17 cents, in New York to 12 1/2 cents, in Pennsylvania, New Jersey and Maryland to about 11 cents. This denomination of money still subsists in the United States, although there is no coin of that value current, except the Spanish coin of 12 1/2/ cents, which is a shilling in the money in the state of New York. Since the adoption of the present coins of the United States, eagles, dollars, centsthe use of the shilling is continued only by habit.
shin: The fore part of the leg, particularly of the human leg; the fore part of the crural bone, called tibia. This bone being only covered by the skin, may be named for that circumstance; skin-bone; or it may be formed from the root of chine
shine: To emit rays of light; to give light; to beam with steady radiance; to exhibit lightness or splendor; as, the sun shines by day; the moon shines by night. Shining differs from sparkling, glistening, glittering, as it usually implies a steady radiation or emission of light, whereas the latter words usually imply irregular or interrupted radiation. This distinction is not always not always observed, and we may say, the fixed stars shine, as well as they sparkle. But we never say the sun or the moon sparkles.
To be bright; to be lively and animated; to be brilliant. Let thine eyes shine forth in their full luster. Denham.
To be unclouded; as, the moon shines.
To be glossy or bright, as silk. Fish with their fins and shining scales. Milton.
To be gay or splendid. So proud she shined in her princely state. Spenser.
shingle: A thin board sawed or rived for covering buildings. Shingles are of different lengths, with one end much thinner than the other for lapping. They are used for covering roofs and sometimes the body of the builking.
Round gravel, or a collection of roundish stones. The plain of La Crau in France, is composed of shingle. Pinkerton.
Shingles, pIu. a kind of tetter or herpes which spreads around the body like a girdle; an eruptive disease.
To cover with shingles; as, to shingle a roof.
shining: Illustrious; distinguished; conspicuous; as a shining example of charity.
Effusion or clearness of light; brightness.
shiny: Bright; luminous; clear; unclouded. Like a distant thunder on a shiny day.
ship: In a general sense, a vessel or building of a peculiar structure, adapted to navigation, or floating on water by means of sails. In an appropriate sense, a building of a structure or form fitted for navigation, furnished with a bowsprit and three masts, a main-mast, a fore-mast and a mizen-mast, each of which is composed a lower-mast, a top-mast and top-gallant-mast, and square rigged. Ships are of various sizes and are for various uses; most of them however fall under the denomination of ships of war and merchant's ships.
as a termination, denotes state or office; as in lordship.
To put on board of a ship or vessel of any kind; as, to ship goods at Liverpoll for New York.
To transport in a ship; to convey by water. The sun shall no sooner the mountains touch, But we will ship him hence.
To receive into a ship or vessel; as, to ship at sea. To ship the oars, to place them in the rowlocks. To ship off, to send away by water; as, to ship off convicts.
ship-builder: A man whose occupation is to construct
ship-bilder: ships and other vessels; a naval architect; a shipwright.
ship-building: Naval architecture; the art of constructing
ship-bilding: vessels for navigation, particularly ships and other vessels of a large kind, bearing masts; in distinction from boatbuilding.
shipboard: To go on shipboard or a shipboard is to go aboard; to enter a ship; to embark; literally, to go over the side. It is a peculiar phrase, and not much used. Seamen say, to go aboard or on board. To be on ship board, to be in a ship; but seamen generally say, to go aboard or on board.
n. The pland of a ship.
ship-boy: A boy that serves on board of a ship.
ship-carpenter: A shipwright; a carpenter that works at ship-building.
ship-chandler: One who deals in cordage, canvas and other furniture of ships.
ship-holder: The owner of a ship or of shipping.
shipmaster: The captain, master or commander of a ship.
shipment: The act of putting any thing on board of another ship or vessel; embarkation; as, he engaged in the shipment of coal for London.

The goods or things shipped, or put on board of another ship or vessel. We say, the merchants have made large shipments to the United States. The question is whether the share of M in the shipment is exempted from the condemnation by reason of his nuetral domicil. J. Story.
ship-money: In English history, the imosition formerly charged on the ports, towns, cities, boroughs and counties of England, for providing and furnishing certain ships for the king’s service. This imosition being laid by the king’s writ under the great seal, without the consent of the parliament, was held to contrary to the laws and statutes of th erealm, and abolished by Stat. 17 Car. 11.
shipped: Put on board of a ship or vessel; received on board.
shipping: Putting on board of a ship or vessel; receiving on board.
a. Relating to ships; as shipping concerns. SHIIPPING.

Ships in general; ships or vessels of any kind for navigation. The shipping of the English nation exceeds that of any other. The tunnage of shipping belonging to the United States is second only to that of Great Britain. To take shipping, to embark; to enter on board a ship or vessel for conveyance or passage.
shipwreck: The destruction of a ship or vessel by being cast ashore or broken to pieces by beating against rocks and the like.
The parts of a shattered ship.
Destruction. To make a shipwreck concerning faith, is to apostatize from the love, profession and dpractice of divine truth which had been embraced.
To destroy by running ashore or on ricks or sand banks. How many vessels are annually shipwrecked on the Bahama rocks!
To suffer the perils of being cast away; to be cast ashore with the loss of the ship. The shipwrecked mariners were saved.
shipwrecked: Cast ashore; dashed upon the rocks or banks; destroyed.
shipwright: One whose occupation is to construct ships; a builder of ships or other vessels.
shire: In England, a division of territory, otherwise called a county. The shire was originally a division of the kingdom under the jurisdiction of an earl or count, whose authority was entrusted to the sherif. On this officer the goverment ultimately devolved. In the United States, the corresponding division of a state is called a county, but we retain shire in the in the compound half-shire; as when the county court is held in two towns in the same county alternately, we call one of the divisions a half-shire. In some states, shire as the constituent part of the name of a county, as Berkshire, Hampshire, in Massachusetts. These being the names established by law, we cannot say, the county of Berkshire, and we cannot with propriety say, the caounty of Berks, for there is no county in Massa chusetts thus named.
shire-mote: Anciently in England, the county court; sherif’s turn or court.
shirk: a different spelling of shark, which see.
shirley: A bird, by some called the greater bullfinch; having the upper part of the body a dark brown, and the throat and breast read.
shirt: A loose garment of linen, cotton or other material, worn by men and boys next the body. It is folly for a nation to export beef and linen, while a great part of the peaple are obliged to subsist on potatoes, and have no shirts to wear. A.M.
To cover or clothe, as with a shirt.
To change the shirt and put on a clean one.
shist: A species of argillaceous earth or slate; clay or slate.
shistus: A species of argillaceous earth or slate; clay or slate.
shistic: Pertaining to shist, or partaking of its properties.
shistous: Pertaining to shist, or partaking of its properties.
shittah: In Scripture, a sort of precious wood of which the tables, altars, and boards of the Jewish tabernacle were made.
shittam: boards of the tabernacle were made among the Jews. The wood is said to be hard, tough and smooth, and very beautiful.
shive: A slice; a thin cut; as a shive of bread.
A thin flexible piece cut off.
A little piece or fragment; as the shives of flax made by breaking .
shiver: In mineralogy, a species of blue slate; shist; shale.
In seamen’s language, a little wheel; a sheeve.
To break into many small pieces or splinters; to shatter; to dash to pieces by a blow. The ground with shiver’'d armor strown. Milton.
To fell at once into many small pieces or parts. The natural world, should gravity once cease, would instantly shiver into of millions of atoms.
To quake; to tremble; to shudder; to shake, as with cold, ague; fear or horror. The man that shiver’d on the brink of sin. Dryden . Prometeus is laid . On icy Caucasus to shiver.
shivered: Broken or dashed into small pieces.
shivering: Breaking or dashing into small pieces.
Quaking; trembling; shaking, as with cold or fear.
The act of breaking or dashing to pieces; division; severance.
A trembling; a shaking with cold or fear.
shiver-spar: A corbonate of lime, so called for its slaty structure; called also slate- spar.
shivery: Easily falling inot many pieces; not firmly cohering; incompact; as shivery stone.
shoad: Among miners, a train of metallic stones which serves to direct them in the discovery of mines.
shoad-stone: A small ston, smooth, of a dark liver color with a shade of purple. Shoad-stoners are loose masses found at the entrances of mines, sometimes running in a straight line from the surface to a vein of ore. They appear to be broken from the strata or larger massess; they usually contain mundic, or marcastic matter, and a portion of the ore of the mine.
shoal: A great multitude assembled; a crowd; a throng; as shoals of people. Immense shoals of herring appear on the coast in the spring. The vices of a prince draw shoals of followers. Decay of Piety .
A place where the water of a river, lake or sea is shallow or af little depth; a sand bank or bar; a shallow. The entrance of rivers is often rendered difficult or dangerous by shoals.
To crowd; to throng; to assemble in a multitude. The fishes shoaled about the place.
To become more shallow. The water shoals as we approach the town.
Shallow; of little depth; as shoal water.
shoaliness: The state of abounding with shoals.
shoaly: Full of shoals or shallow places. The tossing vessel saild on shoaly ground.
shock: A violent collision of bodies, or the concussion which it occasions; a viosent striking or dashing against. The strong unshaken mounds resist the shocks . Of tides and seas. Blackmore.
Violent onset; conflict of contending armies or foes. He stood the shock of a whole host of foes.
External violence; as the shocks of fortune.
Offense; impression of disgust. Fewer shocks a staesman gives his friend. Young.
In electricity, the effect on the animal system of a discharge of the fluid from a charged body.
shocked: Struck, as with horror; offended; disgusted.
shocking: Meeting in onset or violent encounter. And now with shouts the shocking armies clos’d. Pope.
a. Striking, as with horror; causing to recoil with horror or disgust; extremely offensive or disgusting. The French humor- is very shocking to the Italians. Addison .
shockingly: In a manner to strike with horror or disgust.
shoe: A covering for the foot, usually of lether, composed of a thick species for the sole, and a thinner kind for the vamp and quarthers. Shoes for ladies often have some kind of cloth for the vamp and quarters.

A plate or rim of iron nailed to the hoof of a horse to defend it from injury; also, a plate of iron for for an ox’s hoof, one for each division of the hoof. Oxen are shod in New England, sometimes to defend the hoof from injury in stony places, more generally to enable them to wald on ice, in which case the shoes are armed with sharp points. This is called calking.
The plate of iron which is nailed to the bottom of the runner of a sleigh, or any vehicle that slides on snow in the winter.
A piece of timber fastened with pins to the bottom of the runners of a sled, to prevent them from wearing.
shoebuckle: A buckle for fastening the shoe to the foot.
shoeinghorn: A horn used to facilitate the entrance of the foot onto a narrow shoe.
Any thing by which transaction is facilitated; any thing used as a medium; in contempt.
shoeless: Destitute of shoes. Caltrops very much incommoded the shoeless Moors. Dr.
shoemaker: One whose occupation or trade is to make shoes and boots.
shoer: One that fits shoes to the feet; one that furnishes or futs on shoes; as a farrier.
shoestring: A string used to fasten a shoe to the foot.
shoetye: A ribin used to fasten a shoe to the foot.
shog: To move off; to be gone; to jog.
shole: A throng; a crowd; a great multitude assembled.
shoot: pret. and pp. shot. The old participle shotten, is obsolete.
To let fly and drive with force; as, to shoot an arrow.
To discharge and cause to be driven with violence; as, to shoot a ball. And from about her shot darts of desire. Milton.
To let off; used of the instrument. The two ends of a bow shot off, fly from one another.
To strike with any thing shot; as, to shoot with an arrow or a bullet.
shooter: One that shoots; an archer; a gunner.
shooting: Discharging, as fire-arms; driving or sending with violence; pushing out; protuberating; germinating; branching; glancing, as in pain.
shotting: The act of discharging fire-arms, or of sending an arrow with force; a firing.
Sensation of a wuick glancing pain.
In sportsmanship, the act or practice of killing game with guns or fire-arms.
shop: A builking in which goods, wares, drugsare sold by retail.
a building in which mechanics work, and where they keep their manufactures for sale. Keep your shop, and your shop will keep you. Franklin.
To visit shops for purchasing goods; used chiefly in the participle; as, the laky is shopping.
shopboard: A bench on which work is performed; as a doctor or divine taken from the shopboard.
shopbook: A book in which a tradesman keeps his accounts.
shopkeeper: A trader who sells in a shop or by retail; in distinction from a merchant, or one who sells by wholesale.
shoplifter: One who steals any thing from a shop, or takes goods privately from a shop; one who under pretense of buying goods, takes occasion the steal.
shoplifting: Larceny committed in a shop; the stealing of any thing from a shop.
shopman: One who serves in a shop.
shopping: Visiting shops for the purchase of goods.
shore: the old. pret. of shear. Obs.

The coast or land adjacent to the sea, or to a large lake or river. This word is applied primarily to land contiguous to water; but it extends to the ground near the border of the sea or of a lake, which is covered with water. We also use the word to express the land near the border of the sea or of a great lake, to an indefinite extent; as when we say, a town stands on the shore. We do not apply the word to land contiguous to a small stream. This we call a bank.
A prop; a butress; something that supports a building or other thing.
To prop; to support by a post or butress; usually with sup; as, to shore up a building.
shoreless: Having no shore or coast; of indefinite or unlimited extent; as a shoreless ocean.
shorling: In England, the skin of a living sheep shorn, as distinct from the morling, or skin taken from a dead sheep. Hence in some parts of England, a shorling is a sheep shorn, and a morling is one that dies.
shorl: A mineral, usually of a black color, found in masses of an intermediate form, or crystallized in three or nine sided prisms, which when entire are terminated by three sided summits. The surface of the crystals is longitudinally streaked. the amorphous sort presents thin straight distinct columnur concretions, sometimes parallel, sometimes diverging or stelliform. This is called also tourmalin. The shorl of the mineralogists of the last century comprehended a variety of substances which later observations have separated into several species. The green shorl is the epidote, or the vesuvian, or the acinolite. The violet shorl an the lenticular shorl ar ethe axinite. The black volcanic shorl is si the augite. The white Vesuvian shorl is the sommite. The white grenatiform is the leucite. The white prismatic is the pycnite, a species of the topaz, and another is a variety of feldspar. Of the b
shorlaceous: Like shorl; partaking of the nature and characters of shorl.
shorlite: A mineral of a greenish white color, sometimes yellowish; mostly found in irregular oblong masses or columns, inserted in a mixture of quartz and mica or granite. Shorlite of shorlous topaz, the pycnite of Werner, is of a straw yellow color.
shorn: Cut off; as a lock of wool shorn.
Having the hair or wool cut off or sheared; as a shorn lamb.
Deprived; as a prince shorn of his honors.
short: Not long; not having great length or extension; as a short distance; a short ferry; a short flight; a short piece of timber. The bed is shorter than a man can stretch himself on it. Isaiah 28:20.
Not extended in time; not of long duration. The triumphing of the wicked is short. Job 20:5.
Not of usual or suffifient length, reach or extent. Weak though I am of limb, and short of sight. Pope.
Not of long duration; repeated at small intervals of time; as short breath.
Not of adequate extent or quantity; not reaching the point demanded, desired or expected; as a quantity short of our expectations. Not therefore am I short . Of knowing what I ought. Milton.
short-breathed: Having short breath or quick respiration.
shorten: To make short in measure, extent or time; as, to shorten distance; to shorten a road; to shorten days of calamity.
To abridge; to lessen; as, to shorten labor or work.
To curtail; as, to shorten the hair by clipping.
To cintract; to lessen; to diminish in extent or amount; as, to shorten sail; to shorten an allowance of provisions.
To confine; to restrain. Here where the subject is so fruitful, I am shortened by my chain.
shortening: Something used in cookery to make paste short or friable, as butter or lard.
short-hand: Short writing; a compendious method of writing by substituting characters, abbreviations or symbols for words; otherwise called stenography.
short-jointed: A horse is said to be short-jointed when the pastern is to short.
short-lived: Not living or lasting long; being of short continuance; as a short-lived race of beings; short-lived pleasure; short-lived passion.
shortly: Quickly; soon; in a little time. The armies came shortly in view of each other.
In a few words; briefly; as, to express ideas more shortly in verse than in prose.
shortness: The quality of being short in space or time; little length or little duration; as the shortness of a journey or of distance; the shortness of the days in winter; the shortness of life.
Fewness of words; brevity; conciseness; as the shortness of an essay. The prayers of the church, by reason of their shortness, are easy for the memory.
Want of reach or the power of retention; as the shortness of the memory.
Deficiency; imperfection; limited extent; as the shortness of our reason.
short-rib: One of the lower ribs; a rib shorter than the others, below the sternum; a false rib.
shorts: The bran and coarse part of a meal.
short-sight: Short-sightedness; myopy; vision accurate only when the object is near.
shortsighted: Not able to see far; having limited vision; in a literal sense.
Not able to look far into futurity; not able to understand things deep or remite; of limited intellect.
short-sightedness: A defect in vision, consisting in the inability to see things at a distance, or at the distance to which ssight normally extends. Short- sightedness is owing to the too great convexity of the crystaline humor of the eye, by which the rays of light are brought to a focus too soon, that is, before they reach the retina.
Defected or limited intellectual sight; inabilaty to see far into futurity or into things deep or abstruse.
short-waisted: Having a short waist or body.
short-winded: Affected with shortness of breath; having a quick respiration; as asthmatic persons.
short-winged: Having short wings; as a short-winged hawk.
short-witted: Having little wit; not wise; of scanty intellect or judgement.
shory: Lying near th eshore or coast.
shot: The act of shooting; discharge of a missile weapon.V\ . He caused twenty shot of his greatest cannon to be made at the king’s army.

A missile weapon, particularly a ball or bullet. Shot is properly what is discharged from fire-arms or cannons by the force of gunpowder. Shot used in war is of various kinds; as, roundshot or balls; those for cannon make of iron, those for muskets and pistols, of lead. Secondly, double headed shot or bar shot, consisting of a bar with a round head at each end. Thirdly, chain-shot, consisting of to balls chained together. Fourthly, grape-shot, consisting of a number of balls bound together with a cord in canvas on an iron bottom. Fifthly, case shot or canister shot, consisting of a great number of small bullets in a cylindrical tin box. Sixthly, langrel or langrage, which consists of pieces of iron of any kind or shape. Small shot, denotes musket balls.
Small globular masses of lead, used for killing fowls and other small animals. These are not called balls or bullets.
The flight of a missile weapon, or the distance which it passes from the engine; as a cannon shot; a musket shot; a pistol shot; a bow shot.
shot-free: Free from charge; exempted from any share of expense; scot- free.
Not to be injured by shot.
shotten: Having ejected the spawn; as a shotten herring.
Shot out of its socket; dislocated; as a bone.
should: shood. The preterit of shall, but now used as an auxiliary verb, either in the past time or conditional present. “He should have paid the debt at the time the note became due.” Should here denotes past time. “I should ride to town this day if the weather would permit.” Here should expresses present or future time conditionally. In the second and third persons, it denotes obligation or duty, as in the first example above.

I should go. When should in this person is uttered without emphasis, it declares simply that an event would take place, on some condition or under circumstances. But when expressed with emphasis, should in this person denotes obligation, duty or determination.

Thou shouldst go. You should Without emphasis, should, in the second person, is nearly equivalent to ought; you ought to go, it is your duty, you are bound to go. With emphasis, should expresses determination in th espeaker conditionally to compel the person to act. “If I had the care of you, you should go, whether willing or not." .
He should go. should, in the third person, has the same force as in the second.
IIf I should, if you should, if he shoulddenote a figure contingent event.&
shoulder: The joint by which the arm of a human being or the fore leg of a quadruped is connected with the body; or in man, the projection formed by the bones called scapula or shoulder blades, which extend from the basis of the neck in a horizontal direction.
The upper joint of the fore leg of an animal cut for th emarket; as a shoulder of mutton.
Shoulders, in the plural, the upper part of the back. Adown her shoulders fell her length of hair.
Figuratively, support; sustaining power; or that which elevates and sustains. For on thy shoulders do I build my seat.
Among artificers, something like the human shoulder; a horizontal or rectangular projection from the body of a thing.
shoulder-belt: A belt that passes across the shoulder.
shoulder-blade: The bone of the shoulder, or blade bone, broad and triangular, covering the hind part of the ribs; called by anatomists scapula and omoplata.
shoulder-clapper: One that claps another on the shoulder, or that uses great familiarity.
shoulder-knot: An ornamental knot of ribin or lace worn on the shoulder; an epaulet.
shoulder-shotten: Strained in the shoulder, as a horse.
shoulder-slip: Dislocation of the shoulder or of the humerus.
shout: To utter a sudden and loud outcry, usually in joy, triumph or exultation, or to animate soldiers in an onset. It is not the voice of them that shout for mastery. Exodus 32:18. When ye hear th esound of the trumpet, all the people shall shout with a great shout. Joshua 6:5.

A loud burst of voice or voices; a vehement and sudden outcry, particularly of a multitude of men, expressing joy, triumph, exultation or animated courage. It is sometimes intended in derision. The Rhodians seeing an enemy turn their backs, gave a great shout in derision.
To treat with shouts or clamor.
shouting: Uttering a sudden and loud outcry in joy or exultation.
The act of shouting; a loud outcry expressive of joy or animation.
shove: To push; to propel; to drive along by the direct application of strength without impulse; particularly, to push a body by sliding or causing it to move along the surface of another body, either by the hand or by an instrument; as, the shove a bottle along a table; to shove a table along the floor; to shove a boat along the water. And shove away the worthy bidden guest. Milton. Shoving back this earth on which I sit.

To push; to press against. He used to shove an elbow his fellow servants to get near his mistress Arbuthnot. To shove away, to push to a distance; to thrust off. To shove by, to push away; to delay, or to reject; as, to shove by the hearing of a cause; or to shove by justice. To shove off, to thrust or push away. To shove down, to overthrow by pushing.
To push or drive forward; to urge a course.
To push off; to move in a boat or with a pole; as, he shoved from shore. To shove off, to move from shore by pushing with poles or oars.
The act of pushing or pressing against by strength, without a sudden impulse.
shovel: shov'l. An instrument consisting of a broad scoop or hollow blade with a handle; used por throwing earth or other loole substances.
To take up and throw with a shovel; as, to shovel earth into a heap or into a cart, or out of a pit.
shovel-board: A board on which they play by sliding metal pieces at a mark.
shoveler: A fowl of the genus Anas or duck kind.
show: pret. ED; pp. shown. It is sometimes written shew, shewed
To exhibit or present to the view of others. Go thy way, show thyself to the priest. Matthew 8:4.
To afford to the eye or to notice; to contain in a visible form. Nor want we skill o rart, from whence to raise . Magnificence; and what can heaven show more? Milton.
To make or enable to see.
To make or enable to perceive.
show-bread: Among the Jews, bread of exhibition; the loaves of bread which the priest of the week placed before the Lord, on the golden table in the sanctuary. They were shaped like a brick, were ten palms long and five wide, weighing about eight pounds each. They were made of fine flour unleavened, and changed every sabbath. The loaves were twelve in number, and represented the twelve tribes of Israel. They were to be eaten by the priest only.
shower: A fall of rain or hail, a sort of duration. It may be applied to like a fall of snow, but this seldon occurs. It is applied to a fall of rain or hail of short continuance, or of more or less violence, but never to a storm of long continuance.
A fall of thing from the air in thick succession; as a shower of darts or arrows; a shower of stones.
A copious supply bestowed; liberal distribution; as a great shower of gifts.
To water with a shower; to wet copiously with rain; as, to shower the earth.
showered: Wet with a shower; watered abundantly; bestowed or distributed liberally.
showery: Raining in showers; abounding with frequent falls of rain.
showily: In a showy manner; pompously; with parade.
showiness: State of being showy; pompousness; great parade.
showy: Splendid; gay; gaudy; making a great show; fine.
shrag: A twig of a tree cut off.
shragger: One that lops; one that trims trees.
shrap: A place baited with chaff to invite birds.
shrape: A place baited with chaff to invite birds.
shred: pret. and pp. shred. To cut into small pieces, particularly marrow and long pieces, as of cloth or lether. It differs from mince, which signifies to chop into pieces fine and short.
A long narrow piece cut off; as shreds of cloth.
A fragment; a piece; as shreds of wit.
shredding: That which is cut off; a piece.
shrew: A peevish, brawling, turbulent, vexatious woman. It appears to have originally been applied to males as well as females; but is now restricted to the latter. The man had got a shrew for his wife, and there could be no quiet in the house with her. L’Estrange.
shrewd: Having the qualities of a shrew; vexatious; troublesome; mischievous. Obs.
Sly; cunning; arch; subtil; artful; astute; as a shrewd man.
Sagacious; of nice discernment; as a shrewd observer of men.
Proceeding from cunning or sagacity, or containing it; as a shrewd saying; a shrewd conjecture.
Painful; vexatious; troublesome. Every of this number' . That have endured shrewd nights and days with us. Obs. No enemy is so despicable but he may do one a shrewd turn. Obs. L’Estrange.
shrewdly: Mischievously; destructively. This practice hath most shrewdly passed upon thee. Obs.

Vexatiously; used of slight mischief. The obstinate and schismatical are like to think themselves shrewdly hurt by being cut from that body they chos not to be of. Obs. South. Yet seem’d she not to winch, though shrewdly pained. Obs.
Archly; sagaciously; with good guess; as, I shrewdly suspect; he shrewdly observed.
shrewdness: Sly cunning; archness. The neighbors round admire his shrewdness. Swift .
Sagaciousness; sagacity; the quality of nice discernment.
shrewish: Having the qualities of a shrew; forward; peevish; petulantly clamorous. My wife is shrewish when I keep not hours. Shak .
shrewishly: Peevishly; clamorously; turbulently. He speaks very shrewishly.
shrewishness: The qualities of a shrew; forwardness; petulance; turbulent clamorousness. I have no gift in shrewishness.
shrew-mouse: A small animal resembling a mouse, but belong to the genus Sorex; an animal that burrows in the ground, feeding on corn, insectsIt is a harmless animal.
shriek: To utter a sharp shrill cry; to scream; as in sudden fright, in horror or anguish. At this she shriek’d aloud. It was the owl that shriek’d.
A sharp shrill outcry or scream, such as is produced by sudden anger or extreme anguish. Shrieks, clamors, murmurs fill the frighted town.
shrieking: Crying out with a shrill voice.
shrievalty: Sherifalty; the office of a sherif. It was ordained by 28 Ed. 1. that the people shall have election of sherif in every shire, where the shrievalty is not of inheritance. Blackstone.
shrift: Confession made to a priest. Obs.
shrike: The butcher-bird; a genus of birds called Lanius, of several species.
shrill: Sharp; acute; piercing; as sound; as a shrill voice; shrill echoes.
Uttering an acute sound; as the cock’s shrill sounding throat; a shrill trumpet.
To utter an acute piercing sound. Break we out pipes that shrill’ as loud as lark. Spenser.
To cause to make a shrill sound.
shrillness: Acuteness of sound; sharpness or fineness of voice.
shrilly: Acutely, as sound; with a sharp sound or voice.
shrimp: A crustaceous animal of the genus Cancer. It has long slender feelers, claws with a single, hooked fang, and three pair of legs. It is esteemed delicious food.
A little wrinkled man; a dwarf; in contempt.
shrine: A case or box; particularly applied to a case in which sacred things are deposited. Hence we hear much of shrines for relics. Come, offer at my shrine, and I will help thee. Shak .
shrink: pret. and pp. shrunk. the old pret. shrank and pp. shrunken are nearly obsolete. To contract spontaneously; to draw or be drawn into less length, breadth or compass by an inherent power; as, woolen cloth shrinks in hot water; a flaxen of hempen line shrinks in a humid atmosphere. Many substances shrink by drying.
To shrivel; to become wrinkled by contraction; as th eskin.
To withdraw or retire, as from danger; to decline action from fear. A brave man never shrinks from danger; a good man does not shrink from duty.
To recoil, as in fear, horror or distress. My mind shrinks from the recital of our woes. What happier natures shrink at with affright, The hard inhabitant contends is right. Pope.
To express fear, horror or pain by shrugging or contracting the body.
shrinkage: A shrinking or contraction into a less compass. Make an allowance for the shrinkage of grain in drying.
shrinker: One that shrinks; one that withdraws from danger.
shrinking: Contracting; drawing together; withdrawing from danger; declining to act from fear; causing to contract.
shrive: To hear or receive the confession of; to administer confession; as a priest. He shrives this woman. Obs.
shrivel: shriv’l. To comtract; to draw or be drawn into wrinkles; to shrink and form corrugations; as, a leaf shrivels in the hot sun; the skin shrivels with age.
To contract into wrinkles; to cause to shrink into corrugations. A scorching sun shrivels the blades of corn. Snd shrivel’d herbs on withering stems decay.
shroud: A shelter; a cover; that which covers, conceals or protects. Swaddled, as new born, in sable shrouds. Sandys.
The dress of the dead; a winding sheet.

Shroud or shrouds of a ship, a range of large ropes extending from the head of a mast to the right and left sides of the ship, to support the mast; as the main shrouds; fore shrouds; mizen shrouds. There are also futtock shrouds, bowsprit shrouds.
To cover; to shelter from danger or annoyance. Under your beams I will me safely shroud. Spenser. One of these trees with all its young ones, may shroud four hundred horsemen. Raleigh.
shrove: To join in the festivities of Shrove-tide.
shrove-tuesday: Tuesday. Confession-time; confession-Teusday; the Tuesday after Quinquagesima Sunday; or the day immediately preceding the first of Lent, or Ash Wednesday; on which day, all the people of England when of the Catholic religion, were obliged to confess their sins one by one to their parish priests; after which they dined on pancakes or fritters. The latter ppractice still continues. The bell rung on this day is called pancake-bell.
shrub: A low dwarf tree; a woody plant of a size less than a tree; or more strictly, a plant with several permanent woody stems, dividing from the bottom, more slender and lower than trees. Gooseberries and currants are shrubs; oaks and cherries are trees.
A liquor composed of acid and sugar, with spirir to preserve it; usually the acid of lemons.
shrubby: Full of shrubs; as a shrubby plain.
Resembling a shrub; as plants shrubby and curled.
Consisting of shrubs or brush; as shrubby browze.
A shrubby plant is perennial, with several woody stems.
shrug: To raise or draw up the shoulders, as in expressing horror or dissatisfaction. They grin, they shrug, They bow, they snarl, they scratch, they hug.
A drawing up of the shoulders; a motion usually expressing dislike. The Spaniards talk in dialogues . Of heads and shoulders, nods and shrugs. Hudibras.
shudder: To quake; to tremble or shake with fear, horror or aversion; to shiver. I love-alas! I shudder at the name. Smith.
A tremor; a shaking with fear or horror.
shuddering: Trembling or shaking with fear or horror; quaking.
shuffle: Properly, to shove one way and the other; to push from one to another; as, to shuffle money from hand to hand.

To mix by pushing or shoving; to confuse; to throw into disorder; especially, to change the relatibe positions of cards in the pack. A man may shuffle cards or rattle dice from noon to midnight, without tracing a new idea in his head. Rambler.

To remove or introduce by artificial confusion.& It was contrived by your enemies, and shuffled into the papers that were seized. To shuffle off, to push off; to rid one’s self of. When you lay blame to a child, he will attempt to shuffle it off. To shuffle up, to throw together in haste; to make up or form in confusion or with fraudulent disorder; as, he shuffled up a peace.
To change the relative position of cards in a pack by little shoves; as, to shuffle and cut.
To change the position; to shift ground; to prevaricate; to evade fair questions; to pratice shifts to elude detection. Hiding my honor in my necessity, I am fain to shuffle.
shuffle-cap: a play performed by shaking money in a hat or cap.
shuffler: One that shuffles or prevaricates; one that plays tricks; one that shuffles cards.
shuffling: Moving by little shoves one way an the other; changing the places of cards; prevaricating; evading; playing tricks.
a. Evasive; as a shuffling excuse.
The act of throwing into confusion, or of changing the relative position of things by shoving or motion.
shufflingly: With shuffling; with an irregular gait or pace.
shun: To avoid; to keep clear of; not to fall on or come in contact with; as, to shun rocks and shoals in navigation. In shunning Scylla, take care to avoid to avoid Charybdis.
To avoid; not to mix or associate with; as, to shun evil company.
To avoid; not to practice; as, to shun vice.
To avoid; to escape; as, to shun a blow.
To avoid; to decline; to neglect. I haved not shunned to declare the whole counsel of God. Acts 20:27.
shunless: Not to be avoided; inevitable; unavoidable; as shunless destiny.
shut: To close so as to hinder ingress or egress; as, to shut a door or gate; to shut the eyes or the mouth.
To prohibit; to bar; to forbid entrance into; as, to shut the ports of the kingdom by a blockade. Shall that be shut to man, which to the beast . Is open? Milton.
To preclude; to exclude. But shut from every shore.
To close, as the fingers; to contract; as, to shut the hand. To shut in, to inclose; to confine.
shutter: A person that shuts or closes.
A door; a cover; something that closes a passage; as the shutters of a window.
shuttle: An instrument used by weavers for shooting the thread of the woof in weaving from one side of the cloth to the other, between the threads of the warp.
shuttle-cock: A cork stuck with fethers, used to be struck by a battledore in play; also, the play.
shy: Fearful of near approach; keeping at a distance through caution or timidity; shunning approach; as a shy bird. She is represented in a shy retiring posture.
Reserved; not’ familiar; coy; avoiding freedom of intercourse. What makes you so shy, my good friend?
Cautious; wary; careful to avoid committing one’s self or adopting measures. I am very shy of using corrosive liquors in the preparation of medicines.
Suspicious; jealous. Princes are by wisdom of state somewhat shy of their successors. Wotton.
shyly: In a shy or timid manner; not familiarity; with reserve.
shyness: Fear of near approach or of familiarity; reserve; coyness.
sialogogue: sial’ogog. A medicing that promotes the salivary discharge.
sib: a relation, in Saxon, but not in use in English.
siberian: Pertaining to Siberia, a name given to a great and indefinite extent of territory in the north of Asia; as a Siberian winter.
sibilant: Hissing; making a hissing sound. S and z are called sibilant letters.
A letter that is uttered with a hissing of the voice; as s and z.
sibyl: In pagan antiquity, the Sibyls were certain said to be endowed with a prophetic spirit. Their number is variously stated; but the opinion of Varro, who states them to have been ten, is generally adopted. They resided in various parts of Persia, Greece and Italy. It is pretended that they wrote certain prophecies on leaves in verse, which are called Sibylline verses, or Sibylline oracles.
sibylline: Pertaining to the Sibyls; uttered, written or composed by the Sibyls.
sicamore: More usually written sycamore, which see.
siccation: The act or process of drying.
siccative: That which promotes the process of drying.
siccity: Dryness; aridity; destitution of moisture; as the siccity of flesh or af the air.
sick: Affected with nausea; inclined to vomit; as, sick at the stomach. Hence,
Disgusted; having a strong dislike to; with of; as, to be sick of flattery; to be sick of a country life. V . He was not so sick of his master as of his work. L’Estrange.
Affected with a disease of any kind; not in health; followed by of; as to be sick of a fever.
The sick, the person or persons affected with the disease. The sick are healed.
sick-birth: In a ship of war, an apartment for the sick.
sicken: To make sick; to disease. Raise this to strength, and sicken that to death.
To make squeamish. It sickens the stomach.
To disgust. It sickens one to hear the fawning sycophant.
sickish: Exciting disgust; nauseating; as a sickish taste.
sickle: A reaping hook; a hooked instrument with teeth; used for cutting grain. Thou shalt not move a sickle to thy neighbor’s standing corn. Deuteronomy 23:25.
sickleman: One that uses a sickle; a reaper.
sickler: One that uses a sickle; a reaper.
sicklewort: A plant of the genus Coronilla.
sickliness: The state of being sickly; the state of being habitually diseased; .
The state of producing sickness extensively; as the sickliness of a Climate.
sick-list: A list containing the names of the sick.
sickly: Not healthy; somewhat affected with disease; or habitually indisposed; as a sickly person, or a sickly constitution; a sickly plant.
Producing disease extensively; marked with sickness; as a sickly time; a sickly autumn.
Tending to produce disease; as a sickly climate.
Faint; weak; languid. The moon grows sickly at the sight of day.
sickness: Nausea; squeamishness; as sickness of the stomach.
State of being diseased. I do lament the sickness of the king.

Disease; malady; a morbid state of the body of an animal or plant, in which the organs do not perfectly perform their natural functions. Trust not too much your now resistless charms; Those age or sickness soon or late disarms. Himself took our infirmities, and bore our sicknesses. Vatthew 8:17.
side: The broad and long part of surface of a thing, as distinguished from the end, which is of less extent and many be a point; as the side of a plank; the side of a chest; the side of a house or of a ship. One side of a lens may be concave, the other convex. Side is distinguished from edge; as the side of a knife or sword.

Margin; edge; verge; border; the exterior line of any thing, considered in length; as the side of a tract of land or a field, as distinct from the end. Hence we say, the side of a river; the side of a road; the east and west side of the American continent.
The part of an animal between the back and the face and belly; the part of which the ribs are situated; as the right side; the left side. This is quadrupeds is usually the broadest part.
The part between the top and bottom; the slope, declivity or ascent, as of a hill or mountain; as the side of mount Etna.
One part of a thing, or its superficies; as the side of a ball or sphere.
sideboard: A piece of furniture or cabinet work consisting of a table or box with drawers or cells, placed at the side of a room or in a recess, and used to hold dining utensils
side-box: A box or inclosed seat on the side of a theater distinct from the seats in the pit.
sideling: Sidewise; with the side foremost; as, to go sideling through a crowd. It may be used as a participle; as, I saw him sideling through the crowd.
sidelong: Lateral; oblique; not directly in front; as a sidelong glance.
Laterally; obliquely; in the direction of the side.
On the side; as, to lay a thing sidelong.
sider: One that takes a side or joins a party.
sideral: Pertaining to a star or stars; astral; as sideral light.
Containing stars; starry; as sidereal regions. Sidereal year, in astronomy, the period in which the fixed stars apparently complete a revolution and come to the same point in the heavens.
sidereal: Pertaining to a star or stars; astral; as sideral light.
Containing stars; starry; as sidereal regions. Sidereal year, in astronomy, the period in which the fixed stars apparently complete a revolution and come to the same point in the heavens.
sideration: A blasting or blast in plants; a sudden deprivation of sense; an apoplexy; a slight erysipelas.
A sphacelus, or a species of erysipelas, vulgarly called a blast.
siderite: The loadstone; also, iron-wort, a genus of plants; also, the common ground pine .
In mineralogy, a phosphate of iron.
sidero-clepte: A mineral of a yellowish green color, soft and translucid, occurring in reniform or botryoidal masses.
siderographch: Pertaining to siderography, or performed by engraved plates of steel; as siderographic art; siderographic impressions.
siderographocal: Pertaining to siderography, or performed by engraved plates of steel; as siderographic art; siderographic impressions.
siderographist: One who engraves steel plates, or performs work by means of such plates.
siderography: and The art or practice of engraving on steel, by means of which, impressions may be transferred from a steel plate to a steel cylinder in a rolling press of a particular construction.
side-saddle: A saddle for a woman’s seat on horseback.
sidesman: An assistant to the church warden.
sidetaking: A taking sides, or engaging in a party.
sideways: Towards one side; inclining; as, to hold the head sidewise .
Laterally; on one side; as the refraction of light sidewise.
sidewise: Towards one side; inclining; as, to hold the head sidewise .
Laterally; on one side; as the refraction of light sidewise.
siding: The attaching of one’s self to a party.
sidle: To go or move side foremost; as, to sidle through a crowd.
siege: The setting of an army around or before a fortified place for the purpose of compelling the garrison to surrender; or the surrounding or investing of a place by an army, and approaching it by passages and advanced works, which cover the besiegers from the enemy’s fire. A siege differs from a blockade, as in a siege the investing army approaches the fortified place to attach and reduce it by force; but in a blockade, the army secures all the avenues to the place to intercept all supplies, and waits till famine compels the garrison to surrender.
Any continued endeavor to gain possession. Love stood the siege, and would not yield his breast.
sienite: A compound granular rock composed of quartz, hornblend and feldspar, or a grayish color; so called, because there are may ancient monuments consisting of this rock, brought from Syene, in Upper Egypt.
sieur: A title of respect used by the French.
sieve: An utensil for separating flour from bran, or the fine part of any pulverized or fine substance from the coarse, by the hand; as a fine sieve; a coarse sieve. It consists of a hoop with a hair bottom, and performs in the family the service of a bolter in a mill.
sift: To separate by a sieve, as the fine part of a substance from the coarse; as, to sift meal; to sift powder; to sift sand or lime.
To examine minutely or critically; to scrutinize. Let the principles of the party be thoroughly sifted. We have sifted your objections.
sifted: Separated by a sieve; purified from the coarser parts; critically examined.
sifter: One that sifts; that which sifts; a sieve.
sifting: Separating the finer from the coarser parts; critically examining.
sig: a Saxon word signifying victory. is used in names, as in Sigbert, bright victory. In answers to the Greek vix in Nicander, and the L. vic, in Victorinus.
sigh: To inhale a larger quantity of air than usual and immediately expel it; to suffer a single deep respiration. He sighed deeply in his spirit. Mark 8:12.
To lament; to mourn. Ages to come and men unborn Shall bless her name and sigh her fate.
To express by sighs. The gentle swain-sighs back her grief.

A single deep respiration; a long breath; the inhaling of larger quantity of air than usual, and the sudden emission of it. This is an effort of nature to dilate the lungs and give vigor to the circulation of the blood, when the action of the heart and arteries is languid from grief, depression of spirits, weakness or want of exercise. Hence sighs are indications of grief of debility.
sighing: Suffering a deep respiration; taking a long breath.
The act of suffering a deep respiration, or taking a long breath.
sight: The act of seeing; perception of objects by the eye; view; as, to gain sight of land; to have a sight of a landscape; to lose sight of a ship at sea. A cloud received him out of their sight. Acts 7:9.

The faculty of vision, or of perceiving objects by the instrumentality of the eyes. It has been doubted whether moles have sight. Milton lost his sight. The sight usually fails at of before fifty years of age. O loss of sight, of thee I most complain.

Open view; the state of admitting unobstructed vision; a being within the limits of vision. The harbor is in sight of the town. The shore of Long Island is in sight of New Haven. The White mountain is in plain sight at Portland, in Maine; a mountain is or is not within sight; an engagement at sea is within sight of land.
Notice from seeing; knowledge; as a letter intended for the sight of one person only.
Eye; the instrument of seeing. From the depth of hell they lift their sight.
sighted: In composition only, having sight, or seeing in a particular manner; as long- sighted, seeing at a great distance; short-sighted, able to see only at a small distance; quick-sighted, readily seeing, discerning or understanding; sharp-sighted, having a keen eye or acute discernment.
sightless: Wanting sight; blind. Of all who blindly creep, or sightless soar.
Offensive or unpleasing to the eye; as sightless stains.
sightliness: Comely; having an appearance pleasing to the sight.
sightly: Pleasing to the eye; striking to the view. Many brave sightly horses. We have thirty members, the most sightly of all her majesty’s subjects.
Open to the view; that may be seen from a distance. We say; a house stands in a sightly place.
sightsman: Among musicians, one who reads music readily at first sight.
sigmoidal: Curved like the Greek sigma. The sigmoid flexure, in anatomy, is the last curve of the colon, before it terminates in the rectum.
sign: A token; something by which another thing is shown or represented; any visible thing, any motion, appearance or event which indicates the existence or approach of something else. Thus we speak of signs of fair weather or of a storm, and of external marks which are signs of a good constitution.
A motion, action, nod or gesture indicating a wish or command. They made signs to his father, how he would have him called. Luke 1:62.
A wonder; a miracle; a prodigy; a remarkable transaction, event or phenomenon. Through mighty signs and wonders. Romans 15:19; Luke 11:16.
Some visible transaction, event or appearance intended as proof or evidence of something else; hence; proof; evidence by sight. Show me a sign that thou talkest with me. Judges 6:17.
signal: A sign that gives or is intended to give notice; or the notice given. Signals are used to communicate notice, information, orders and the like, to persons at a distance, and by any persons and for the purpose. A signal may be a motion of the hand, the raising of a flag, the firing of a gun, or any thing which, being understood by persons at a distance, may communicate notice. Signals are particularly useful in the navigation of fleets and in naval engagements. There are day-signals, which are usually made by the sails, by flags and pendants, or guns; night-signals, which are lanterns disposed in certain figures, or false fires, rockets, or the firing of guns; fog-signals, which are made by sounds, as firing of guns, beating of drums, ringing of bellsThere are signals of evolution, addressed to a whole fleet, to a division or to a squadron; signals of movements to particular ships; and si
Eminent; remarkable; memorable; distinguished from what is ordinary; as a signal exploit; a signal service; a signal ace of benevolence. It is generally but not always used in a good sense.
signality: Quality of being signal or remarkable.
signalize: To make remarkable or eminent; to render distinguished from what is common. The soldier who signalizes himself in battle, merits his country’s gratitude. Men may signalize themselves, their valor or their talents.
signally: Eminently; remarkably; memorably; in a distinguished manner.
signatory: Relating to a seal; used in sealing.
signature: A sign, stamp or mark impressed. The brain being well furnished with various traces, signatures and images. The natural and indelible signature of God stamped on the human soul.

In old medical writers, and external mark or character on a plant, which was supposed to indicate its suitableness to cure particular diseases, or diseases of particular parts. This plants with yellow flowers were supposed to be adapted to the cure of the jaundiceSome plants bear a very evident signature of their nature and use.
A mark for proof, or proof from marks.
Sign manual; the name of a person written or subscribed by himself.

Among printers, a letter or figure at the bottom of the first page of a sheet or half sheet, by which the sheets are distinguished and their order designated, as a direction to the binder. Every successive sheet has a different letter or figure, and if the sheets are more numerous than the letters of the alphabet, the a small letter is added to the capital one; thus Aa, Bb, In large volumes, the signatures are sometimes composed of letters and figures; thus 5A, 5B. But some printer now use figures only for signatures.
signaturist: One who holds to the doctrine of signatures impressed upon objects, indicative of character or qualities.
signer: One that signs or subscribes his name; as a memorial with a hundred signers.
signet: A seal; particularly in Great Britain, the seal used by the king in sealing his private letters, and grants that pass by bill under his majesty’s hand.
significance: Meaning; import; that which is intended to be expressed; as the significance of a nod, or of a motion of the hand, or of a word or expression.
Force; energy; power of impressing the mind; as a duty enjoined with particular significance.
Importance; moment; weight; consequence. Many a circumstance of less significancy has been construed into an event act of high treason.
significancy: Meaning; import; that which is intended to be expressed; as the significance of a nod, or of a motion of the hand, or of a word or expression.
Force; energy; power of impressing the mind; as a duty enjoined with particular significance.
Importance; moment; weight; consequence. Many a circumstance of less significancy has been construed into an event act of high treason.
significant: Expressive of something beyond the external mark.
Bearing a meaning; expressing or containing signification of sense; as a significant word or sound; a significant look.
Betokening something; standing as a sign of something. It was well said of Plotinus, that the stars were significant, but not efficient.

Expressive or representative of some fact or event, The passover among the Jews was significant of the escape of the Israelites from the destruction which fell on the Egyptians. The bread and wine in the sacrament are significant of the body and blood of Christ.
signification: The act of making known, or of communicating ideas to another by signs or by words, by any thing that is understood, particularly by words. All speaking, or signification of one’s mind, implies an act or address of one man to another.

Meaning; that which is understood to be intended by a sign, character, mark or word; that idea or sense of a sign, mar, word or expression which the person using it intends to convey, or that which men in general who use it, understand it to convey. The signification of words was originally arbitrary, and is dependent on usage. But when custom has annexed a certain sense to a letter or sound, or to a combination of letters or sounds, this sense is always to be considered the signification which the person using the word intends to communicate. So by custom, certain signs or gestures have a determinate signification. Such is the fact also with figures, algebraic character.
significative: Betokening or representing by an external sign; as the significative symbols of the eucharist.
Having signification or meaning; expressive of a certain idea or thing. Neither in the degrees of kindred were they destitute of significative words.
significatively: So as represent or express by an external sign.
significatory: That which betokens, signifies or represents.
signify: To make known something, either by signs or words; to express or communicate to another any idea, thought, wish, a hod, wink, gesture, signal or other sign. A man signifies his mind by his voice or by written characters; he may signify his mind by a nod or other motion, provided the person to whom he directs it, understands what is intend by it. A general or an admiral signifies his commands by signals to officers as a distance.

To mean; to have or contain a certain sense. The word sabbath signifies rest. Less, in composition, as in faithless, signifies destitution or want. The prefix re, in recommend, seldom signifies any thing.

To import; to weigh; to have consequence; used in particular phrases; as, it signifies much or little; it signifies nothing. What does it signify? What signify the splendors of a court? Confession of sin without reformation of life, can signify nothing in the view of God.
To make known; to declare. The government should signify to the protestants of Ireland that want of silver is not to be remedied.
signior: A title of respect among the Italians.
signorize: To exercise dominion; or to have dominion.
signory: A different, but less common spelling of seigniory, which see. It signifies lordship, dominion, and in Shakespeare
sign-post: A post on which a sign hangs, or on which papers are placed to give public notice of any thing. By the laws of some of the New England states, a sign-post is to be erected near the center of each town.
silence: In a general sense, stillness, or entire absence of sound or noise; as the silence of midnight.
In animals, the state of holding the peace; forbearance of speech in man, or of noise in other animals. I was dumb with silence; I held my peace, even from good. Psalm 39:2.
Secrecy. These things were transacted in silence.
Stillness; calmness; quiet; cessation of rage, agitation or tumult; as the elements reduced to silence.
silent: Habitually taciturn; speaking little; not inclined to much talking; not loquacious. Ulysses, he adds was the most eloquent and most silent of men.
Still; having not noise; as the silent watches of the night; the silent groves; all was silent.
Not mentioning; not proclaiming. This new created world, of which in hell Fame is not silent.
silentiary: One appointed to keep silence and order in court; one sworn not to divulge secrets of state.
silently: Without speech or words. Each silently demands thy grace, and seems to watch thy eye.
without noise; as, to march silently.
Without mention. He mentioned other difficulties, but this he silently passed over.
silentness: State of being silent; stillness; silence.
silisia: sile’zha. A duchy or country now chiefly belonging to Prussia; hence, a species of linen cloth so called; thin coarse linen.
silesian: sile’zhan Pertaining to Silesia; mad in Silesia; as silesian linen.
silex: One of the supposed primitive earths m usually found in the state of stone. When pure, it is perfectly white or colorless. The purer sorts are mountain crystal and quartz. Recent experiments prove this to be a compound substance, the base of which is a metal called silicium. Silica then is an oxyd of silicium.
silcia: One of the supposed primitive earths m usually found in the state of stone. When pure, it is perfectly white or colorless. The purer sorts are mountain crystal and quartz. Recent experiments prove this to be a compound substance, the base of which is a metal called silicium. Silica then is an oxyd of silicium.
silice: In botany, a little pod or bivalvular pericarp, with seeds attached to both sutures.
silicule: In botany, a little pod or bivalvular pericarp, with seeds attached to both sutures.
silicle: In botany, a little pod or bivalvular pericarp, with seeds attached to both sutures.
silicicalcarious: Consisting of silex and calcarious matter.
silicicalce: A mineral of the silicious kind, occurring in amorphous masses; its color is gray or brown.
siliciferous: Producing silex; or united with a portion of silex.
silicify: To convert into silex. The specimens found near Philadelphia are completely silicified.
silicimurite: An earth composed of silex and magnesia.
silicious: Pertaining to silex, or partaking of its nature and qualities.
silicium: The undecomposed and perhaps undecomposable base of silex or silica.
siliculous: Havin silicles or little pods, or pertaining to them.
siliqua: With gold finers, a carat, six of which make a scruple.
A pod; an oblong, membranaceous, bivalvular pericarp, having the seeds fixed to both sutures.
silique: A pod; an oblong, membranaceous, bivalvular pericarp, having the seeds fixed to both sutures.
siliquose: Having that species of pericarp called silique; as siliquous plants.
siliquous: Having that species of pericarp called silique; as siliquous plants.
silk: The fine soft thread produced by the insect called silk-worm or bombyx. That which we ordinarily call silk, is a thread composed of several finer threads which the worm draws from its bowels, like the web of a spider, and with which the silk-worm envelopes itself, forming what is called a cocoon.
Cloth made of silk. In this sense, the word has a plural, silks, denoting different sort and varieties, as black silk, white silk, colored silks.
The filiform style of the female flower of maiz, which resembles real silk in fineness and softness. Virginia silk, a plant of the genus Periploca, which climbs and winds about other plants, trees.
Pertaining to silk; consisting of silk.
silk cotten-tree: A tree of the genus Bombax, growing to an immense size; a native of both the Indies.
silken: Made of silk; as silken cloth; a silken vail.
Like silk; soft to the touch.
Soft; delicate; tender; smooth; as mild and silken language.
Dressed in silk; as a silken wanton.
silk’n. To render soft or smooth.
silkiness: The qualities of silk; softness and smoothness to the feel.
silkmercer: One whose occupation is to weave silk stuffs.
silkworm: The worm which produces silk, of the genus Phalaena. Silk-worms are said to have been first introduced into the Roman empire from China, in the reign of Justinian.
silky: Made of silk; consisting of silk.
Like silk; soft and smooth to the touch.
sill: Properly, the basis of foundationof a thing; appropriately, a piece of timber on which a building rests; the lowest timber of any stucture; as the sills of a house, of a bridge, of a loom and the like.
The timber or stone at the foot of a door; the threshhold.
The timber or stone on which a window frame stands; or the lowest piece in a window frame.
The shaft or thill of a carriage.
sillabub: A liquor made by mixing wine or cider with milk, and thus forming a soft curd.
sillimanite: A mineral found at Saybrook in Connecticut, so named in honor of Prof, Silliman of Yale College. It occurs in long, slender, rhombic prisms, engaged in gneiss. Its color is dark gray and hair brown; luster shining upon the external planes, but brilliant and pseudometallic upon those produced by cleavage in a direction parallel with the longer diagonal of the prism. Hardness about the same with quartz. Specific gravity, 3.410.
silliness: Weakness of understanding; want of sound sense or judgment; simplicity; harmless folly.
silly: Weak in intellect; foolish; witless; destitute of ordinary strength of mind; simple; as a silly man; a silly child.
Proceeding from want of understanding or common judgment; characterized by weakness of folly; unwise; as silly thoughts; silly actions; a silly scheme; writings stupid or silly.
Weak; helpless. After long storms- With which my silly bark was toss d .
sillyhow: The membrane that covers the head of the fetus.
silt: Saltness, or salt marsh or mud.
silure: The sheat-fish; also a name of the sturgeon.
silurus: The sheat-fish; also a name of the sturgeon.
silvan: Pertaining to a wood or grove; inhabiting woods.
Woody; abounding with woods. Betwixt two rows of rocks, a silvan scene.
silver: A metal of a white color and lively brilliancy. It has neither taste nor smell; its specific gravity is 10.552, according to Bergman, but according to Kirwan it is less. A cubic foot weighs about 660 Ibs. Its ductility is little inferior to that of gold. It is harder and more elastic that tin of iron. It is found native in thin plates or leaves, or in fine threads, or it is found mieralized by various substances. Great quanitities of the metal are furnished by the mines of South America, and it is found in small quantities in Norway, Germany, Spain, the United State.
Any thing of soft splendor. Pallas-piteous of her plaintive cries, In slumber clos’d her silver-streaming eyes.
Made of silver; as a silver cup.
White like silver; as silver hair. Others on silver lakes and rivers bath’d Their downy breast.
silver-beater: One that foliates silver, or forms it into a leaf.
silver-bush: A plant, a species of Anthyllis.
silvered: Covered with a thin coat of silver; rendered smooth and lustrous; made white or a hoary.
silver-fish: A fish of the size of a small carp, having a white color, striped with silvery lines.
silvering: Covering the surface with a thin coat of silver; foliating; rendering mildly lustrous; rendering white.
The art, operation or practice of covering the surface of any thing with silver; as the silvering of copper or brass.
silversmith: One whose occupation is to work’in silver, or in manufactures of which the precious metals form a part.
silver-tree: A plant of the genus Protea.
silver-weed: A plant of the genus Potentilla.
silvery: Like silver; having the appearance of silver; white; of a mild luster. Of all the enameled race whose silvery wing Waves to the tepid zephyrs of the spring.
similar: Like; resembling; having a like form or appearance. Similar may signify exactly alike, or having a general likeness, a likeness in the principal points. Things perfectly similar, in their nature, must be of the same essence, or homogeneous; but we generally understand similar to denote a likeness that is not perfect. Many of the statutes of Connecticut are similar to the statutes of Massachusetts on the same subjects. The manners of the several states of New England are similar, the people being derived from common ancestors.
similarity: Likeness; resemblance; as a similarity of features. There is aas a similarity in the features of the Laplanders and Samoiedes, but little similarity between the features of Europeans and the woolly haired Africans.
simile: sim’ily. In rhetoric, similitude; a comparison of two thing which, however different in other respects, have some strong point or points of resemblance; by which comparison, the character or qualities of a thing are illustrated or presented in an impressive light. Thus, the eloquence of Demosthenes was like a rapid torrent; that of Cicero, like a large stream that glides smoothly along with majestic tranquility.
similitude: Likeness; resemblance; likeness in nature, qualities of appearance; as similitude of substance. Let us make man in our image, man in our similitude. Fate some future bard shall join in sad similitude of griefs to mine.
Comparison; simile. Tasso, in his similitude, never departed from the woods.
similor: A name given to an alloy of red copper and zinc, made in the best proportions to imitate silver and gold.
simmer: To boil gently, or with a gentle hissing. Simmering is incipient ebullition, when little bubbles are formed on the edge of the liquor, next to the vessel. These are occasioned by the escape of heat and vapor.
simnel: A kind of sweet cake; a bun.
simoniac: One who buys or sells preferment in the church.
simoniacal: Consisting in simony, or the crime of buying or selling ecclesiastical preferment; as a simoniacal presentation.
simoiacally: With the guilt or offense of simony.
simonious: Partaking of simony; given to simony.
simony: The crime of buying or selling ecclesiastical preferment; or the corrupt presentation of any one to an ecclesiastical benefice of money or reward. By Stat. 31 Elizabeth6. severe penalties are enacted against this crime.
simous: Having a very flat or snub nose, with the end turned up.
Concave; as the simous part of the liver.
simper: To smile in a silly manner.
To smile with an air of silliness.
simpering: The act of smiling with an air of silliness.
simple: Single; consisting of one thing; uncompounded; unmingled; uncombined with any thing else; as a simple substance; a simple idea; a simple sound.
Plain; artless; not given to design, stratagem or duplicity; undesigning; sincere; harmless. A simple husbandman in garments gray.
Artless; unaffected; unconstrained; inartificial; plain. In simple manners all the secret lies.
Unadorned; plain; as a simple style or narration; a simple dress.
Not complex or complicated; as a machine of simple construction.
simpleness: The state or quality of being simple, single or uncompounded; as the simpleness of the elements.
simpler: One that collects simples; as herbalist; asimplist.
simpless: for simplicity or silliness, is not in use.
simpleton: A silly person; a person of weak intellect; a trifler; a foolish person.
simplicain: An artless, unskilled or undesigning person.
simplicity: Singleness; the state of being unmixed or uncompounded; as the simplicity of metals or of earths.
The state of being not complex, or of consisting of few parts; as the simplicity of a machine.
Artlessness of mind; freedom from a propensity to cunning or stratagem; freedom from duplicity; sincerity. Marquis Dorset, a man for his harmless simplicity neither misliked nor much regarded.
Plainness; freedom from artificial ornament; as the simplicity of a dress, of style, of languageSimplicity in writing is the first or excellences.
Plainness; freedom from subtilty or abstruseness; as the simplicity of scriptural doctrines or truth.
simplification: The act of making simple; the act of reducing to simplicity, or to a state not complex.
simplify: To make simple; to reduce what is complex to greater simplicity; to make plain or easy. The collection of duties is drawn to a point, and so far simplified. It is important in scientific pursuits, to be cautious in simplifying our deductions. This is the true way to simplify the study of science.
simplist: One skilled in simples or medical plants.
simply: Without art; without subtilty; artlessly; plainly. Subverting worldly strong and worldly wise By simply meek.
Of itself; without addition; alone. They make that good or evil, which otherwise of itself were not simply the one nor the other.
Merely; solely. Simply the thing I am Shall make me live .
simular: One who simulates or counterfeits something.
simulate: To feign; to counterfeits; to assume the mere appearance of something, without the reality. The wicked often simulate the virtuous and good.
simulating: Feigning; pretending; assuming the appearance of what is not real.
simulation: The act of feigning to be that which is not; the assumption of a deceitful appearance or character. Simulation differs from dissimulation.
