The former denotes the assuming of a false character; the latter denotes the concealment of the true character. Both are comprehended in the word hypocrisy.
simultaneous: Existing or happening at the same time; as simultaneous events. The exchange of ramifications may be simultaneous.
simultaneousness: The state or quality of being or happening at the same time; as the simultaneousness of transactions in two different places.
sin: The voluntary departure of a moral agent from a known rule of rectitude or duty, prescribed by God; any voluntary transgression of the divine law, or violation of a divine command; a wicked act; iniquity. Sin is either a positive act in which a known divine law is violated, or it is the voluntary neglect to obey a positive divine command, or a rule of duty clearly implied in such command. Sin comprehends not action only, but neglect of known duty, all evil thoughts purposes, words and desires, whatever is contrary to God’s commands or law. 7 John 3:4; Matthew 15:3; James 4:17. Sinner neither enjoy the pleasures of nor the peace of piety. Among divines, sin is original or actual. Actual sin, above defined, is the act of a moral agent in violating a known rule of duty. Original sin, as generally understood, is native depravity of heart to the divine will, that corruption of nature of deter
A sin-offering; an offering made to atone for sin. He hath made him to be sin for us, who knew no sin. 2 Corinthians 5:21.
Sin differs from crime, not in nature, but in application. That which is a crime against society, is sin against God.

To depart voluntarily from the path of duty prescribed by God man; to violate the divine law in any particular, by actual transgression or by the neglect or non-observance of its injunctions; to violate any known rule of duty. All have sinned and come short of the glory of God. Romans 3:23. Against thee, thee only, have I sinned. Psalm 51:4.
sinapism: In Pharmacy, a cataplasm composed of mustard seed pulverized, with some other ingredients, and used as an external application. It is a powerful stimulant.
since: After; from the time that. The proper signification of since is after, and its appropriate sense includes the whole period between an event and the present time. I have not seen my brother since January. The Lord hath blessed thee, since my coming. Genesis 30:30. Holy prophets, who have been since the world began. Luke 1:70; John 9:32. Since then denotes, during the whole time after an event; or at any particular time during that period.
Ago; past; before this. “About two years since, an event happened,” that is, two years having passed.

Because that; this being the fact that. Since truth and constancy are vain, since neither love nor sese of pain nor force of reason can persuade, then let example be obey’d. Since, when it precedes a noun, is called a preposition, but when it precedes sentence it is called an adverb. The truth is, the character of the word is the same in both cases. It is probably an obsolete participle, and according to the usual classification of words, may be properly ranked with the prepositions. In strictness, the last clause of the passage above cited is the case absolute. “The Lord hath blessed the, since my coming,” that is, my arrival being past. So, since the world began, is strictly past the world began, the beginning of the world being past. In the first case, since considered as a preposition, has coming, a noun, for its object, and in the latter case, the clause of a sentence. So we say, ag
sincere: Pure; unmixed. As new-born babes, desire the sincere milk of the word. 7 Peter 2:2. A joy which never was sincere till now. There is no sincere acid in any animal juice. I would have all gallicisms avoided, that our tongue may be sincere.
Unhurt; uninjured. The’ inviolable body stood sincere.

Being in reality what it appears to be; not feigned; not simulated; not assumed or said for the sake of appearance; real; not hypocritical or pretended. This is the present use of the word. Let your intentions be pure and your declarations sincere. Let love and friendship be sincere. No prayer can avail with a heart-searching God, unless it is sincere.
sincerely: Honestly; with real purity of heart; without simulation or disguise; to love virtue sincerely.
sincerity: Honesty of mind or intention; freedom from simulation or hypocrisy. We may question a man’s prudence, when we cannot question his sincerity.
Freedom from hypocrisy, disguise or false pretense; as the sincerity of a declaration or of love.
sinciput: The fore part of the head from the forehead to the coronal suture.
sine: In geometry, the right sine of an arch or arc, is a line drawn from one end of that arch perpendicular to the radius drawn through the other end, and is always equal to half the chord of double the arch.
sinecure: An office which has revenue without employment; in church affairs, a benefice without cure of souls. Sine die, An adjournment sine die is and adjournment without fixing the time of resuming business. When a defendant is suffered to go sine die, he is dismissed the court.
sinew: In anatomy, a tendon; that which unites a muscle to a bone.
In the plural, strength; or rather that which supplies strength. Money is the sinews of war.
sinewed: Furnished with sinews; as a strong-sinewed youth.
Strong; firm; vigorous. When he sees ourselves well sinewed to our defense.
sinew-shrumk: Gaunt-bellied; having the sinews under belly shrunk by excess of fatigue, as a horse.
sinewy: Consisting of a sinew or nerve. The sinewy thread my brain lets fall.
Nervous; strong well braced with sinews; vigorous; firm; as the sinewy Ajax. The northern people are large, fair complexioned, strong, sinewy and courageous.
sinful: Tainted with sin; wicked; iniquitous; criminal; unholy; as sinful men. Ah, sinful nation, a people laden with iniquity! Isaiah 1:4.
Containing sin, or consisting in sin; contrary to the laws of God; as sinful actions; sinful thoughts; sinful words.
sinfully: In a manner which the laws of God do not permit; wickedly; iniquitously; criminally.
sinfulness: The quality of being sinful or contrary to the divine will; wickedness; iniquity; criminality; as the sinfulness of an action; the sinfulness of thoughts or purposes.
Wickedness; corruption; depravity; as the sinfulness of men or of the human race.
sing: To utter sounds with various inflections of melodious modulations of voice, as fancy may dictate, or according to the notes of a song or tune The noise of them that sing do I hear Exodus 32:18.
To utter sweet or melodious sounds, as birds. It is remarkable that the female of no species of birds ever sings. And singing birds in silver cages hung.
To make a small shrill sound; as, the air sings in passing through a crevice. O’er his head the flying spear sung innocent, and spent its force in air.
To tell or relate something in numbers of verse. Sing of human hope by cross event destroy’d.
singe: sinj. To burn slightly or superficially; to burn the surface of a thing as the nap of cloth, or the hair of the skin; as, to singe off the beard. Thus riding on his curls, he seem’d to pass A rolling fire along, and singe the grass.
A burning of the surface; a slight burn.
singer: One versed in music, or one whose occupation is to sing; as a chorus of singers.
singing: Uttering melodious or musical notes; making a shrill sound; celebrating in song; reciting in verse.
The act of uttering sounds with musical inflections; musical articulation; the utterance of melodious notes.
singing-book: A music book, as it ought to be called; a book containing tunes.
singingly: With sounds like singing; with a kind of tune.
singing-man: A man who sings, or is employed to sing; as in cathedrals.
singing-master: A music master; one that teaches vocal music
single: Separate; one; only; individual; consisting of one only; as a single star; a single city; a single act.
Particular; individual. No single man is born with a right of controlling the opinions of all the rest.
Uncompounded. Simple ideas are opposed to complex, and single to compound./ .
Alone; having no companion or assistant. Who single hast maintain’d against revolted multitudes the cause of truth.
Unmarried; as a single man; a single woman.
singleness: The state of being one only or separate from all others; the opposite of doubleness, complication or multiplicity.
Simplicity; sincerity; purity of mind or purpose; freedom from duplicity; as singleness of belief; singleness of heart.
singly: Individually; particularly; as, to make men singly and personally good.
Only; by himself. Look thee, ‘tis so, thou singly honest man.
Without partners, companions or associates; as, to attack another singly. At ombre singly to decide their doom.
singular: Single; not complex or compound. That idea which represents one determinate thing, is called a singular idea, whether simple, complex or compound.
In grammar, expressing one person or thing; as the singular number. The singular number stands opposed to dual and plural.
Particular; existing by itself; unexampled; as a singular phenomenon. Your case is hard, but not singular.
Remarkable; eminent; unusual; rare; as a man of singular gravity, or singular attainments.
singularity: Peculiarity; some character or quality of a thing by which it is distinguished from all, or from most others. Pliny addeth this singularity to that soil, that the second year the very falling of the seeds yieldeth corn.
An uncommon character or form; something curious or remarkable. I took notice of this little figure for the singularity of the instrument.

Particular privilege, prerogative or distinction. No bishop of Rome ever took upon him this name of singularity, (universal bishop.) Catholicism-must be understood in opposition to the legal singularity of the Jewish nation.
Character or trait of character different from that of others; peculiarity. The singularity of living according to the strict precepts of the gospel is highly to be commended.
singularly: Peculiarly; in a manner or degree not common to others. It is no disgrace to be singularly good.
So as to express one or the singular number.
sinister: Left; on the left hand, or the side of the left hand; opposed to dexter or right; as the sinister cheek; or the sinister side of an escutcheon.
Evil; bad; corrupt; perverse; dishonest; as sinister means; sinister purpose. He scorns to undermine another’s interest by any sinister or inferior arts.
Sinister aspect, in astrology, an appearance of two planets happening according to the succession of the signs; as Saturn in Aries, and Mars in the same degree of Gemini.
sinistrorsal: Rising from left to right, as a spiral line or helix.
sinistrous: Being on the left side; inclined to the left.
Wrong; absurd; perverse. A knave or fool can do no harm, even by the most sinistrous and absurd choice.
sinistrously: With a tendency to use the left as the stronger hand.
sink: pret. sunk; pp. id. The old pret. sank is nearly obsolete.

To fall by the force of greater gravity, in a medium or substance of less specific gravity; to subside; opposed to swim or float. Some species of wood or timber will sink in water. Oil will not sink in water and many other liquids, for it is specifically lighter. I sink in deep mire. Psalm 69:2.
To fall gradually. He sunk down in his chariot, 2 Kings 9:24.
To enter or penetrate into any body. The stone sunk into his forehead. 1 Samuel 17:49.
To fall; to become lower; to subside or settle to a level. The Alps and Pyrenees sink before him.
sinking: Falling; subsiding; depressing; declining. Sinking fund, in fiance, a fund created for sinking or paying a public debt, or purchasing the stock for the government.
sinless: Free from sin; pure; perfect. Christ yielded a sinless obedience.
Free from sin; innocent; as a sinless soul.
sinner: One that has voluntarily violated the divine law; a moral agent who has voluntarily disobeyed any divine precept, or neglected any known duty.
It is used in contradistinction to saint, to denote an unregenerate person; one who has not received the pardon of his sins.
To act as a sinner; in ludicrous language. Whether the charmer sinner it or saint it.
sin-offering: A sacrifice for sin; something offered as an expiation for sin. Exodus 29:36.
sinoper: Red ferruginous quartz, of a blood or brownish red color, sometimes with a tinge or yellow. It occurs in small but very perfect crystals, and in masses that resemble some varieties of jasper.
sinople: Red ferruginous quartz, of a blood or brownish red color, sometimes with a tinge or yellow. It occurs in small but very perfect crystals, and in masses that resemble some varieties of jasper.
sinter: In mineralogy, calcarious sinter is a variety of carbonate of lime, composed of a series of successive layers, concentric, plane or undulated, and nearly or quite parallel. It appears under various forms. Silicious sinter is white or grayish, light, brittle, porous, and of a fibrous texture. Opaline silicious sinter somewhat resembles opal. It is whitish, with brownish, blackish or bluish spots, and its fragments present dendritic appearances. Pearl sinter or fiorite occurs in stalactitic, cylindrical, botryoidal, and globular masses, white or grayish.
sinuate: To wind; to turn; to bend in and out.
In botany, a sinuate leaf is one that has large curved breaks in the margin, resembling bays, as in the oak.
sinuation: A winding or bending in and out.
sinuosity: The quality of bending or curving in and out; or a series of bends and turns in arches or other irregular figures.
sinuous: Wind; crooked; bending in and out; as a sinuous pipe. Streaking the ground with sinuous trace.
sinus: A bay of the sea; a recess in the shore, or an opening in the land.
In anatomy, a cavity in a bone or other part, wider at the bottom than at the entrance.
In surgery, a little cavity or sack in which pus is collected; an abscess with only a small orifice.
sip: To take a fluid into the mouth in small quantities by the lips; as, to sip wine; to sip tea or coffee.
To drink or imbibe in small quantities. Every herb that sips the dew.
To draw into the mouth; to extract; as, a bee sips nectar from the flowers.
To drink out of. They skim the floods, and sip the purple flow’rs.
To drink a small quantity; to take a fluid with the lips.
sipe: To 00ze; to issue slowly; as a fluid.
siphilitic: Pertaining to the venereal disease, or partaking of its nature.
siphon: A bent pipe or tube whose legs are of unequal length, used for drawing liquor out of a vessel by causing it to rise over the rim or top. For this purpose, the shorter leg is inserted in the liquor, and the air is exhausted by being drawn through the longer leg. The liquor then rises by the weight of the atmosphere to supply the vacuum, till it reaches the top of the vessel, and then descends in the longer leg of the siphon .
The pipe by which the chambers of a shell communicate.
siphunculated: Having a little siphon or spout, as a valve.
sipped: Drawn in with the lips; imbibed in small quantities.
si quis: These words give name to a notification by a candidate for orders of his intention to inquire whether any impediment may be alleged against him.
sir: A word or respect used in addresses to men, as madam is in addresses to women. It signifies properly lord, corresponding to dominus in Latin, in Spanish, and herr in German. It is used in the singular or plural. Speak onBut sirs, be sudden in the execution.
The title of a knight or baronet; as Sir Horace Vere.
It is used by Shakespeare for man. In the election of a sir so rare.
In American colleges, the title of a master of arts.
It is prefixed to loin, in sirloin; as a sirloin of beef. This practice is said to have originated in the knighting of a loin of beef by one of the English kings in a fit of good humor.
sire: A father; used in poetry. And raise his issue like a loving sire.
The male parent of a beast; particularly used of horses; as, the horse had a good sire, but a bad dam.
It is used in composition; as in grandsire, for grandfather; great grandsire, great grandfather.
To beget; to procreate; used of beasts.
siren: A mermaid. In ancient mythology, a goddess who enticed men into her power by the charms of music, and devoured them. Hence in modern use, an enticing woman; a female rendered dangerous by her enticements. Sing, siren, to thyself, and I will dote .
A species of lizard in Carolina, constituting a peculiar genus, destitute of posterior extremities and pelvis.
Pertaining to a siren, or to the dangerous enticements of music; bewitching; fascinating; as a siren song.
siriasis: An inflammation of the brain, proceeding from the excessive heat of the sun; phrensy almost peculiar to children.
sirius: The large and bright star called the dog star, in the mouth of the constellation Canis major.
sirloin: A particular piece of beef so called.
sirocco: A pernicious wind that blows from the south east in Italy, called the Syrian wind. It is said to resemble the steam from the mouth of an oven.
sirrah: A word of reproach and contempt; used in addressing vile characters. Go sirrah, to my call.
sirup: sur'up, The sweet juice of vegetables or fruits, or other juice sweetened; or sugar boiled. with vegetable infusions.
siruped: Moistened or tinged with sirup or sweet juice.
sirupy: Like sirup, or partaking of its qualities.
siskin: A bird, the green finch; another name of the aberdavine. The siskin or aberdavine is the Fringilla spinus; the green finch, the Fr. chloris, a different species.
siss: To hiss; a legitimate word in universal popular use in New England.
sister: A female born of the same patents; correlative to brother.
A woman of the same faith; a female fellow christian. If a brother or sister be naked and destitute of daily food. James 2:15.
A female of the same kind.
One of the same kind, or of the same condition; as sister- fruits.
A female of the same society; as the nuns of a convent.
sisterhood: Sisters collectively, or a society of sisters; or a society of females united in one faith or order.
The officer of duty of a sister.
sisterly: Like a sister; becoming a sister; affectionate; as sisterly kindness.
sit: To rest upon the buttocks, as animals; as, to sit on a sofa or on the ground.
To perch; to rest on the feet; as fowls.
To occupy a seat or place in an official capacity. The scribes and the Pharisees sit in Moses’ seat. Matthew 23:2.
To be in a state of rest or idleness. Shall your brethren go to war, and shall ye sit here? NWumbers 32:6.
site: Situation; local position; as the site of a city or of a house.
Aseat or ground-plot; as a mill-site. But we usually say, mill-seat, by which we understand the place where a mill stands, or a place convenient for a mill.
The posture of a thing with respect of itself. The semblance of a lover fix’d in melancholy site.
sitfast: A hard knob growing on a horse’s back under the saddle.
sitter: One that sits. The Turks are great sitters.
A bird that sits or incubates.
sitting: Resting on the buttocks, or on the feet, as fowls; incubating; brooding; being in the actual exercise of authority, or being assembled for that purpose.
The posture of being on a seat.
The act of placing one’s self on a seat; as a sitting down.
The act or time of resting in a posture for a painter or take the likeness. For a portrait, six or seven sittings may be required.
situate: Placed, with respect to any other object; as a town situate on a hill or on the sea shore.
Placed; consisting. Pleasure situate in hill and dale' .
situated: Seated, placed or standing with respect to any other object; as a city situated on a declivity, or in front of a lake; a town well situated for trade or manufactures; an observatory well situated for observation of the stars. New York is situated in the forty first degree of N. latitude.
Placed or being in any state or condition with regard to men or things. Observe how the executor is situated with respect to the heirs.
situation: Position; seat; location in respect to something else. The situation of London is more favorable for foreign commerce than that of Paris. The situation of a stranger among people of habits differing from his own, cannot be pleasant.
State; condition. He enjoys a situation of ease and tranquility: .
Circumstances; temporary state; used of persons in a dramatic scene.
Place; office. He has a situation in the war department, or under government.
sivan: The third month of the Jewish ecclesiastical year, answering to part of our May and part of June.
six: Twice three; one more than five.
The number of six or twice three. To be at six and seven, or as more generally used, at sixes and sevens, is to be in disorder.
sixfold: Six times repeated; six double; six times as much.
sixpence: An English silver coin of the value of six pennies; half a shilling.
The value of six pennies or half a shilling.
six-penny: Worth sixpence; as a six-penny loaf.
six-petaled: In botany, having six distinct petals or flower leaves.
sixscore: Six times twenty; one hundred and twenty.
sixteen: Six and ten; noting the sum of six and ten.
sinteenth: The sixth after the tenth; the ordinal of sixteen.
sixth: The first after the fifth; the ordinal of six.
In music, a hexachord, as interval of two kinds; the minor sixth, consisting of three tones and two semitones major, and the major sixth, composed of four tones and a major semitone.
sixty: The number of six times ten.
sizable: Being of reasonable of suitable size; as sizable timber.
size: Bulk; bigness; magnitude; extent of superficies. Size particularly expresses thickness; as the size of a tree or of a mast; the size of a ship or of a rock. A man may be tall, with little size of body.
Figurative bulk; condition as to rank and character; as men of less size and quality.
To adjust or arrange according to size or bulk.
To settle; to fix the standard of; as, to size weights and measures.
sized: Adjusted according to size; prepared with size.
a. Having a particular magnitude. And as my love is siz’d my fear is so.
sizel: In coining, the residue of bars of silver, after pieces are cut out for coins.
sizier: In the university of Cambridge, a student of the rank nest below that of a pensioner.
siziness: Glutinousness; viscousness; the quality of size; as the siziness of blood.
sizy: Glutinous; thick and viscous; ropy; having the adhesiveness of size; as sizy blood.
skain: A knot of thread, yarn or silk, or a number of knots collected.
skald: An ancient Scandinavian poet or bard.
skate: A sort or shoe furnished with a smooth iron for sliding on ice.
To slide or move on skates.
A fish of the ray kind, (Raia Batis;) called the variegated ray-fish, It is a flat fish, the largest and thinnest of the genus, some of them weighing nearly two hundred pounds.
skean: A short sword, or a knife.
skeel: A shallow wooden vessel for holding milk or cream.
skeet: A long scoop used to wet the sides of ships or the sails.
skeleton: The bones of an animal body, separated from the flesh and retained in their natural position or connections. When the bones are connected by the natural ligaments, it is called a natural skeleton; when by wires, or any foreign substance, an artificial skeleton.
The compages, general structure or frame of any thing; the principal parts that support the rest, but without the appendages.
A very thin or lean person.
skep: A sort of basket, narrow at the bottom and wide at the top.
sketch: An outline or general delineation of any thing; a first rough or incomplete draught of a plan or any design; as the sketch of a building; the sketch of an essay.
To draw the outline or general figure of a thing; to make a rough draught.
To plan by giving the principal points or ideas.
skew: To look obliquely upon; to notice slightly.
To shape or form in an oblique way.
skewer: A pin of wood or iron for fastening meat to a spit, or for keeping it in form while roasting.
skid: A curving timber to preserve a ship’s side from injury by heavy bodies hoisted or lowered against it; a slider.
A chain used for fastening the wheel of a wagon, to prevent its turning when descending a steep hill.
skiff: A small light boar resembling a yawl.
é. To pass over in a light boat.
skill: Calleo, that is to strain, stretch, reach, and with to perfect, that is, to make sound, or to reach the utmost limit. The sense of folly, error, sin, preverseness, is from wandering

The familiar knowledge of any art or science, united with readiness and dexterity in execution or performance, or in the application of the art or science to practical purposes. Thus we speak of the skill of a mathematician, of a surveyor, of a physician or surgeon, of a mechanic or seaman. So we speak of skill in management or negotiation.
To be knowing in; to be dextrous in performance.
skilled: Having familiar knowledge united with readiness and dexterity in the application of it; familiarly acquainted with; followed by in; as a professor skilled in logic or geometry; one skilled in the art of engraving.
skillful: Knowing; well versed in any art; hence, dextrous; able in management; able to perform nicely any manual operation in the arts or professions; as a skillful mechanic; a skillful operator in surgery.
Well versed in practice; as a skillful physician. It is followed by at or in; as skillful at the organ; skillful in drawing.
skillfully: With skill; with nice art; dextrously; as a machine skillfully made; a ship skillfully managed.
skillfulness: The quality of possessing skill; dextrousness; ability to perform well in any art or business, or to manage affairs with judgement and exactness, or according to good taste or just rules; knowledge and ability derived from experience.
skilling: An isle or bay of a barn; also, a skight addition to a cottage
skim: Scum; the thich matter that forms on the surface of a liquor.
To take off the thick gross matter which separates from any liquid substance and collects on the surface; as, to skim milk by taking off the cream.
To take off by skimming; as, to skim cream.
To pass near the surface; to brush the surface slightly.
To pass lightly; to glide along in an even smooth course, or without flapping; as, an eagle or hawk skims along the etherial regions.
skim-coulter: A coulter for paring off the surface of land.
skimmer: An utensil in the form of a scoop; used for skimming liquors.
One that skims over a subject.
skim-milk: Milk from which the cream has been taken.
skimmings: Matter skimmed from the surface of liquors.
skin: The natural covering of animal bodies, consisting of the cuticle or scarf-skin, the rete mucosum, and the cutis or hide. The cuticle is very thin and insensible; the cutis is thicker and very sensible.
A hide; a pelt; the skin of an animal separated from the body, whether green, dry or tanned.
The body; the person; in ludicrous language .
The bark or husk of a plant; the exterior coat of fruits and plants.
To strip off the skin or hide; to flay; to peel.
skink: A small lizard of Egypt; also, the common name of a genus of lizards, with a long body entirely covered with rounded imbricate scales, all natives of warm climates.
To bestow, to make a present. To serve drink.
skinless: Having a thin skin; as skinless fruit.
skinner: One that deals in skins, plets or hides.
skinny: Consisting of skin, or of skin only; wanting flesh.
skip: To leap; to bound; to spring; as a goat or lamb. To skip over, to pass without notice; to omit.
To pass over or by; to omit; to miss.
A leap; a bound; a spring.
skipper: The master of a small trading vessel.
A youngling; a young thoughtless person.
skipping: Leaping; bounding. Skipping notes, in music, are notes that are not in regular course, but separate.
skirmish: A slight fight in war; a light combat by armies at a great distance from each other, or between detachments and small parties.
Acontest; a contention. They never meet but there’s a skirmish of wit.
To fight slightly or in small parties.
skirmishing: Fighting slightly or in detached parties.
The act of fighting in a loose or slight encounter.
skirr: To scour; to ramble over in order to clear.
To scour; to scud; to run hastily.
skirret: A plant of the genus Sium.
skirt: The lower and loose part of a coat or other garment; the part below the waist; as the skirt of a coat or mantle. 7 Samuel 15:27.
The edge of any part of dress.
Border; edge; margin; extreme part; as the skirt of a forest; the skirt of a town.
The diaphragm or midriff in animals. To spread the skirt over, in Scripture, to take under one’s care and protection Ruth 3:9.
skit: A wanton girl; a reflection; a jeer or jibe; a whim.
skittish: Shy; easily frightened; shunning familiarity; timorous; as a restif skittish jade. L’Estrange.
skittishness: Shyness; aptness to fear approach; timidity.
skolezite: A mineral allied to Thomsonite, occurring crystallized and massive, colorless and nearly transparent. When a small portion of it is placed in the exterior flame of the blowpipe, it twists like a worm, becomes opake, and is converted into a blebby colorless glass.
skoradite: A mineral of a greenish color of different shades or brown and nearly black, resembling the martial arseniate of copper. It occurs massive, but generally crystallized in rectangular prisms.
skringe: properly scringe; a vulgar corruption of cringe.
skulk: To lurk; to withdraw into a corner or into a close place for concealment
skull: The bone that forms the exterior of the head, and incloses the brain; the brain-pan. It is composed of several parts united at the sutures.
A person. Skulls that cannot teach and will not learn.
Skull, for skeal or school, of fish .
skull-cap: A plant of the genus Scutellaria.
skunk: In America, the popular name of a fetid animal of the weasel kind; the Viverra Mephitis of Linne.
skunk-cabbage: A plant vulgarly so called, the Tetodes fatidus, so named from its smell.
skunk-weed: A plant vulgarly so called, the Tetodes fatidus, so named from its smell.
sky: The aerial region which surrounds the earth; the apparent arch or vault of heaven, which in a clear day is of a blue color.
sky-colored: Like the sky in color; blue; azure.
skyish: Like the sky, or approaching the sky. The skyish head of blue Olympus.
skylark: A lark that mounts and sings as it flies.
sky-light: A window placed in the top of a house or ceiling of a room for admission of light.
sky-rocket: A rocket that ascends high and burns as it flies; a species of fire works.
slab: A plane or table of stone; as a marble slab.
An outside piece taken from timber in sawing it into boards, planks.
slabs of tin: the lesser masses which the workers cast the metal into. These are run into molds of stone.
slabber: To let the saliva or other liquid fall from the mouth carelessly; to drivel. It is also written slaver.
To sup up hastily, as liquid food.
To wet and foul by liquids suffered to fall carelessly from the mouth.
slab-line: A line or small rope by which seamen truss up the main-sail or fore-sail.
slack: Not tense; not hard drawn; not firmly extended; as a slack rope; slack rigging; slack shrouds.
Weak; remiss; not holding fast; as a slack hand.
Remiss; backward; not using due diligence; not earnest or eager; as slack in duty or service; slack in business.
Not violent; not rapid; slow; as a slack pace.
Partially; insufficiently; not intensely; as slack dried hops; bread slack baked.
slack in stays: in seamen’s language, slow in going about; as a ship.
slack water: in seamen’s language, the time when the tide runs slowly, or the water is at rest; or the interval between the flux and reflux of the tide.
slacken: To become less tense, firm or rigid; to decrease in tension; as, a wet cord slackens in dry weather.
To be remiss or backward; to neglect. Deuteronomy 23:21.
To lose cohesion or the quality of adhesion; as, lime slacks and crumbles into power.
To abate; to become less violent. Whence these raging fires will slacken, if his breath stir not their flames.
To lose rapidity; to become more slow; as, a current of water slackens; the tide slackens.
slackness: Looseness; the state opposite to tension; not tightness or rigidness; as the slackness of a cord or rope.
Remissness; negligence; inattention; as the slackness of men in business or duty; slackness in the performance of engagements.
Slowness; tardiness; want of tendency; as the slackness of flesh to heal.
slade: A little dell or valley; also, a flat piece of low moist ground.
slag: The dross or recrement of a metal; or vitrified cinders.
slain: of slay; so written for slayen. Killed.
slake: To quench; to extinguish; as, to slake thirst. And slake the heav’nly fire.
To go out; to become extinct.
slam: To strike with force and noise; to shut against; a violent shutting of a door.
To win all the tricks in a hand; as we say, to take all at a stroke or dash.
A violent driving and dashing against; a violent shutting of a door.
slander: A false tale or report maliciously uttered. and tending to injure the reputation of another by lessening him in the esteem of his fellow citizens, by exposing min to impeachment and punishment, or by impairing his means of lining; defamation. Slander, that worst of poisons, ever finds an easy entrance to ignoble minds.
To defame; to injure by maliciously uttering a false report respecting one; to tarnish or impair the reputation of one by false tales, maliciously told or propagated.
slandered: Defamed; injured in good name by false and malicious reports.
slanderer: A defamer; one who injures another by maliciously reporting something to his prejudice.
slanderous: That utters defamatory words or tales; as a slanderous tongue.
Containing slander or defamation; calumnious; as slanderous words, speeches or reports, false and maliciously uttered.
slanderously: With slander; calumniously; with false and malicious reproach.
slanderousness: The state or quality of being slanderous or defamatory.
slang: old pret. of sling. We now use slung.
slant: Sloping; oblique; inclined from a direct line, whether horizontal or perpendicular; as a slanting ray of light; a slanting floor.
To turn form a direct line; to give an oblique or sloping direction to.
An oblique reflection or gibe; a sarcastic remark.
A copper coin of Sweden, of which 196 pass for one rix- dollar.
stanting: Sloping; oblique; inclined from a direct line, whether horizontal or perpendicular; as a slanting ray of light; a slanting floor.
slantingly: With a slope or inclination; also, with an oblique hint or remark.
slap: A blow given with the open hand, or with something broad.
To strike with the open hand, or with something broad.
With a sudden and violent blow.
slash: To cut by striking violently and at random; to cut in long cuts.
To strike violently and at random with a sword, hanger or other edger instrument; to lay about one with blows. Hewing and slashing at their idle shades.
A long cut; a cut made at random.
slashing: Striking violently and cutting at random.
slat: A narrow piece of board or timber used to fasten together larger pieces; as the slats of a cart ora chair.
slatch: In seamen’s language, the period of a transitory breeze.
slate: An argillaceous stone which readily splits into plates; argillite; argillaceous shist.
A piece of smooth argillaceous stone, used for covering buildings.
A piece of smooth stone of the above species, used for writing on.
To cover with slate or plates of stone; as, to slate a roof.
To set a dog loose at any thing.
slete: To set a dog loose at any thing.
slate-ax: A mattock with an ax-end; used in slating.
slater: To be careless of dress and dirty .
To be careless, negligent or awkward; to spill carelessly.
slattern: A woman who is negligent of her dress, or who suffers her clothes and furniture to be in disorder; one who is not neat and nice.
To slattern away, to consume carelessly or wastefully; to waste.
slaty: Resembling slate; having the nature or properties of slate; as a slaty color or texture; a slaty feel.
slaughter: In a general sense, a killing. Applied to men, slaughter usually denotes great destruction of life by violent means; as the slaughter of men in battle.
Applied to beasts, butchery; a killing of oxen or other beasts for market.
To kill; to slay; to make great destruction of life; as, to slaughter men in battle.
slaughter-house: slaw’ter-house, A house where beasts are butchered for the market.
slaughtering: slaw’tering, Killing; destroying human life; butchering,
slave: A person who is wholly subject to the will of another; one who has no will of his own, but whose person and services are wholly under the control of another. In the early state of the world, and to this day among some barbarous nations, prisoners of war are considered and treated as slaves. The slaves of modern times are more generally purchased, like horses and oxen.
One who has lost the poser of resistance; or one who surrenders himself to any power whatever; as a slave to passion, to lust, to ambition.
A mean person; one in the lowest state of life.
A drudge; one who labors like a slave.
To drudge; to toil; to labor as a slave.
slaver: To suffer the spittle to issue from the mouth.
To smear with saliva issuing from the mouth; to defile with drivel.
slavery: Bondage; the state of entire subjection of one person to the will of another. Slavery is the obligation to labor for the benefit of the master, without the contract of consent of the servant. Slavery may proceed from crimes, from captivity or from debt. Slavery is also voluntary or involuntary; voluntary, when a person sells or yields his own person to the absolute command of another; involuntary, when he is placed under the absolute power of another without his own consent. Slavery no longer exists in Great Britain, not in the northern states of America.
The offices of a slave; drudgery.
slave-trade: The barbarous and wicked business of purchasing men and women, transporting them to a distant country and selling them for slaves.
slaveish: Pertaining to slaves; servile; mean; base; such as becomes a slave; as a slavish dependence on the great.
Servile; laborious; consisting in drudgery; as a slavish life.
slavishness: The state or quality of being slavish; servility; meanness.
slay: To kill; to put to death by a weapon or by violence. We say, he slew a man with a sword, with a stone, or with a club, or with other arms; but we never say, the serif slays a malefactor with a halter, or a man is slain on the gallows or by poison. So the slay retains something of its primitive sense of striking or beating. It is particularly applied to killing in battle, but is properly applied also to the killing of a individual man or beast.
slayer: One that slays; a killer; a murderer; an assassin; a destroyer of life.
sleave: The knotted or entangled part of silk or thread; silk or thread untwisted.
To separate threads; or to divide a collection of threads; to sley; a word used by weavers.
sleazy: Thin; flimsy; wanting firmness of texture or substance; as sleezy silk or muslin.
sleezy: Thin; flimsy; wanting firmness of texture or substance; as sleezy silk or muslin.
sled: A carriage or vehicle moved on runners, much used in America for conveying heavy weights in winter, as timber, wood, stone and the like.
To convey or transport on a sled; as, to sled wood or timber.
sledding: The act of transporting on a sled.
The means of conveying on sleds; snow sufficient for the running of sleds. Thus we say in America, when there is snow sufficient to run a sled, it is good sledding during the winter.
sledge: A larger heavy hammer; used chiefly by ironsmiths.
In England, a sled; a vehicle moved on runners or on low wheels. In this sense, the word is not used in America; but the same word is used in a somewhat different sense, and written sleigh.
sleek: Smooth; having an even smooth surface; whence, glossy; as sleek hair. So sleek her skin, so faultless was her make.
Not rough or harsh, Those rugged names to our like mouths grow sleek.
To make even and smooth; as, to sleek the hair.
To render smooth, soft and glossy. Gentle, my lord, sleek o’er your rugged looks.
sleeky: Of a sleek or smooth appearance.
sleep: To take rest by a suspension of the voluntary exercise of the powers of the body and mind. The proper time to sleep in during the darkness of night.
To rest; to be unemployed; to be inactive or motionless; as, the sword sleeps in its sheath.
To rest; to lie or be still; not to be noticed or agitated. The question sleeps for the present.
To live thoughtlessly. We sleep over our happiness.
sleep of plants: a state of plants at night, when their least droop or are folded.
sleeper: A person that sleeps; also, a drone or lazy person.
That which lies dormant, as a law not executed.
AN animal that lies dormant in winter, as the bear, the marmot.
In building, the oblique rafter that lies in a gutter,
In New England, a floor timber.
sleepily: Dully; in a lazy manner; heavily Raleigh .
sleeping: The state of resting in sleep.
sleepless: Having no sleep; without sleep; wakeful.
Having no rest; perpetually agitated; as Biscay’s sleepless bay.
sleepy: Not awake. She wak’d her sleep crew.
Tending to induce sleep; soporiferous; somniferous; as a sleepy drink or potion.
sleet: A fall of hail or snow and rain together, usually in fine particles.
In gunnery, the part of the mortar passing form the chamber to the trunnions for strengthening that part.
To snow or hail with a mixture of rain.
sleeve: The part of a garment that is fitted to cover the arm; as the sleeve of a coat or gown.

The raveled sleeve of car, in Shakespeare. To laugh in the sleeve, to laugh privately or unperceived; that is perhaps, originally, by hiding the face under the sleeve or arm. To hang on the sleeve, to be or make dependent on others.
To furnish with sleeves; as a sleeveless coat.
Wanting a cover, pretext or palliation; unreasonable; as a sleeveless tale of tran substantiation; a sleeveless errand.
sleid: To sley or prepare for use in the weaver’s sley or slaie.
sleigh: A vehicle moved on runners, and greatly used in America for transporting persons or goods on snow or ice.
sleight: An artful trick; sly artifice; a trick or feat so dexterously performed that the manner of performance escapes observation; as sleight of hand.
slender: Thin; small in circumference compared with the length; not thick; as a slender stem or stalk of a plant.
Small in the waist; not thick or gross. A slender waist is considered as a beauty.
Not strong; small; slight. Mighty hearts are held in slender chains.
Weak; feeble; as slender hope; slender probabilities; a slender constitution.
Small; inconsiderable; as a man of slender parts.
slenderly: Slightly; meanly; as a debt to be slenderly regarded.
Insufficiently; as a table’ slenderly supplied.
slenderness: Thinness; smallness of diameter in proportion to the length; as the slenderness of a hair.
Want of bulk or strength; as the slenderness of a cord or chain.
Weakness; slightness; as the slenderness of a reason.
Weakness; feebleness; as the slenderness of a constitution.
Want of plenty; as the slenderness of supply.
sley: To separate; to part threads and arrange them in a reed; as weavers.
slice: To cut into thin pieces, or to cut off a thin broad piece.
A thin broad piece cut off; as a slice of bacon; a slice of cheese; a slice of bread.
A broad piece’ as a slice of plaster.
slich: The ore of a metal when pounded and prepared for working.
slick: the popular pronunciation of sleek, and so written by some authors.
slickensides: A name which workmen give to a variety of galena in Derbyshire.
slide: To move along the surface of any body by slipping, or without bounding or rolling; to slip; to glide; as, a sled slides on snow and ice; a snow-slip slides down the mountain’s side.
To move along the surface without stepping; as, a man slides on ice.
To pass inadvertently. Make a door and a bar for thy mouth; beware thou slide not by it.
To pass smoothly along without jerks or agitation; as, a ship or boat slides through the water.
slider: The part of an instrument or machine that slides.
sliding: Moving along the surface by slipping; gliding; passing smoothly, easily or imperceptibly.
sliding-rule: A mathematical instrument used to determine measure or quantity without compasses, by sliding the parts one by another.
slight: Weak; inconsiderable; not forcible; as a slight impulse; a slight effort.
Not deep; as a slight impression.
Not violent; as a slight disease, illness or indisposition.
Trifling; of no great importance. Slight is the subject, but not so the praise.
Not strong; not cogent. Some firmly embrace doctrines upon slight grounds.
slightly: Weakly; superficially; with inconsiderable force or effect; in a small degree; as a man slightly wounded; an audience slightly affected with preaching.
Negligently; without regard; with moderate contempt.
slightness: Weakness; want of force or strength; superficialness; as the slightness of a wound or an impression.
Negligence; want of attention; want of vehemence. How does it reproach the slightness of out sleepy heartless addresses!
slily: With artful or dexterous secrecy. Satan slily robs us of our grand treasure.
slim: Slender; of small diameter or thickness in proportion to the highth; as a slim person; a slim tree.
slime: Soft moist earth having an adhesive quality; viscous mud. The had brick for stone, and slime had they for mortar. Genesis 11:3.
slime-pit: A pit of slime or adhesive mire.
sliminess: The quality of slime; consisting of slime.
slimy: Abounding with slime; consisting of slime.
Overspread with slime; as a slimy eel.
Viscous; glutinous; as a slimy soil.
sliness: Dexterous artifice to conceal any thing; artful secrecy.
sling: An instrument for throwing stones, consisting of a strap and two strings; the stone being lodged in the strap, is thrown by losing one of the strings With a sling and a stone David killed Goliath .
A kind of hanging bandage put round the neck, in which a wounded limb is sustained.
A rope by which a cask or bale is suspended and swung in or out of a ship .
A drink composed of equal parts of rum or spirit and water sweetened.
slinger: One who slings or uses the sling.
slinging: Throwing with a sling; hanging so as to swing; moving by a sling.
slink: To sneak; to creep away meanly; to steal away. He would pinch the children in the dark, and then slink into a corner.
To cast prematurely; to miscarry of; as the female of a beast.
Produced prematurely, as the young of a beast.
slip: To slide; to glide; to move along the surface of a thing without bounding, rolling or stepping.
To slide; not to tread firmly. Walk carefully, lest your foot should slip.
TO move or fly out of place; usually without; as, a bone may slip out of its place.
To sneak; to slink; to depart or withdraw secretly; with away. Thus one tradesman slips away to give his partner fairer play.
To err; to fall into error or fault. One slippeth in his speech, but not from his heart.
slip-knot: A bow-knot; a knot which will not beat a strain, or which os easily untied.
slipper: A kind of shoe consisting of a sole and vamp without quarters, which may be slipped on with ease and worn in undress; a slip- shoe.
Akind of apron for children, to be slipped over their other clothes to keep them clean.
A kind of iron slide or lock for the use of a heavy wagon.
slipperiness: The state or quality of being slippery; lubricity; smoothness; glibness; as the slipperiness of ice or snow; the slipperiness of the tongue.
slippery: Smooth; glib; having the quality opposite to adhesiveness; as, oily substances render things slippery.
Not affording firm footing or confidence; as a slippery promise. The slipp’ry tops of human state.
Not easily held; liable or apt to slip away. The slipp’ry god will try to loose his hold.
Not standing firm, as slippery standers.
Unstable; changeable; mutable; uncertain; as the slippery state of kings.
slipshod: Wearing shoes like slippers, without pulling up the quarters.
slipstring: One that has shaken off restraint; a prodigal; called also slopthrift, but I believe seldom or never used.
slit: pret. slit; pp. slot or slitted.
To cut lengthwise; to cut into long pieces or strips; as, to slit iron bard into nail rods.
To cut or make a long fissure; as, to slit the ear or tongue, or the nose.
slitting-mill: A mill where iron bars are slit into nail rods etc.
sliver: To cut or divide into long thin pieces, or into very small pieces; to cut or rend lengthwise; as, to sliver wood.
A long piece cut or rent off, or a piece cut or rent lengthwise. In Scotland, it is said to signify a slice; as a sliver of beef.
sloat: A narrow piece of timber which holds together larger pieces; as the sloats of a cart.
slobber: and its derivatives, are a different orthography of slabber, the original pronunciation of which was probably slobber.
slock: to quench, is a different orthography of slake, but not used.
sloe: A small wild plum, the fruit of the black thorn.
sloop: A vessel with one mast, the main-sail of which is attached to a gaff above, toa boom below, and to the mast on its foremost edge. It differs from a cutter by having a fixed steeving bowsprit, and a jib-stay. Sloops are of various sizes, from the size of a boat to that of more than 100 tons burthen.

A vessel with one mast, the main-sail of which is attached to a gaff above, to a boom below, and to the mast on its foremost edge. It differs from a cutter by having a fixed steeving bowsprit, and a jib-stay. Sloops are of various sizes, from the size of a boat to that of more than 100 tons burthen. Sloop of war, a vessel of war rigged either as a ship, brig, or schooner, and usually carrying from 10 to 18 guns.
slop: Water carelessly thrown about on a table or floor; a puddle; a soiled spot.
Trowsers; a loose lower garment; drawers; hence, ready made clothes.
slopseller: A shop where ready made clothes are sold.
slope: Inclined or inclining form a horizontal direction; forming an angle with the plane of the horizon; as slope hills.
An oblique direction; a line or direction inclining from a horizontal line; properly, a direction downwards.
An oblique direction in general; a direction forming an angle with a perpendicular or other right line.
A declivity; any ground whose surface forms an angle with the plane of the horizon; also, an acclivity, as every declivity must be also an acclivity.s .
To form with a slope; to form to declivity or obliquity; to direct obliquely; to incline; as, to slope the ground in a garde; to slope a piece of cloth in cutting a garment.
sloping: Taking an inclined direction. 2.a. Oblique; declivous; inclining or inclined from a horizontal of other right line.
sloppy: Wet, as the ground; muddy; plashy.
slot: To shut with violence; to slam, that is to drive.
sloth: Slowness; tardiness I abhor this dilatory sloth and tricks of Rome.
Disinclination to action or labor; sluggishness; laziness; idleness. They change their course to pleasure, ease and sloth. Sloth, like rust, consumes faster than labor wears.

An animal, so called from the remarkable slowness of his motions. There are two species of this animal; the ai or three toed sloth, and the unau or two toed sloth; both found in South America. It is said that its greatest speed seldom exceeds three yard an hour. it feeds on vegetables and ruminates.
slothful: Inactive; sluggish; lazy; indolent; idle. He that is slothful in his work, is brother to him that is a great waster. Proverbs 18:9.
slothfulness: The indulgence of sloth; inactivity; the habit of idleness; laziness. Slothfulness casteth into a deep sleep. Proverbs 19:15.
slouch: A hanging down; a depression of the head or of some other part of the body, an ungainly clownish gait.
To hang down; to have a down cast clownish look, gait or manner.
To depress; to cause to hang down; as, to slouch the hat.
slouching: Causing to hang down. 2 .a. Hanging down; walking heavily and awkwardly.
slough: A place of deep mud or mire; a hole full of mire.
The skin or cast skin of a serpent.
The part that separates from a foul sore. The dead part which separates from the living in mortification.
sluff. To separate from the sound flesh; to come off; as the matter over a sore; a term in surgery. To slough off, to separate from the living parts, as the dead part in mortification.
sloven: A man careless of his dress, or negligent of cleanliness; a man habitually negligent of neatness and order.
slovenliness: Negligence of dress; habitual want of cleanliness.
slovenly: Negligent of dress or neatness; as a slowenly man.
Loose; disorderly; not neat; as a slovenly dress.
slovenry: Negligence of order or neatness; dirtiness.
slow: Moving a small distance in a long time; not swift; not quick in motion; not rapid; as a slow stream; a slow motion.
Late; not happening in short time. These changes in the heavens though slow, produc’d like change on sea and land, sidereal blast.
Not ready; not prompt or quick; as slow of speech, and slow of tongue. Exodus 4:10.
Dull; in active; tardy. The Trojans are not slow to guard their shore from an expected foe.
Not hasty; not precipitate; acting with deliberation. The Lord is merciful, slow to anger. He that is slow the wrath is of great understanding. Proverbs 14:29.
slowback: A lubber; an idle fellow; a loiterer.
slowly: With moderate motion; not rapidly; not with velocity or celerity; as, to walk slowly.
Not soon; not early; not in a little time; not with hasty advance; as a country that rises slowly into importance.
Not hastily; not rashly; not with precipitation; as, he determines slowly.
Not promptly; not readily; as, he learns slowly.
Tardily; with slow progress. The building proceeds slowly.
slowness: softening of a disjunct interval by the introduction of intermediate sounds. TRANSITION. Transition rocks, in geology, rocks supposed to have been formed when the world was passing from an uninhabitable to a habitable state. These rocks contain few organic remains, and when they occur with others, lie immediately over those which contain none, and which are considered as primitive. TRANSITION.
Moderate motion; want of speed or velocity. Swifness and slowness are relative ideas.
Tardy advance; moderate progression; as the slowness of an operation; slowness of growth or improvement.
Dullness to admit conviction or affection; as slowness of heart.
Want of readiness or promptness; dullness of intellect.
slow-worm: A kind of viper, the blind worm, scarcely venomous.
An insect found on the leaves of the sloe-tree, which often changes its skin and assumes different colors. It changes into a four winged fly.
sloe-worm: An insect found on the leaves of the sloe-tree, which often changes its skin and assumes different colors. It changes into a four winged fly.
slubber: To do lazily, imperfectly or coarsely; to daub; to stain; to cover carelessly.
slue: In seamen’s language, to turn any thing conical or cylindricalabout its axis without removing it; to turn.
slug: A drone; a slow, heavy, lazy fellow.
A kind of snail, very destructive to plants, of the genus Limax. It is without a shell.
A cylindrical or oval piece of metal, used for the charge of a gun.
To move slowly; to lie idle.
slugabed: One who indulges in lying abed.
sluggard: A person habitually lazy, idle and inactive; a drone.
sluggish: Habitually idle and lazy; slothful; dull; inactive; as a sluggish man.
Slow; having little motion; as a sluggish river or stream.
Inert; inactive; having no power to move itself. Matter is sluggish and inactive.
sluggishness: Natural or habitual indolence or laziness; sloth; dullness; applied to persons.
Inertness; want of power to move; applied to inanimate matter.
Slowness; as the’ sluggishness of a steam.
sluice: The stream of water issuing through a flood-gate; or the gate itself. If the word had its origin in shutting; it denoted the frame of boards or planks which closes the opening of a mill dam; but I believe it is applied to the stream, the gate and channel. It is a common saying, that a rapid stream runs like a sluse.
An opening; a source of supply; that through which any thing flows. Each sluice of affluent fortune open’d soon.
sluse: The stream of water issuing through a flood-gate; or the gate itself. If the word had its origin in shutting; it denoted the frame of boards or planks which closes the opening of a mill dam; but I believe it is applied to the stream, the gate and channel. It is a common saying, that a rapid stream runs like a sluse.
An opening; a source of supply; that through which any thing flows. Each sluice of affluent fortune open’d soon.
a more correct orthography of sluice.
sluicy: Falling in streams as from a sluice. And oft whole sheets descent of sluicy rain.
slusy: Falling in streams as from a sluice. And oft whole sheets descent of sluicy rain.
slumber: To sleep lightly; to doze. He that keepth Israel shall neither slumber nor sleep. Psalm 121:4.
To sleep. Slumber is used as synonymous with sleep, particularly in the poetic and eloquent style.
To be in a state of negligence, sloth, supineness or inactivity. Why slumbers Pope?
slumberous: Inviting or causing sleep; soporiferous. While pensive in the slumberous shade .
slumbery: Inviting or causing sleep; soporiferous. While pensive in the slumberous shade .
slump: To fall or sink suddenly into water or mud, when walking on a hard surface, as on ice or frozed ground, not strong enough to bear the person.
slur: To soil; to sully; to contaminate; to disgrace.
To pass lightly; to conceal. With periods, points and tropes he slurs his crimes.
In music, to sing or perform in a smooth gliding style .
Properly, a black mark; hence, slight reproach or disgrace. Every violation of moral duty should be a slur to the reputation.
slush: Soft mud, or a soft mixture of filthy substances.
slut: A woman who is negligent of cleanliness, and who suffers her person, clothes, funiture, to be dirty or in disorder.
Aname of slight contempt fora woman.
sluttery: The qualities of a slut; more generally, the practice of a slut; neglect of cleanliness and order; dirtiness of clothes, rooms, furniture or provisions.
sluttish: Not neat or cleanly; dirty; careless of dress and neatness; disorderly; as a sluttish woman.
Disorderly; dirty; as a sluttish dress.
sluttishly: In a sluttish manner; negligently; dirtily,
sluttishness: The qualities or practice of a slut; negligence of dress; dirtiness of dress, furniture and in domestic affairs generally.
sly: Artfully dextrous in performing things secretly, and escaping observation or detection; usually implying some degree of meanness; artfully cunning; applied to persons; as a sly man or boy.
Done with artful and dextrous secrecy; as a sly trick.
Marked with artful secrecy; as sly circumspection.
Secret; concealed. Envy works in a sly imperceptible manner.
sly-boots: A sly, cunning or waggish person.
smack: To kiss with a close compression of the lips, so as to make a sound when they separate; to kiss with violence.
To make a noise by the separation of the lips after tasting any thing.
To have a taste; to be tinctured with any particular taste.
To have a tincture or quality infused. All sects, all ages smack of this vice.
To kiss with a sharp noise.
small: Slender; thin; fine; of little diameter; hence in general, little in size or quantity; not great; as a small house; a small horse; a small farm; a small body; small particles.
Minute; slender; fine; as a small voice.
Little in degree; as small improvement; small acquirements; the trouble is small. There arose no small stir about that way. Acis 19:23.
Being of little moment, weight or importance; as, it is a small matter or thing; a small subject.
Of little genius or ability; petty; as a small poet or musician.
smallage: A plant of the genus Apium, water parsley.
small-coal: Little wood coals unsed to light fires.
small-craft: A vessel, or vessels in general, of a small size, or below the size of ships and bigs intended for foreign trade.
smallness: Littleness of size or extent; littleness of quantity; as the smallness of a fly or of a horse; the smallness of a hill.
Littleness in degree; as the smallness of trouble or pain.
Littleness in force or strength; weakness; as smallness of mind or intellectual powers.
Fineness; softness; melodiousness; as the smallness of a female voice.
Littleness in amount of value; as the smallness of a sum.
small-pox: A very contagious disease, characterized by an eruprion of pustules on the skin; the variolous disease.
smally: small’-ly. In a little quantity or degree; with minuteness.
smalt: A beautiful blue glass of cobalt; flint and potash fused together.
smaragdine: Pertaining to emerald; consisting of emerald, or resembling it; of an emerald green.
smaragdite: A mineral; called also green diallage.
smaris: A fish of a dark green color.
smart: Quick, pungent, lively pain; a pricking local pain, as the pain from puncture by nettles; as the smart of bodily punishment.
Severe pungent pain of mind; pungent grief; as the smart of affliction.
To feel a lively pungent pain, particularly a pungent local pain from some piercing or irritating application. Thus Cayeene pepper applied to the tongue makes it smart.
To feel a pungent pain of mind; to feel sharp pain; as, to smart under sufferings.
To be punished; to bear penalties or the evil consequences of any thing. He that is surety for a stranger shall smart for it. Proverbs 11:15.
smartly: With keen pain; as, to ake smartly.
smartness: The quality of being smart or pungent; poignancy; as the smartness of pain.
Quickness; vigor; as the smartness of a blow.
Liveliness; briskness; vivacity; wittiness; as the smartness of a reply or of a phrase.
smart-weed: A name given to the arsmart or persicaria.
smash: To break in pieces by violence; to dash to pieces; to crush. Here every thing is broken and smashed to pieces.
smatter: To talk superficially or ignorantly. Of state affairs you cannot smatter.
To have a slight taste, or a slight superficial knowledge.
smatterer: One who has only a slight superficial knowledge.
smear: To overspread with any thing unctuous, viscous or adhesive; to besmear; to daub; as, to smear any thing with oil, butter, pitch.
To soil; to contaminate; to pollute; as smeared with infamy.
smeared: Overspread with soft or oily matter; soiled.
smearing: Overspreading with any thing soft and oleaginous; soiling.
smectite: An argillaceous earth; so called from its property of taking grease out of cloth
smegmatic: Being of the nature of soap; soapy; cleansing; detersive.
smell: pret. and pp. smelledTO perceive by the nose, or by the olfactory nerves; to have a sensation excited in certain organs of the nose by particular qualities of a body, which are transmitted in fine particles, often form a distance; as, to smell a rose; to smell perfumes. To smell out, is a low phrase signifying to find out by sagacity. To smell a rat, is a low phrase signifying to suspect strongly.
To affect the olfactory nerves; to have an odor or particualr scent; followed by of; as to smell of smoke; to smell of musk.
To have a particular tincuture or smack or any quality; as, a report smells of calumny.
smelt: A small fish that is very delicate food. But in Europe, a fish of the truttaceous kind, so names from its peculiar smell.
To melt, an ore, for the purpose of separating the metal from extraneous substances.
smellfeast: One that is apt to find and frequent good tables; an epicure; a parasite.
smelted: Melted for the extraction of the metal.
smeltery: A house or place for smelting ores.
smelting: The operation of melting ores for the purpose of extracting the metal.
smerk: Nice; smart; janty. So smerk, so smooth he prick’d his ears.
smew: An aquatic fowl, the Mergus albellus.
smicker: To smerk; to look amorously or wantonly.
smickering: An affected smile or amorous look.
smight: for smile, in Spenser, is a mistake.
smile: To contract the features of the face in such a manner as to express pleasure, moderate joy, or love and kindness; the contrary to frown. The smiling infant in his hand shall take the crested basilisk and speckled snake. She smil’d to see the doughty hero slain,
To express slight contempt by a smiling liik, implying sarcasm or pity; to sneer. ‘Twas what I said to Craggs and Child, who prais’d my modesty, and smil’d.
To look gay and joyous; or to have an appearance to excite joy; as smiling spring; smiling plenty. The desert smil’d, and paradise was open’d in the wild.
To be propitious or favorable; to favor; to countenance. May heaven smile on out labors.
smiling: Having a smile on the countenance; looking joyous or gay; looking propitious.
smirch: smerch. To cloud; to dusk; to soil; as, to smirch the face.
smirk: smerch. To look affectedly soft or kind.
smite: To strike; to throw, drive or force against, as the fist or hand, a stone or a weapon; to reach with a blow or a weapon; as, to smite one with the fist; to smite with a rod or with a stone. Whoever shall smite thee on the right cheek, turn to him the other also. Matihew 5:39.

To kill; to destroy the life of by beating or by weapons of any kind; as, to smite one with the sword, or with an arrow or other engine. David smote Goliath with a sling and a stone. The Philistines were often smitten with great slaughter.
To blast; to destroy life; as by a stroke or by something sent. The flax and the barley were smitten. Exodus 9:31.
To afflict; to chasten; to punish. Let us not mistake God’s goodness, nor imagine, because he smites us, that we are forsaken by him.
smiter: One who smites or strikes. I gave my back to the smiters. Isaiah 50:6.
smith: Literally, the striker, the beater; hence, one who forges with the hammer; one who works in metals; as an iron-smith; gold-smith; silver-smithNor yet the smith hath learn’d to form a sword.
He that makes or effects any thing. Hence the name Smith, which, from the number of workmen employed in working metals in early ages, is supposed to be more common than any other.
To beat into shape; to forge.
smithcraft: The art of occupation of a smith.
smithing: The act or art of working a mass of iron into the intended shape.
smitt: The finest of the clayey ore made up into balls, used for marking sheep.
smitten: Affected with some passion; excited by beauty or someting impressive.
smock: A shift; a chemise; a woman’s under garment.
In composition, it is used for female, or what relates to women; as smock-treason.
smock-faced: Pace faced; maidenly; having a feminine countenance or complexion.
smoke: The exhalation, visble vapor or substance that escapes or is expelled in combustion from the substance burning. It is paricularly applied to the volatile matter expelled from vegetable matter, or wood coal, peatThe matter expelled from metallic substances is more generally called fume.
To emit smoke; to throw off volatile matter in the form of vapor or exhalation. Wood and other fuel smokes when burning; amd smokes most when there is the least flame.
To burn; to be kindled; to rage; in Scripture. The anger of the Lord and his jealousy shall smoke against that man. Deuteronomy 29:20.
To raise a dust or smoke by rapid motion. Proud of his steeds, be smokes along the field.
smoked: Cured, cleansed or dried in smoke.
smoke-jack: An engine for turning a spit by means of a fly or wheel turned by the current of ascending air in a chimney.
smokeless: Having no smoke; as smokeless towers.
smoker: One that used tobacco by burning it in a pipe or in the form of a cigar.
smoking: Using tobacco in a pipe or cigar.
The act of applying smoke to.
smoky: Emitting smoke; fumid; as smoky fires.
Having the appearance or nature of smoke; as a smoky fog.
Filled with smoke, or with a vapor resembling it; thick. New England in autumn frequently has a smoky atmosphere.
Subject to be filled with smoke from the chimneys or fire-places; as a smoky house.
Tarnished with smoke; noisome with smoke; as smoky rafters; smoky cells.
smoldering: the more correct orthography of smouldering, which see.
smooth: Having an even surface, or a surface so even that no roughness or points are perceptible to the touch; not rough; as smooth glass; smooth porcelain. The out lines must be smooth, imperceptible to the touch.
To free from obstruction; to make easy. Thou, Abelard, the last sad office pay, And smooth my passage to the realms of day.
To free from harshness; to make flowing. In their motions harmony divine so smooths her charming tones.
To palliate; to soften; as, to smooth a fault.
To calm; to mollify; to allay. Each perturbation smooth’d with outward calm.
smoothen: for smooth, is used by mechanics; though not, I believe, in the U. States.
smooth-faced: Having a mild, soft look; as smooth-faced wooers.
smoothly: With even flow or motion; as, to flow or glide smoothly.
Without obstruction or difficulty; readily; easily.
smoothness: Evenness of suface; freedom from roughness or asperity; as the smoothness of a floor or wall; smoothness of the skin; smoothness of the water.
Softness or mildness to the palate; as the smoothness of wine.
Softness and sweetness of numbers; easy flow of words. Virgil, though smooth where smoothness is required, is far from affecting it.
Mildness or gentleness of speech; blandness of address.
smother: To suffocate of extinguish life by causing smoke or dust to enter the lungs; to stifle.
To suffocate or extinguish by closely covering, and be the exclustion of air; as, to smother a child in bed.
To suppress; to stifle; as, to smother the light of the understanding.
smug: Nice; neat; affectedly nice in dress.
To make spruce; to dress with affected neatness.
smuggle: To import or export secretly goods which are forbidden by the goverment to be imported or exported; or secretly to import or export dutiable goods without paying the duties imposed by law; to run.
smuggled: Imported or exported clandestinely and contrary to law.
smuggler: One that imports or exports goods privately and contrary to law, either contraband goods or dutiable goods, without paying the customs.
A vessel employed in running goods. SWVUGGLER.
smuggling: Importing or exporting goods contrary to law.
The offense of importing or exporting prohibited goods. or other goods without paying the customs.
smut: A spot made with soot or coal; or the foul matter itself.
A foul black substance which forms on corn. Sometimes the whole ear is blasted and converted into smut. This is often the fact with maiz. Smut lessens the value of wheat.
To gather smut; tobe converted into smut.
smutch: To blacken with smoke, soot or coal.
smutty: Soiled from smut, coal, soot or the like.
Tainted with mildew; as smutty corn.
Obscene; not modest or pure; as smutty language.
snack: A share. It is now chiefly or wholly used in the phrase, to go snacks with one, that is, to have a share.
snaffle: A bridle consisting of a slender bit-mouth, without branches.
To bridle; to hold or manage with a bridle.
snag: A short branch, or a sharp or rough branch; a shoot; a knot. The coat of arms now on a naked snag in triumph borne.
A tooth, in contempt; or a tooth projecting beyond the rest.
snagged: Full of snags; full of short rough branches or sharp points; abounding with knots; as a snaggy tree; a snaggy stick; a snaggy oak.
snaggy: Full of snags; full of short rough branches or sharp points; abounding with knots; as a snaggy tree; a snaggy stick; a snaggy oak.
snail: A slimy slow creeping animal, of the genus Helix, and order of Mollusca. The eyes of this insect are in the horns, one at the end of each, which it can retract at pleasure.
A drone; a slow moving person.
snail-claver: A plant of the genus Medicago.
snail-trefoil: A plant of the genus Medicago.
snail-flower: A plant of the genus Phaseolus.
snail-like: Resembling a snail; moving very slowly.
In the manner of a snail; slowly.
snake: A serpent of the oviparous kind, distinguished from a viper, says Johnson. But in America, the common and general name of serpents, and so the word is used by the poets.
In seamen’s language, to wind a small rope round a large one spirally, the small ropes lying in the spaces between the strands of the large one. This is called also worming.
snakeroot: A plant, a species of birth-wort, growing in North Am merica the Aristolochia serpentaria.
snakeshead iris: A plant with a lily shaped flower, or one leaf, shaped like an iris; the hermodactyl, or Iris tuberosa.
snakeweed: A plant, bistort, of the genus Polygonum.
snakewood: The smaller branches of a tree, growing in the isle of Timor and other parts of the east, having a bitter taste, and supposed to be a certain remedy for the bite of the hooded serpent. It is the wood of the SlIrychnos colubrina.
snaking: Winding small ropes spirally round a large one.
snaky: Pertaining to a snake or to snakes; resembling a snake; serpentine; winding.
Sly; cunning; insinuating; deceitful. So to the coast of Jordan he directs his easy steps, girded with snaky wiles.
Having serpents; as a snaky rod or want. That sanky headed gorgon shield.
snap: To break at once; to break short; as substances that are brittle. Breaks the doors open, smaps the locks.
To strike with a sharp sound.
To bite or seize suddenly with the teeth.
To break upon suddenly with sharp angry words.
To crack; as, to snap a whip. To snap off.
snap-dragon: A plant, calfs snout, of the genus Antirrhinum, and another of the genus Ruellia, and one of the genus Barleria .
A play in which raisins are snatched from burning brandy and put into the mouth.
snapped: Broken abruptly; seized or bitten suddenly; cracked, as a whip.
snappish: Eager to bite; apt to snap; as a snappish cur.
Peevish; sharp in reply; apt to speak angrily or tartly.
snappishness: The quality of being snappish; peeevishness; tartness.
snare: An instrument for catching animals, particularly fowls, by the leg. It consists of a cord or string with slip-knots, in which the leg is entangled. A snare is not a net.
Any thing by which one is entangled and brought into troble. Corinthians 7:35. A fool’s lip are the snare of his soul. Proverbs 18:7.
To catch with a snare; to ensnare; to entangle; to bring into unexpected evil, perplexity or danger. The wicked is snared in the work of his own hands. Psalm 9:16.
snarer: One who lays snares or entangles.
snarl: To growl, as an angry or surly dog; to gnarl; to utter grumbling sounds; but it expresses more violence than grumble. That I should snarl and bit and play the dog.
To speak roughly; to talk in rude murmuring terms. It is malicious and unmanly to snarl at the little lapses of a pen, from with Virgil himself stands not exempted.
To entangle; to complicate; to involve in knots; as, to snarl the hair; to snarl a skain of thread.
Entanglement; a knot or complication of hair, thread, which it is difficult to disentangle.
snarler: One who snarls; a surly growling animal; a grumbling quarrelsime fellow.
snary: Entangling; insidious. Spiders in the vault their snary webs have spread.
snatch: t@ pret. and pp. snatched or snacht.
To seize hastily or abruptly. When half our knowledge we must snatch, not take.
To seize without permission or ceremony; as, to snatch a kiss.
To seize and transport away; as, snatch me to heaven.
To catch at; to attempt to seize suddenly. Nay, the ladies too will be snatching. He shall snatch on the right hand, and be hungry. Isaiah 9:20.
snatch-block: A particular kind of block used in ships, having an opening in one side to receive the bight of a rope.
snatcher: One that snatches or takes abruptly.
snatching: Seized hastily or abruptly; catching at.
sneak: To creep or steal away privately; to withdraw meanly, as a person afraid or ashamed to be seen; as, to sneak away from company; to sneak into a corner or behind a screen. You skulk’d behind the fence, and sneak’d away.
To speak roughly; to talk in rude murmuring terms. It is malicious and unmanly to snarly at the little lapses of a pen, from which Virgil himself stands not exempted.
sneap: To check; to reprove abruptly; to reprimand.
sneer: To show contempt by turning up the nose, or by a particular cast or countenance; “naso suspendere adunco.” .
To insinuate contempt by covert expression. I could be content to be a little sneered at.
A look of contempt, or a turning up of the nose to manifest contempt; a look of disdain, derision or ridicule.
sneering: Manifesting contempt or scorn by turning up the nose, or by some grimace or significant look.
sneeringly: With a look of contempt or scorn.
sneeze: To emit air through the nose audibly and violently, by a kind of involuntary convulsive force, occasioned by irriatation of the inner membrance of the nose. Thus snuff or any thing that tickles the nose, makes one sneeze.
A sudden and violent ejection of air through the nose with an audible sound.
sneeze-wort: A plant, a species of Achillea, and another of Xeranthemum.
sneezing: Emitting air from the nose audibly.
The act of ejecting air violently and audibly through the nose; sternutation.
snib: to nip or reprimand, is only a different spelling of sneb
snick: A small cut or mark; a latch. Snick and snee, a combat with knives.
snicker: To laugh slily; or to laugh in one’s sleeve.
snigger: To laugh slily; or to laugh in one’s sleeve.
sniff: To draw air audibly up the nose.
é. To draw in with the breath.
sniggle: To fish for eels, by thrusting the bait into their holes.
snip: To clip; to cut off the nip or neb, or to cut off at once with shears or scissors.
Aclip; a single cut with shears or scissors.
snipe: A bird tha frequents the banks of rivers and the borders of fens, distinguished by the length of its bill; the scolopax gallinago.
snipsnap: a cant word, formed by repeating snap, and signifying a tart dialogue with quick replies.
snite: To blow the nose. In Scotland, snite the candle, snuff it.
snivel: sniv’l. Snot; mucus running from the nose.
To cry as children, with snuffing or sniveling. SNIIVEL.
sniveler: One that weeps for slight causes, or manifests weakness by weeping.
snively: Running at the nose; pitiful; whining.
snook: To lurk; to lie in ambush.
snore: To breathe with a rough hoarse noise in sleep.
A breathing with a harse noise in sleep.
snort: To force the air with violence through the nose, so as to make a noise, as high spirited horses in prancing and play.
To turn up in anger, scorn or derision, as the nose.
snorting: Forcing the air violently through the nose.
The act of forcing the air through the nose with violence and noise. Jeremiah 8:16.
snout: The long projecting nose of a beast, as that of swine.
The nose of a man; in contempt.
The nozzle or end of a hollow pipe.
To furnish with a nozzle or point.
snow: Frozen vapor; watery particles congealed into white crystals in the air, and falling to the earth. When there is no wind, these crystals fall in flakes or unbroken collections, sometimes extremely beautiful.
A vessel equipped with two masts, resembling the main and fore- masts of a ship, and a third small mast just abaft the mainmast, carrying a try-sail.
To fall in snow; as, it snows; it snowed yesterday.
snowball: A round mass of snow, pressed or rolled together.
snowball tree: A flowering shrub of the genus Virburnum; gelder rose.
snow-bird: A small bird whcih appears in the time of snow, of the genus Emberiza; called also snow-bunting. In the U. States, the snow-bird is the Fringilla nivalis.
snowbroth: Snow and water mixed; very cold liquor.
snow-crowned: 2. Crowned or having the top covered with snow.
snow-drift: A bank of snow driven together by the wind.
snow-drop: A plant bearing a white flower, cultivated in gardens for its beauty; the Galanthus nivalis.
snow-shoe: A shoe or racket worn by men traveling on snow, to prevent their feet from sinking into the snow.
snow-slip: A large mass of snow which slips down the side of a mountain, and sometimes buries houses.
