snowy: Abounding with snow; covered with snow. The snowy top of cold Olympus.
snub: A knot or protuberance in wood; a snag.
To nip; to clip or break off the end. Hence,
To check; to reprimand; to check, stop or rebuke with a tart sarcastic reply or remark.
snudge: A miser, or a sneaking fellow.
snuff: The burning part of a candle wick, or that which has been charred by the flame, whether burning or not.
Pulverized tobacco, taken or prepared to be taken into the nose.
Resentment; huff, expressed by a snuffing of the nose.
To draw in with the breath; to inhale; as, to snuff the wind.
snuffers: An instrument for cropping the snuff of a candle.
snuffle: To speak through the nose; to breathe hard through the nose, or through the nose when obstructed. Some senseless Phillis, in a broken not, SnuffLing at nose.
snuffler: One that snuffles or speaks through the nose when obstructed.
snuffles: Obstruction of the nose by mucus.
snufftaking: One that takes snuff, or inhales in into the nose.
snug: To lie close; as, a child snugs to its mother or nurse.
Lying close; closely pressed; as, an infant lies snug.
Close; concealed; not exposed to notice. At Will’s lie snug and hear what critics say.
Being in good order; all convenient; neat; as a snug little farm.
Close; neat; convenient; as a snug house.
snuggle: To move one way and the other to get a close place; to lie close for convenience or warmth.
snugness: Closeness; the state of being neat or convenient.
so: In like manner, answering to as, and noting comparison or resemblance; as with the people, so with the priest.
In such a degree; to that degree. Why is his chariot so long in coming? Judges 5:28.
In such a manner; sometimes repeated, so and so; as certain colors, mingled so and so.

It is followed by as. There is something equivalent in France and Scotland; so as it is a hard calumny upon our soil to affirm that so excellent a fruit will not grow here. But in like phrases, we now use that; “so that it is a hard calumny;” and this may be considered as the extablished usage.
In the smae manner. Use your tutor with great respect, and cause all your family to do so too.
soak: To steep; to cause or suffer to lie in a fluid till the substance has imbibed what it can contain; to macerate in water or other fluid; as, to soak cloth; to soak bread.
To drench; to wet thoroughly. The earth is soaked with heavy rain. Their land shall be soaked with blood. Isaiah 34:7.
To draw in by the pores; as the skin.
To lie steeped in water or other fluid. Let the cloth lie and soak.
soaked: Steeped or macerated in a fluid; drenched.
soaker: One that soaks or macerates in a liquid.
soaking: a. That wets thoroughly; as a soaking rain.
soap: A compound of oil and alkali, or oil and earth, and metallic oxyds; usually, a compound of oil and vegetable alkali or lye; used in washing and cleansing, in medicine
To rub or wash over with soap.
soapherry tree: A tree of the genus Sapindus.
soap-boiler: One whose occupation is to make soap.
soapstone: Steatite; a minera; or species of magnesian earth, usually white or yellow;; the lapis ollaris.
soap-suds: Suds; water well impregnated with soap.
soapwort: A plant of the genus Saponaria.
soapy: Resembling soap; having the qualities of soap; soft and smooth.
soar: To fly aloft; to mount upon the wing; as an eagle. Hence,
To rise high; to mount; to tower in thought or imagination; to be sublime; as the poet or orator.
To rise high in ambition or heroism. Valor soars above what the world calls misfortune.
In general, to rise aloft; to be lofty: .
soaring: Mounting on the wing; rising aloft; towering in thought or mind.
The act of mounting on the wing, or of towering in thought or mind; intellectual flight.
sob: To sigh with a sudden heaving of the breast, or a kind of convulsive motion; to sigh with deep sorrow or with tears. She sigh’d, she sobb’d, and furious wtih despair, she rent her garments, and she tore her hair.
A convulsive sign or catching of the breath in sorrow; a convulsive act of respiration obstructed by sorrow. Break, heart, or choke with sobs my hated breath.
sobbing: sighing with a heaving of the breast.
sober: Temperate in the use of spiritous liquors; habitually temperate; as a sober man. Live a sober, righteous and godly life.
Not intoxicated or overpowered by spiritous liquors; not drunken. The sot may at times be sober.

Not mad or insane; not wild, visionary or heated with passion; having the regular exercise of cool dispassionate reason. There was not a sober person to be had; all was tempestuous and blustering. Not sober man would put himself in danger, for the applause of escaping without breaking his neck.
Regular; calm; not under the influence of passion; as sober judgment; a man in his sober senses.
Serious; solemn; grave; as the sober livery of autumn. What parts gay France from sober Spain? See her sober over a sampler, or gay over a jointed baby.
soberly: Without intemperate passion; coolly; calmly; moderately.
soberminded: Having a disposition or tempter habitually sober, calm and temperate.
soberminedness: n, Calmness; freedon from inordinate passions; habitual sobriety.
soberness: Freedom from heat and passion; calmness; coolness. The soberness of Virgil might have shown him the difference.
sobriety: Habitual soberness or temperance in the use of spirituous liquors; as when we say. a man of sobriety.
Freedom from intoxication. Public sobriety is a relative duty.
Habitual freedom from enthusiasm, inordinate passion or overheated imagination; calmness; coolness; as the sobriety of riper years; the sobriety of age.
Seriousness; gravity without sadness or melancholy. Mirth makes them not mad, nor sobriety sad.
soc: Properly, the sequela, secta or suit, or the body of suitors; hence, the power or privilege of holding a court in a district, as in a manor; jurisdiction of causes, and the limits of that jurisdiction.
Liberty or privilege of tenants excused from customary burdens.
An exclusive privilege claimed by millers of grinding all the corn used within the manor or township in which the mill stands.
socage: In English law, a tenure of lands and tenements by a cetain or determinate service; a tenure distinct from chivalry or knight’s service, in which the render was uncertain. The service must be certain, in order to be denominated socage; as to hold by fealty and twenty shillings rent. Socage is of two kinds; free socage, where the services are not only certain, but honorable, and villein socage, where the services, though certain, are of a baser nature.
socager: A tenant by socage; a socman.
sociability: Sociableness; disposition to associate and converse with others; or the practive of familiar converse.
sociable: That may be conjoined; fit to be united in one body or company; as sociable parts united in one body.
Ready or disposed to unite in a general interest. To make man mild, and sociable to man.
Ready and inclined to join in company or society; or frequently meeting for conversation; as sociable neighbors.
Inclined to converse when in company; disposed to freedom in conversation; opposed to reserved’ and taciturn.
Free in conversation; conversing much or familiarly. The guests were very sociable.
sociableness: Disposition to associate; inclination to company and converse; or actual frequent union in society or free converse. This word may signify either the disposition to associate, or the disposition to enter into familiar conversation, or the actual practice of associating and conversing.
sociably: In a sociable manner; with free intercourse; conversibly; familiarly; as a companion.
social: Pertaining to society; relating to men living in society. or to the publice as an aggregate body; as social interests or concerns; social pleasures; social benefits; social happiness; social duties. True self-love and social are the same.
Ready or disposed to mix in friendly converse; companionable. Withers, adieu? yet not with thee remove thy martial spirit or thy social love.
Consisting in union or mutual converse.
Disposed to unite in society. Man is a social being.
sociality: Socialness; the quality of being social.
socially: In a social manner or way.
society: The union of a number of rational beings; or a number of persons united, either for a temporary or permanent purpose. Thus the inhabitants of a state or of a city constitute a society, having common interests; and hence it is called a community. In a more enlarged sense, the whole race or family of man is a society, and called human society. The true and natural foundation of society, are the wants and fears of individuals.

Any number of persons associated for a particular purpose, whether incorporated by law, or only united by articles of agreement; a fraternity. Thus we have bible societies for various objects; societies for mechanics, and leaned societies; societies for encouraging arts.
Company; a temporary association of persons for profit or pleasure. In this sense, company is more generally used.W .
Conpany; fellowship. We frequent the society of those we love and esteem.
Partnership; fellowship; union on equal terms. Among unequals what society can sort? Heav’n’s greatness no society can bear.
socinian: Pertaning to Socinus, or his religious creed.
One of the followers of Socinian.
socinianism: The tenets or doctrines of Socinus, who held Christ to be a mere man inspired, denied his divinity and atonement, and the doctrine of original depravity.
sock: The shoe of the ancient actors of comedy. Hence the word is used for comedy, and opposed to buskin or tragedy. Great Fletcher never teads in buskin here, nor greater Jonson dares in socks appear.
A garment for the foot, like the foot of a stocking.
socket: The little hollow tube or place in which a candle is fixed in the candlestick. And in the sockets oily bubbles dance.

Any hollow thing or place which receives and holds something else; as the sockets of the teeth or of the eyes. his eyeballs in their hollow sockets sink. Gomphosis is the connection of a tooth to its socket.
socket-chisel: A chisel made with a socket; a stronger sort of chisel.
socle: In architecture, a flat squre member under the basis of pedestals of vases and statues, serving as a foot or stand.
socman: One who holds lands or tenements by socage.
socome: A custom of tenants to grind corn at the lord’s mill.
socotorine: Socotorine or socotrine aloes, a fine kind of aloes from Socotra, an isle in the Indian oceans.
socotrine: Socotorine or socotrine aloes, a fine kind of aloes from Socotra, an isle in the Indian oceans.
socratic: Pertaining to Socrates, the Grecian sage, or his language or manner of teaching and philosophizing. The Socratic metod of resoning and instruction was by inter rugatories.
socratical: Pertaining to Socrates, the Grecian sage, or his language or manner of teaching and philosophizing. The Socratic metod of resoning and instruction was by inter rugatories.
socratism: The doctrines of philosophy of Socrates.
sod: Turf; sward; that stratum of earth on the surface which is filled with the roots of grass, or any portion of that surface. It differs from clod, which may be compact mass of earth without roots; but sod is formed by earth held together by roots.
To cover with sod; to turf.
of seethe; also the passive participle.
soda: Mineral fixed alkali; natron; so called because it forms the basis of marine salt. It is found native in Egypt; but it is generally obtained form the salsola kali. Soda is an oxyd, or the protoxyd of sodiem, a metal.
sodalite: A mineral; so called from the large portion of mineral alkali which enters into its composition. It is of a bluish green color, and found crystalized or in masses.
soda-water: A very weak solution of soda in water supersaturated with carbonic acid, and constituting a favorite beverage.
soddy: Turfy; consisting of sod; covered with sod.
soder: To unite and make solid, as metallic substances; to join separate things or parts of the same thing by a metallic substance in a state of fustion, which hardens in cooling, and renders the joint solid.
Metallic cement; a metal or metallic composition used in uniting other metallic substances.
sodium: The metallic base of soda. It is soft, sectile, white and opake, and very malleable. It is lighter than water.
soe: A large wooden vessel for holding water; a cowl.
soever: so and ever, found in compounds, as in whosoever, whatsoever, wheresoever, See these words. It is sometimes used separate from the pronoun; as, in what things soever you undertake, use diligence and fidelity.
sofa: An elegant long seat, usually with a stuffed bottom. Sofas are variously made. In the United States, the frame of stuffed cloth, with a covering of silkcalico or hair-cloth. The sofa of the orientals is a kind of alcove raised half a foot above the floor, where visitors of distinction are received. It is also a seat by the side of the room covered with a carpet.
soffit: In architecture, any timber ceiling formed of cross beams, the compartments of which are enriched with sculpture, painting or gilding.
The under side of face of an architrave, enriched with compartment of roses.
soft: Easily yielding to pressure; the contrary of hard; as a soft bed; a soft peach; soft earth.

Not hard; easily separated by an edged instrument; as soft wood. The chestnut is a soft wood, but more durable than hickory, with is a very hard wood. So we Say, a soft stone, when it breaks or is hewed with ease.
Easily worked; malleable; as soft iron.
Not rough, rugged or harsh; smooth to the touch; delicate; as soft silk; soft raiment a soft skin.
Delicate; feminine; as the softer sex.
soften: sofn. To become less hard; to become more pliable and yielding to pressure; as, irion or wax softens in heat; fruits soften as they ripen.
To make soft or more soft; to make less hard. their arrow’s point they soften in the flame.

To mollify; to make less fierce or intractable; to make more susceptible of humane or fine feelings; as, to soften a hard heart; to soften savage natures. The heart is softened by pity. Diffidence concilliates the proud, and softens the severe.
To make less harsh or severe; as, to soften an expression.
softened: Made less hard or less harsh; made less obdurate or cruel, or less glaring.
softening: Making more soft; making less rough or cruel
The act of making less hard, less cruel or obdurate, less violent, less glaring. etc.
softhearted: Having tenderness of heart; susceptible of pity or other kindly affection; gentle; meek.
softling: An effeminate person; one vitiously nice.
softly: Not with force or violence; gently; as, be softly pressed my hand.
Not loudly; without noise; as, speak softly; walk softly. In this dark silence softly leave the town.
Gently; placidly. She softly lays him on a flowery bed.
Mildly; tenderly. The king must die; though pity softly pleads within my soul.
softness: The quality of bodies which renders them capable of yielding to pressure, or of easily receiving impressions from other bodies; opposed to hardness.
Susceptibility of feeling or passion; as the softness of the heart or of our natures.
Mildness; kindness; as softness of words or expressions.
Mildness; civility; gentleness; as softness of manners.
Effeminacy; vicious delicacy. He was not delighted with the softness of the court.
soggy: Wet; filled with water; soft with moisture; as soggy land. Timber that has imbibed water is said to be soggy.
soho: A word used in calling from a distant place; a sportman’s halloo. I
soil: To make dirty on the surface; to foul; to dirt; to stain; to defile; to tarnish; to sull; as, to soil a garment with dust. Out wonted ornaments now soil’d and stain’d.
To cover or tinge with any thing extraneous; as, to soil the earth with blood.
To dung; to manure. To soil a horse, is to purge him by giving him fresh grass. To soil a cattle, in husbandry, is to feed them with grass daily mowed for them, instead of pasturing them.
Dirt; and foul matter upon another substance; foulness; apot.
Stain; tarnish. A lady’s honor will not bear a soil.
soiled: Fouled; stained; tarnished; manured; fed with grass.
soiling: Defiling; fouling; tarnishing; feeding with fresh grass; manuring.
The act of practice of feeding cattle or horses with fresh grass, instead of pasturing them.
sojourn: so’jurn. To dwell for a time; to dwell or live in a place as a temporary resident, or as a stranger, not considering the place as his permanent habitation. So Abram sojourned in Egypt. Genesis 12:10.
A temporary residence, as that of a traveler in a foreign land.
sojourner: A temporary resident; a stranger or traveler who dwells in a place for a time. We are strangers before thee and sojourners, as all out father were. 1 Chronicles 29:15.
sojourning: The act of dwelling in a place for a time; also, the time of abode. Exodus 12:40.
sojournment: Temporary residence, as that of a stanger or traveler.
sol: In France, a small copper coin; a penny; usually sou sor sous.
A copper coin and money of acount in Switzerland.
The name of a not in music.
solace: To cheer in grief or under calamity; to comfort; to relieve in afflication; to console; applied to persons; as, to solace one’s self with the hop of future reward.
To allay; to assuage; as, to solace grief.
To take comfort; to be cheered or relieved in grief.
Comfort in grief; alleviation of grief or anxiety; also, that which relieves in distress; recreation. The propersolaces of age are not music and compliments, but wisdom and devotion.
solan-goose: The gannet, (Pelecanus bassanus,) as aquatic fowl found on the coasts of Great Britain and Ireland. It is nearly of the size of the domestic goose.
solano: A hot S.E. wind in Spain which produces inflammatory effects on men.
solar: Pertaining to the sun, as the solar system; .
Belonging to the sun as solar herbs.
In astrology, born under the predominant influence of the sun; as a solar people.
Measured by the progress of the sun, or by its revolution; as the solar year.
solar flowers: are those which open and shut daily, at certain determinate hours.
solar spots: dark spots that appear on the sun’s disk, usually visible only by the telescope, but sometimes so large as to be seen by the naked eye. They adhere to the body of the sun; indicate its revolutions on its axis; are very changeable in their figure and dimensions; and vary in size from mere points to spaces 50,000 miles in diametet.
solder: To unite by a metallic cement.
soldier: A man engaged in military service; one whose occupation is military; a man enlisted for service in an army; a private, or noe in the ranks. There ought to be some time for sober reflection between the life of a soldier and his death.
Aman enrolled for service, when on duty or embodied for military discipline; a private; as a militia soldier.
Emphatically, a brave warrior; a man of military experience and skill, or a man of distinguished valor. In this sense, an officer of any grade may be denominated a soldier.
soldierlike: Like or becoming a real soldier; brave; martial; heroic; honorable.
soldierly: Like or becoming a real soldier; brave; martial; heroic; honorable.
soldiership: Military qulities; military character or state; martial skill; behavior becoming a soldier.
soldiery: Soldiers collectively; the body of military men. I charge not the soldiery with ignorance and contempt of learning, without exception.
sole: The bottom of the foot; and by a figure, the foot itselft.
The bottom of the shoe; or the piece of lether which constitutes the bottom. The cliga was a military show with a very thick sole, tied above the instep.
The part of any thing that forms the bottom, and on which it stands upon the ground. Elms is proper for mills, soles for wheels, and pipes.

A marine fish of the genus Pleurinectes, so called probably because it keeps on or near the bottom of the sea. These fish abound on the British coast, and hence the name of sole bank, to the southward of Ireland. This fish sometimes grows to the weight of six or seven pounds.
In ship-building, a sort of lining, used to prevent the wearing of any thing.
solecism: Impropriety in language, or a gross deviation from the rules of syntax; incongruity of words; want of correspondence or consistency. A barbarism may be in one word; a solecism must be of more.
Any unfitness, absurdity or impropriety. Cesar, by dismissing his guards and retaining his power, committed a dangerous solecism in politics.
solecist: One who is guilty of impropriety in language.
solely: Singly; alone; only; without another; as, to rest a cause solely on one argument; to rely solely on one’s own strength.
solemn: Anniversary; observed once a year with religious ceremonies. The worship of this image was advanced and a solemn supplication observed every year.
Religiously grave; marked with pomp and sanctity; attended with religious rites. His holy rites and solemn feasts profan’d.
Religiosly serious; piously grave; devout; marked by reverence to God; as solemn prayer; the solemn duties of the sanctuary.

Affectiong with seriousness; impressing or adapted to impress seriousness, gravity or reverence; sober; serious. There reign’d a solemn silence over all. To ‘swage with solemn touches troubled thoughts.
solemness: The state or quality of being solemn; reverential manner; gravity; as the solemness of public worship.
solemnity: A rite or ceremony annualy performed with religious reverence. Great was the cause; our old solemnities from no blind zeal or fond tradition rise, but sav’d from death, our Arguves yearly pay these grateful honors to the god of day.
A religious ceremony; a ritual performance attended with religious reverence; as the solemnity of a funral or of a sacrament.
A ceremony adapted to impress awe; as the solemnities of the last day.
Manner of acting awfully serious. With horrible solemnity he caused every thing to be prepared for his triumph of victory.
Gravity; steady seriouness; as the solemnity of the Spanish language.
solemnization: The act of solemnizing; celebration; as the solemnization of a marriage.
solemnize: To dignify or honor by ceremonies; to celebrate; as, to solemnize the birth of Christ. Their choice nobility and flow’r met from all parts to solemnize this feast.
To perform with ritual ceremonies and respect, or according to legal forms; as, to solemnize a marriage.
To peform religiouly once a year.
To make grave, serious and reverential; as, to solemnize the mind for the duties of the sanctuary.
solemnly: With gravity and religious reverence. Let us solemnly address the throne of grace.
With official formalities and be due authority. This question of law has been solemnly decided in the highest court.
With formal gravity and stateliness, or with affected gravity. There in deaf murmurs solemnly are wise.
With religious seriousness; as, I solemnly declare myselft innocent. I do solemnly assure the reader.
solenness: Singleness; a state of being unconnected with others.
solenite: Petrified solen, a genus of shells.
sol-fa: To pronounce the notes of the gammut, ascending or descending, ut, re ,mi, sol, la, and e converso.
solicit: To ask with some degree of earnestness; to make petition to; to apply to for obtaining something. This word implies earnestness in seeking, but I think less earnestness in seeking, but I tinks less earnestness than beg, imploreand importune, and more than ask or request; as when we Say, a man solicits the minister for an office; he solicits his father for a favor. Did I solicit thee form darkness to promote me?
TO ask for with some degree of earnestness; to seek by petition; as, to solicit an office; to solicit a favor.
To awake or excite to action; to summon; to invite. That fruit solicited her longing eye. Sounds and some tangible qualities solicit their proper senses, and force an entrance to the mind.
To attempt; to try to obtain. I view my crime, but kindle at the view, repeat old pleasures and solicit nes.
TO disturb; to disquiet; a Latinism rarely used. But anxious fears solicit my weak breast.
solicitation: Earnest request; a seeking to obtain something from another with some degree of zeal and earnestness; sometimes perhapsHe obtained a grant by repeated solicitations.
Excitement; invitation; as the solicitation of the senses.
soliciting: Requesting with earnestness; asking for; attempting to obtain.
solicitor: One who asks with earnestness; one that asks for another.

An attorney, advocate or counselor at law who is authorized to practice in the English court or chancery. In America, an advocate or counselor at law, who, like the attorney general or state’s attorney, prosecutes actions for the state.
solicitor-general: A lawyer in Great Britain, who is employed as counsel for the queen.
solicitous: Careful; anxious; ver desirous, as to obtain something. Men are often more solicitous to obtain the favor of their king or of the people, than of their Maker.

Careful; anxious concerned; as respecting an unknown but interesting event; followed usually by about or for. We say, a man is solicitous about the fate of his petition, or about the result of the negotiation. He is solicitous for the safety of his ship.
Anxious; concerned; followed by for, as when something is to be obtained. Be not solicitous for the future.
solicitously: Anxiously; with car and concern. Errors in religion or in science are to be solicitously avoided. A wise prince solicitously promotes the prosperity of his subjects.
solicitress: A female who solicits pr petitions.
solicitude: Carefulness; concern; anxiety; uneasiness of mind occasioned by the fear of evil or the desire of good. A man feels soliciude when his friend is sick. We feel solicitude for the success of an enterprise. With what solicitude should men seek to secure future happiness.
solid: Hard; firm; compact; having its constituent particles so close or dense as to resist the impression or penetration of other bodies. Hence solid bodies are not penetrable, not are the parts moveable and easily displaced like those of fluids. Solid is opposed to fluid and liquid.
Not hollow; full of matter; as a solid globe or cone, as distinguished from a hollow one.
Having all the gemetrical dimensions; cubic; as, a solid foot contains 1728 solid inhes.
Firm; compact; strong; as a solid pier; a solid pile; a solid wall.
Sound; not weakly; as a solid constitution of body.
solid angle: an angle formed by three or more plain angles meeting in a point.
solid foot: contains 1728 solid inches, weighing 1000 ounces of rain water.
solid square: in military language, is a square body of troops; a body in which the ranks and files are equal.
solidity: Firmness; hardness; density; compactness; that quality of bodies whcih resists impression and penetration; opposed to fluidity. That which hinders the apporach of two bodies moving ine towards another, I call solidity: .
Fullness of matter; opposed to hollowness.
Moral firmness; soundness; strength; validity; truth; certainty; as opposed to weakness or fallaciounes; as the soildity of arguments or reasonig; the solidity of principles, truths or opinious./ .
In geometry, the solid contents of a body.
solidly: Firmly; densely; compactly; as the parts of a pier solidly united.
Firmly; truly; on firm grounds. A complete brave man ought to know solidly the main end of his being in the world.
solidness: The quality of being firm, dense or compact; firmness; compactness; solidity; as of material bodies.
Soundness; strength; truth; validity; as of arguments, reasons, principles.
solidungulous: Having hoofs that are whole or not cloven. A horse is a solidungulous animal.
solifidian: One who maintains that faith alone, without works, is necessary to justification.
soliloquy: A talking to one’s self; a talking or discourse of a person alone, or not addressed to another person, even when others are persent. Lovers are always allowed the comfort of soliloquy.
A written composition, reciting what it is supposed a person speaks to himself. The whole poem is a solioquy.
soliped: AN animal whose foot is not cloven. The solopeds constitute an order of quadrupeds with undivided hoofs, corresponding to the Linnean genus Equus.
solitair: A person who lives in solitude; a recluse; a hermit.
solitarily: In solitude; alone; without company. Feed they people with thy rod, the flock of thy heritage, that dwell solitarily in the wood. Micah 7:14.
solitariness: The state of being alone; forbearance of company; retirement, or habitual retirement. At home, in wholesome solitariness.
Solitude; loneliness; destitution of company or of animated beings; applied to place; as the solitariness of the country or of a wood.
solitary: Living alone; not having company. Some of the more ferocious animals are solitary, seldom or never being found in flocks or herds. Thus the lion is called a solitary animal. Those rare and solitary, these in flocks.
Retired; remote from society; not having company, or not much frequented; as a solitary residence or place.
Lonely; destitute of company; as a solitary life.
Gloomy; still; dismal. Let that night be solitary, let no joyful voice come therein. Job 3:7.
Single; as a solitary instance of vengeance; a solitary example.
solitude: Loneliness; a state of being alone; a lonely life. Whoever is delighted with solitude, is either a wild beast or a god.
Loneliness; remote ness from society; as the solitude of a wood or a valley; the solitude of the country. The solitude of his little parish is become matter of great comfort to him.
A lonely place; a desert. In these deep solitudes and awful cells, where heavenly-pensive contemplation dwells.
solmization: A solfaing; a repetition or recital of the notes of the gammut.
solo: A tune, air or strain to be played by a single instrument, or sung by a single voice.
solstice: In astronomy, the point in the ecliptic at which the sun stops or ceases to recede from the equator, eith north in summer, or south in winter; a tropic or tropical point. There are two solstices; the summer solsitce, the first degree of Cancer, which the sun enter on the 21st of June, and the winter solstice, the first degree of Capricorn, which the sun enters on the 21st of December.
solsittial: Pertaining to a solstice; as a solstitial point.:
Happening at a solstice; usually with us, at the summer solstice or midsummer; as solstitial heat.
solubility: The quality of a body which renders it susceptible of solution; susceptibility of being dissolved in a fluid. The solubility of resins is chiefly confined to spirits or alcohol.
soluble: Susceptible of being dissolved in a fluid; capable of solution. Sugar is solube in water; salt is soluble only to a certain extent, that is, till water is saturated.
solute: In a general sense, loose; free; as a solute interpretation.
In botany, loose; not adhering; opposed to adnate; as a solute stipule.
solution: The act of separating the parts of any body; disruption; breach.

The operation or process of dissolving or melting in a fluid; as the solution of sugar or salt. The term solution is appiled to a very extensive class of phenomena. When a solid disappears in a liquid, if the compound exhibits perfect transparency, we have an example of solution. The word is applied both to the act of combination and to the result of the process. Thus common salt disappears in water, that is its solution takes place, and the liquid obtain ed is called a solution of salt in water. Solution is the result of attraction or affinity between. the fluid and the solid. This affinity continues to operate to a certain point, where it is overbalanced bly the cohesion of the solid; it then ceases the fluid issaid to be saturated, the point where the operation ceases is called saturation, and the fluid is called a saturated solution. Solution is a true chimical union. Mixture is a me
Resolution; explanation; the act of explaning or removing difficulty or doubt; as the solution of a doubt in casuistry.
In algebra and geometry, the answering of a question, or the resolving of a problem proposed.
solution of continuity: the separation of connection of connected substances or parts; applied, in surgery, to a fracture, laceration
solvability: Ability to pay all just debts.
solvable: That may be solved, resolved or explained.
solve: Properly, to loosen or separate the parts of any thing; hence, to explain; to resolve; to eclaircise; to unfold; to clear up; as what is obscure or difficult to be understood; as, to solve questions; to solve difficulties or a problem. When God shall solve the dark decrees of fate.
To remove; to dissipate; as, to solve doubts.
solvency: Ability to pay all debts or just claims; as, the solvency of a merchant is undoubted. The credit of a nation’s notes depends on a favorable opinion of its solvency.
solvent: Having the power of dissolving; as a solvent body.
Able to pay all just debts. The merchant is solvent.
Sufficient to pay all just debts. The estate is solvent.
A fluid that dissolves any substance, is called the solvent.
somatist: One who admits the existence or corporeal or material beings only; one who denies the existence of spiritual substances.
somatology: The doctrine of bodies or material substances.
some: Noting a certain quantity of a thing, but indeterminate; a portion greater or less. Give me some bread; drink some wine; bring some water.
Noting a number of persons or things, greater or less, but indeterminate. Some theoretical writes allege that there was a time when there was no such thing as society.

Noting a person or thing, but not known, or not specific and definite. Some person, I know not who, gave me the information. Enter the city, and some man will direct you to the house. Most gentlemen of property, as some period or other of their lives, are ambitious of representing their country in parliament.

It sometimes precedes a word of number or quantity, with the sense of about or near, noting want of certainty as to the specific number of amount, but something near it; as a village or some eighty houses; some two or three persons; some seventy miles distant; an object at some good distance.
Some is often opposed to others. Some men believe one thing, and others another.
somebody: A person unknown or uncertain; a person indeterminate. Jesus said, somebody hath touched me Luke 8:46. We must draw in somebody that may stand ‘Twixt us and danger.
A person of consideration. Before these days rose up Theudas, boasting himself to be somebody. Acis 5:36.
somersault: A leap by which a person jumps from a highth, turns over his head and falls upon his feet.
somerset: A leap by which a person jumps from a highth, turns over his head and falls upon his feet.
somehow: One way or other; on some way not yet known. The thing must have happened somehow or other.
something: An indeterminate or unknown event. Something must have happened to prevent the arrival of our friends at the time fixed. I shall call at two o’clock, unless something should prevent. [See Thing.
A substance or material thing, unknown indeterminate or not specified. A machine stops because something obstructs its motion. There must be something to support a wall or an arch.

A part; a portion more or less. Something yet of doubt remains. Still from his little he could something spare, to feed the hungry and to clothe the bare. Something of it arises from our infant state.
A little; an indefinite quantity or degree. The man asked me a dollar, but I gave him something more.
Distance not great. It must be done tonight, and something from the palace.
sometime: Once; formerly. That fair and warlike form, in which the majesty of buried Denmark did sometime march.
At one time or other hereafter.
sometimes: At times; at intervals; not always; not and then. We are sometimes indisposed, sometimes occupied, sometimes at leisure; that is, at some times. It is good that we be sometimes contradicted.
At one time; opposed to another time.
somewhat: More or less; a certain quantity or degreeThese salts have somewhat of a nitrous taste.
A part, greater or less. Somewhat of his good sense will suffer in this transfusion, and much of the beauty of his thoughts will be lost.
In some degree or quantity. This is somewhat more or less than was expected; he is somewhat aged; he is somewhat disappointed; somewhat disturbed.
somewhere: In some place, unknown or not specified; in one place or another. He lives somewhere in obscurity. Dryden somewhere says peace to the manes of the dead.
sommite: Nepheline; a mineral which occurs in small crystals and crystaline grains in the lava of mount Somma on Vesuvius.
somnambulation: The act of walking in sleep.
somnambulist: A person who walks in his sleep.
somniferous: Causing or inducing sleep; soporiferous; narcotic; as a somniferous potion.
somnific: Causing sleep; tending to induce sleep.
son: A male child; the male issue of a parent, father or mother. Jacob had twelve sons. Ishmael was the son of Hagar by Abraham.
A male descendant, however distant; hence in the plural, sons signifies descendants in general, a sense much used in the Scriptures. The whole human race are styled sons of Adam.
The compellation of an old man to a young one, or of a confessor to his penitent; a term of affection. Eli called Samuel his son. Be plain, good son, and homely in thy drift.
A native or inhabitant of a country; as the sons of Britain. Let our country never be ashamed of her sons.
The produce of any thing. Earth’s tall sons, the cedar, oak and pine.
sonata: A tune intended for an instrument only, as cantata is for the voice.
song: In general, that which is sung or uttered with musical modulations of the voice, whether of the human voice or that of a bird.

A little poem to be sung, or uttered with musical modulations; a ballad. The songs of a country are characteristic of its manners. Every country has its love songs, its war songs, and its patriotic songs.

A hymn; a sacred poem or hymn to be sung either in joy or thanksgiving, as that sung by Moses and the Israelites after escaping the dangers of the Arabian gulf and of Pharaoh; or of lamentation, as that of David over the death of Saul and Jonathan. Songs of joy are represented as constituting a part of heavenly felicity.
A lay; a strain; a poem. The bard that first adorn’d our native tongue, tun’d to his British lyre this ancient song.
Poetry; poesy; verse. This subject for heroic song pleas’d me.
songster: One that sings; one skilled in singing; not often applied to human beings, or only in slight contempt.
A bird that sings; as the little songster in his cage.
son-in-law: A man married to one’s daughter.
sonnet: A short poem of fourteen lines, two stanzas of four verses each and two of three each, the chymes being adjusted by a particular rule.
A short poem. I have a sonnet that will serve the turn.
sonneteer: A composer of sonnets or small poems; a small poet; usually in contempt.
sonometer: An instrument for measuring sounds or the intervals of sounds.
sonoriferous: That gives sound; sounding; as the sonoriferous particles of bodies.
sonorific: Producing sound; as the sonorific quality of a body.
sonorous: Giving sound when struck. Metals are sonorous bodies.
Loud sounding; giving a clear or loud sound; as a sonorous voice.
Yielding sound; as, the vowels are sonorous.
High sounding; magnificent of sound. The Italian opera, amidst all the meanness and familiarity of the thoughts, has something beautiful and sonorous in the expression.
sonorously: With sound; with a high sound.
sonorousness: The quality of yielding sound when struck or coming in collision with another body; as the sonorousness of metals.
Having or giving a loud or clear sound; as the sonorousness of a voice or an instrument.
sonship: The state of being a son, or of having the relation of a son.
Filiation; the character of a son.
soon: In a short time; shortly after any time specified or supposed; as soon after sunrise; soon after dinner; I shall soon return; we shall soon have clear weather.
Early; without the usual delay; before any time supposed. How is it that ye have come so soon to-day? Exodus 2:18.

Readily; willingly. But in this sense it accompanies would, or some other word expressing will. I would as soon see a river winding among woods or in meadows, as when it is tossed up in so many whimsical figures at Versailles. As soon as, or so soon as, immediately at or after another event. As soon as the mail arrives, I will inform you. As soon as Moses came nigh to the camp, he saw the calf and the dancing. Exodus 32:19.
soosoo: Among the Bengalese, the mane of a cetaceous fish, the Delphinus Gangeticus.
soot: A black substance formed by combustion, or disengaged from fuel in the process of combustion, rising in fine particles and adhering to the sides of the chimney or pipe conveying the smoke. Soot consists of oil, carbon and other substances. The soot of burnt pine forms lampblack.
To cover or foul with soot.
sooterkin: A kind of false birth fabled to be produced by the Dutch women from sitting over their stoves.
soothe: To flatter; to please with blandishments or soft words. Can I soothe tyranny? I’ve tried the force of every reason on him. Sooth’d and caress’d, been angry, sooth’d again .
To soften; to assuage; to mollify; to calm; as, to soothe one in pain or passion; or to soothe pain. It is applied both to persons and things.
To gratify; to please. Sooth’d with his future fame.
soother: A flatterer; he or that which softens or assuages.
soothsayer: A foreteller; a prognosticator; one who undertakes to foretell future events without inspiration.
soothsaying: The foretelling of future events by persons without divine aid or authority, and thus distinguished form prophecy.
sootiness: The quality of being sooty or foul with soot; fuliginousness.
sooty: Consisting of soot; fuliginons; as sooty matter.
Black like soot; dusky; dark; as the sooty flag of Acheron.
To black or foul with soot.
sop: Anything steeped or dipped and softened in liquor, but chiefly something thus dipped in broth or liquid food, and intended to be eaten. Sops in win, quantity for quantity, inebriate more than win itself.
Any thing given to pacify; so called from the sop given to Cerberus, in mythology. Hence the phrase, to give a sop to Cerberus.
To steep or dip in liquor.
soph: In colleges and universities, a student in his second year; a sophomore.
sophi: A title of the king of Persia.
sophism: A specious but fallacious argument; asubtilty in reasoning; an argument that is not supported by sound reasoning, or in which the inference is not justly deduced from the premises. When a false argument puts on the appearance of a true one, then it is properly called a sophism or fallacy.
sophist: A professor of philosophy; as the sophists of Greece.
sophister: A disputant fallaciously subtil; an artful but insidious logician; as an atheistical sophister. Not all the subtil objection of sophisters and rabbies against the gospel, so much prejudiced the reception of it, as the reproach of those crimes with which they aspersed the assemblies of Christians.
A professor of philosophy; a sophist.
To maintain by a fallacious argument.
sophistic: Fallaciously subtil; not sound; as sophistical reasoning or argument.
sophistical: Fallaciously subtil; not sound; as sophistical reasoning or argument.
sophisticate: To adulterate; to corrupt by something spurious or foreign; to pervert; as, to sophisticate nature, philosophy or the understanding.
To adulterate; to render spurious; as merchandise; as, to sophisticate wares or liquors. They purchase but sophisticated ware.
Adulterated; not pure; not genuine. So truth, when only one supplied the state, grew scarce and dear, and hey sophisticate.
sophistication: The act of adulterating; a counterfeiting or debasing the purity of some thing be a foreign admixture; adulteration.
sophisticator: One that adulterates; one who injures the purity and genuineness of any thing by foreign admixture.
sophistry: Fallacious reasoning; reasoning sound in appearance only. These men have obscured and confounded the nature of things by their false principles and wretched sophistry.
sophomore: A student in a college or university, in his second year.
soporiferous: Causing sleep, or tending to produce it; narcotic; opiate; anodyne; somniferous. The poppy pssesses soporiferous qualities.
soporific: Causing sleep; tending to cause sleep; narcotic; as the soporific virtues of opium.
A medicine, drug, plant or other thing that has the quality of inducing sleep.
sopper: One that sops or dips in liquor some thing to be eaten.
sorb: The service tree or its fruit .
sorbate: A compound of sorbic acid with a base.
sorbic: Pertaining to the sorbus or service tree; as sorbic acid.
sorbile: That may be drank or sipped.
sorbition: The act or drinking or sipped.
sorbonist: A doctor of the Sorbonne in the university of Paris. Sorbonne is the place of meeting, and hence is used for the whole faculty of theology.
sorcerer: A conjurer; an enchanter; a magician. The Egyptian sorcerers contended with Moses.
sorcery: Magic; enchantment; witchcraft; divination be the assistance of evil spirits, or the power of commanding evil spirits. Adder’s wisdom I have learn’d to fence my ears against the sorceries.
sordawalite: A mineral so named from Sordawald, in Wibourg. It is nearly black, rarely gray or green.
sordes: Foul matter; excretions; dregs; filthy, useless or rejected matter of any kind.
sordet: A little pipe in the mouth of a trumpet to make it sound lower or shriller.
sordine: A little pipe in the mouth of a trumpet to make it sound lower or shriller.
sordid: Filthy; foul; dirty; gross. There Charon stands a sordid god.
Vile; base; mean; as vulgar, sordid mortals.
Meanly avaricious; covetous; niggardly. He may be old and yet not sordid, who refuses gold.
sordidness: Meanness; baseness; as the execrable sordidness of the delights of Tiberius.
sore: A place in an animal body where the skin and flesh are ruptured or bruised, so as to be pained with the slightest pressure.
Tender and susceptible of pain from pressure; as, a boil, ulcer or abscess is very sore; a wounded place is sore; inflammation renders a part sore.

Tender, as the mind; easily pained, grieved or vexed; very susceptible of irritation from any thing that crosses the inclination. Malice and hatred are very fretting, and apt to make our minds sore and uneasy.
sorehon: A kind of servile tenure which subjected the tenant to maintain his chieftain gratuitously, whenever he wished to indulge himself in a debauch. So that when a person obtrudes himself on another for bed and board, he is said to sorn, or be a sorner.
sorn: A kind of servile tenure which subjected the tenant to maintain his chieftain gratuitously, whenever he wished to indulge himself in a debauch. So that when a person obtrudes himself on another for bed and board, he is said to sorn, or be a sorner.
sorel: A buck of the third year.
sorely: With violent pain and distress; grievously; greatly; as, to be sorely pained or afflicted.
Greatly; violently; severely; as, to be sorely pressed with want; to be sorely wounded.
soreness: The tenderness of any part of an animal body, which renders it extremely susceptible of pain from pressure; as the soreness of a boil, an abscess or wound.
Figuratively, tenderness of mind, or susceptibility of mental pain.
sorgo: A plant of the genus Holcus.
sorites: In logic, an argument where one proposition is accumulated on another. Thus, all men of revenge have their souls often uneasy. Uneasy souls are a plague to themselves. Now to be one’s own plague is folly in the extreme.
sororicide: The murder or murderer of a sister.
sorrage: The blades of green wheat or barley.
sorrance: In farriery, any disease or sore in horses.
sorrel: Of a reddish color; as a sorel horse.
A reddish color; a faint red.
A plant of the genus Rumex, so named from its acid taste. The wood sorrel is of the genus Oxalis. The Indian red and Indian white sorrels are of the genus Hibiscus.
sorrily: Meanly; despicably; pitiably; in a wretched manner. Thy pipe, O Pan, shall help though I sing sorrily.
sorrow: The uneasiness or pain of mind which is produced by the loss of any good. or of frustrated hopes of good, or expected loss of happiness; to grieve; to be sad. I rejoice not that ye were made sorry, but the ye sorrowed to repentance. 2 Corinthians 7:9. Sorrowing most of all for the words which he spoke, that they should see his face no more. Acts 20:38.
sorrowful: Sad; grieving for the loss of some good, or on account of some expected evil.
Producing sorrow; exciting grief; mournful; as a sorrowful accident.
Expressing grief; accompanied with grief; as sorrowful meat. Job 6:7.
sorrowfully: In a sorrowful manner; in a manner to produce grief.
sorry: Grieved for the loss of some good; pained for some evil that has happened to one’s self or friends or country. It does not ordinarily imply severe grief, but rather slight or transient regret. It may be however, and often is used to express deep grief. We are sorry to lose the company of those we love; we are sorry to lose friends or property; we are sorry for the misfortunes of our friends or of out country. And the king was sorry. Matthew 14:9.
Poor; mean; vile; worthless; as a sorry slave; a sorry excuse. Coarse complexions, and cheeks of sorry grain- .
sort: A kind or species; any number or collection of individual persons or thing characterized by the same or like qualities; as a sort of men; a sort of horses; a sort of trees; a sort of poems or writings. Sort is not a technical word, and therefore is used with less precision or more latitude than genus or species in the sciences.
Manner; form of being or acting. Flowers, in such sort worn, can neither be smelt not seen well by those that wear them. To Adam in what sort shall I appear?
Class or order; as men of the wiser sort, or the better sort; all sorts of people.
A company or knot of people.
sortal: Pertaining to or designating a sort.
sortilege: The act or practice of drawing lots.
sortment: The act of sorting; distribution into classes of kinds.
sory: A fossil substance, firm, but of a spungy, cavernous structure, rugged on the surface, and containing blue vitriol; a sulphate of iron.
soss: To fall at once into a chair or seat; to sit lazily.
sot: A stupid person; a blockhead; a dull fellow; a dolt.
A person stupefied by excessive drinking; an habitual drunkard. What can ennoble sots?
To stupefy; to infatuate; to besot. I hat to see a brave bold bellow sotted.
sottish: Dull; stupid; senseless; doltish; very foolish. How ignorant are sottish pretenders to astrology!
sottishness: Dullness in the exercise reason; stupidity. Few consider into what a degree of sottishness and confirmed ignorance men may sin themselves.
sou: sous. A French money of account, and a copper coin, in value the 20th part of a livre or of a franc.
sought: and pp. of seek, pronI am found of them who sought me not. Isaiah 65:1.
soul: The spiritual, rational and immortal substance in man, which distinguishes him from brutes; that part of man which enables him to think and reason, and which renders him a subject of moral government. The immortality of the soul is a fundamental article of the christian system. Such is the nature of the human soul that it must have a God, an object of supreme affection.
The understanding; the intellectual principle. The eyes of our soul then only begin to see, when our bodily eye are closing.
Vital principle. Thou son, of this great world both eye and soul.
Spirit; essence; chief part; as charity, the soul of all the virtues. Emotion is the soul of eloquence.
Life; animation principle or part; as, an able commander is the soul of an army.
soul-destroying: Pernicious to the soul. Procrastination of repentance and faith is a soul-destroying evil.
souled: Furnished with a soul or mind; as Grecian chiefs largely souled.
soulless: Without a soul, or without greatness or nobleness of mind; mean; spiritless. Slave, soulless villain.
soul-scot: A funeral duty, or money paid by the Romanists in former times for a requiem for the soul.
soul-shot: A funeral duty, or money paid by the Romanists in former times for a requiem for the soul.
soul-selling: Selling persons; dealing in the purchase and sale of human beings.
soul-sick: Diseased in mind or soul; morally diseased.
sound: Entire; unbroken; not shaky, split or defective; as sound timber.
Undecayed; whole; perfect, or not defective; as sound fruit; a sound apple or melon.
Unbroken; not bruised or defective; not lacerated or decayed; as a sound limb.
Not carious; not decaying; as a sound tooth.
Not broken or decayed; not defective; as a sound ship.
sound-board: A board which propagates the sound in an organ. To many a row of pipes the sound-board breathes.
sounding board: A board which propagates the sound in an organ. To many a row of pipes the sound-board breathes.
sounded: Caused to make a noise; uttered audibly.
sounding: Trying the depth of water by the plummet; examining the intention or will.
Having a magnificent sound; as words more sounding or significant.
The act of uttering noise; the act or endeavoring to discover the opinion or desires; the act of throwing the lead.
sounding-board: A board or structure with a flat surface, suspended over a pulpit to prevent the sound of the preacher’s voice from ascending, and thus propagating it further in a horizontal direction.
sounding-rod: A rod or piece of iron used to ascertain the depth of water in a ship’s hold. It is let down in a groove by a pump.
soundings: Any place or part of the ocean, where a deep sounding line will reach the bottom; also, the kind of ground or bottom where the lead reaches.
soundless: That cannot be fathomed; having no sound.
soundly: Severely; lustily; with heavy blows; smartly; as, to beat one soundly.
Truly; without fallacy or error; as, to judge or reason soundly.
Firmly; as a doctrine soundly settled.
Fast; closely; so as not to be easily awakened; as, to sleep soundly.
soundness: Wholeness; entireness; an unbroken, unimpaired or undecayed state; as the soundness of timber, of fruit, of the teeth, of a limb.

An unimpaired state of an animal or vegetable body; a state in which the organs are entire and regularly perform their functions. We say, the soundness of the body, the soundness of the constitution, the soundness of health.
Firmness; strength; solidity; truth; as soundness of reasoning or argument, of doctrine or principles.
Truth; rectitude; firmness; freedom from error or fallacy; orthodoxy; as soundness of faith.
soup: Broth; a decoction of flesh for food.
sour: Acid; having a pungent taste; sharp to the taste; tart; as, vinegar is sour; sour cider; sour beer.
Acid and austere or astringent; as, sunripe fruits are often sour.
Harsh of temper; crabbed; peevish; austere; morose; as a man of a sour temper.
Expressing discontent or peevishness. He never uttered a sour word. The lord treasurer often looked on me with a sour countenance.
source: Properly, the spring or fountain from which a stream of water proceeds, or any collection of water within the earth or upon its surface, in which a stream originates. This is called also the head of the stream. We call the water of a spring, where it issues from the earth, the source of the stream or rivulet proceeding form it. We say also that springs have their sources in subterranean ponds, lakes or collections of water. We say also that a large river has is source in a lake. For example, the St. Lawrence has its source in the great lakes of America.

First cause; original; that which gives rise to any thing. Thus ambition, the love of power and of fame, have been the sources of half the calamities of nations. Intemperance is the source of innumerable evils to individuals.
The first producer; he or that which originates; as Greece the source of arts.
sourdet: The little pipe of a trumpet.
sour-gourd: A plant of the genus Adansonia.
souring: Making acid; becoming sour; making peevish.
sourish: Somewhat sour; moderately acid; as sourish fruit; a sourish taste.
sourly: With peevishness; with acrimony. The stern Athenian prince the sourly smil’d.
sourness: Acidity; sharpness to the taste; tartness; as the sourness of vinegar or of fruit. Sourness being one of those simple ideas which one cannot describe.
Asperity; harshness of temper. Take care that no sourness and moroseness mingle with our seriousness of mind.
sour-sop: A plant, the annona muricata. The custard apple.
souse: Something kept or steeped on pickle.
To steep in pickle. But souse the cabbage with a bounteous heart.
To plunge into water. They soused me into the Thames, with as little remorse as they drown blind puppies.
souterrain: A grotto or cavern under ground.
south: The north and south are opposite points in the horizon; each ninety degrees or the quarter of a great circle distant from the east and west. A man standing with his face towards the east or rising sun, has the south on his right hand. The meridian of every place is a great circle passing through the north and south points. Strictly, south is the horizontal point in the meridian of a place, on the right hand of a person standing with his face towards the east. But the word is applied to any point in the meridian, between the horizon and the zenith.
In a less exact sense, any point or place on the earth or in the heavens, which is near the meridian towards the right hand as one faces the east.

A southern region, country or place; as the queen of the south, in Scriptures. So in Europe, the people of Spain and Italy are spoken of as living in the south. In the United States, we speak of the states of the south, and of the north.
The wind that blows from the north.
In any place north of the tropic of Cancer, pertaining to or lying in the meridian towards the sun; as a south wind.
southeast: The point of the compass equally distant from the south and east.
In the direction of southeast, or coming from the southeast; s a southeast wind.
southerly: Lying at the south, or in a direction nearly south; as a southerly point.
Coming from the south or a point nearly south; as a southerly wind.
southern: Belonging to the south; meridional; as the southern hemisphere.
Lying towards the south; as a southern country or climate.
Coming form the south; as a southern breeze.
sothernwood: suth’ernwood, A plant agreeing in most parts with the wormwood. The southernwood is the Artemisia abrotanum, a different species form the wormwood.
southing: Going towards the south; as the southing sun.
Tendency or motion to the south.
The southing of the moon, the time at which the moon passes the meridian.
Course or distance south; as a ship’s southing.
southward: suth’ard. Towards the south; as, to go southward.
suth’ard. The southern regions or countries.
southwest: The point of the compass equally distant from the south and west.
Lying in the direction of the southwest; as a southwest country.
Coming from the southwest; as a southwest wind.
southwesterly: In the direction of southwest, or nearly so.
Coming from the southwest, or a point near it; as a southwesterly wind.
southwestern: In the direction of southwest, or nearly so; as, to sail a southwestern course.
sovereign: Supreme in power; possessing supreme dominion; as a sovereign ruler of the universe.
Supreme; superior to all others; chief. God is the sovereign good of all who love and obey him.
Supremely efficacious; superior to all others; predominant; effectual; as a sovereign remedy.
Supreme; pertaining to the first magistrate of a nation; as sovereign authority: .
sovereignly: suv’eranly. Supreme power; supremacy; the possession power. Absolute sovereignty belongs to God only.
sow: To scatter seed for growth and the production of a crop. In New England, farmers begin tosow in April. They that sow in tears, shall reap in joy. Psalm 126:5.
The female of the hog kind or of swine.
pret. sowed; pp. sowed or sown.
sow-bread: A plant of the genus Cyclamen.
sow-thistle: A plant of the genus Sonchus. The downy sow-thistle is of the genus Andryala.
sowed: Scattered on ground, as seed; sprinkled with seed, as ground. We say, seed is sowed; or land is sowed.
sower: He that scatters seed for propagation. Behold, a sower went forth to sow. Matthew 13:3.
One who scatters or spreads; as a sower of words.
A breeder; a promoter; as a sower of suits.
sowing: Scattering, as seed; sprinkling with seed, as ground; stocking with seed.
The act of scattering seed for propagation.
sowins: Flummery made of oatmeal somewhat soured.
sown: Scattered, as seed; sprinkled with seed, as ground.
soy: A kind of source, used in Japan.
sozzle: A sluttish woman, or one that spills water and other liquids carelessly.
space: Room; extension. Space in the abstract, is mere extension. Pure space is capable neither of resistance nor motion.

Any quantity of extension. In relation to bodies, space is the interval between any two or more object’ as the space between two stars or two hills The quantity of space or extent between bodies, constitutes their distance form each other.
The distance or interval between lines; as in books. The space in music are named as well as the lines.
Quantity of time; also, the interval between two points of time. Nine times the space that measures day and God may defer his judgment for a time, and give a people a longer space for repentance.
A short time; a while. To stay your deadly strife a space.
spacious: Wide; roomy; having large or ample room; not narrow; as a spacious church; a spacious hall or drawing room.
Extensive; vast in extent; as the spacious earth; the spacious ocean.
spaciousness: Wideness; largeness of extent; roominess; as the spaciousness of the rooms in a building.
Extensiveness; vastness of extent; as the spaciousness of the ocean.
spade: An instrument for digging, consisting of a broad palm with a handle.
A deer three years old; written also spaid.
spadeful: As much as a spade will hold.
spadiceous: Of a light red color, usually denominated bay.
In botany, a spadiceous flower, is a sort of aggregate flower, having a receptacle common to many florets, within a spathe, as in palms, dracontium, arum.
spadille: spadil’. The ace of spades at omber.
spadix: In botany, the receptacle in palms and some other plants, proceeding from a spathe.
spake: of speak; nearly obsolete. We not use spoke.
spalt: A whitish scaly mineral, used to promote the fusion of metals.
spelt: A whitish scaly mineral, used to promote the fusion of metals.
A species of grain of the genus Triticum; called also German wheat.
span: The space from the end of the thumb to the end of the little finger when extended; nine inches; the eighth of a fathom.
A short space of time. Life’s but a span; I'll every inch enjoy.

A span of horses, consists of two of nearly the same color, and otherwise nearly alike, which are usually harness side by side. The word signifies properly the same as yoke, when applied to horned cattle, from buckling or fastening together. But in America, span always implies resemblance in color at least; it being an object of ambition with gentlemen and with teamters to unite two horses abreast that are alike.
In seamen’s language, a small line or cord, the middle of which is attached to a stay.
To measure by the hand with the fingers extended, or with the fingers encompassing the object; as, to span a space or distance; to span a cylinder.
snancel: A rope to tie a cow's hind legs.
spancel: To tie the legs of a horse or cow with a rope .
spancounter: A play at which within a span or circuit marked.
spanfarting: A play at which within a span or circuit marked.
spandrel: The space between the curve of an arch and the right lines inclosing it.
spang: A spangle or shining ornament; a thin piece of shining metal; something brilliant used as an ornament.
An y little thing sparkling and brilliant like pieces of metal; as crystals of ice. For the rich spangles that adorn the sky.
spangle: To set or sprinkle with spangles; to adorn with small distinct brilliant bodies; as a spangled breastplate. What stars do spangle heaven with such beauty-
spaniel: A dog used in sports of the field, remarkable for his sagacity and obedience.
TO fawn; to cringe; to be obsequious.
spanish-broom: A plant of the genus Spartium.
spanish-brown: A species of earth used in paints.
spanish-fly: A fly or insect, the cantharis used in vesicatories, or composition for raising blisters.
spanish-white: A white earth from Spain, used in paints.
spank: To strike with the open hand; to slap
spanker: In seamen’s language, a ship driver; a large sail occasionally set upon the mizenyard or gaff, the foot being extended by a boom.
One that takes long strides in walking; also a stout person.
span-long: Of the length of a span.
spanner: The lock of a fusee or carbine; or the fusee itself.
spanning: Measuring with the hand; encompassing with the fingers.
spar: A stone that breaks into a regular shape; marcasite. This name is popularly given to any crystalized mineral of a shining luster.
Around piece of timber. This name is usually given to the round pieces of timber used for the yards and top-masts of ships.
To bar; to shut close or fasten with a bar.
To dispute; to quarrel in words; to wrangle.
spare: To use frugally; not to be profuse; not to waste. Thou thy Father’s thunder did’st not sparel .

To save or withhold from any particular use or occupation. He has no bread to spare, that is, to withhold from his necessary uses. All the time he could spare from the necessary cares of his weighty charge, he bestowed on prayer and serving of God.
To part with without much inconvenience; to do without. I could have better spar’d a better man. Nor can we spare you long- .
To omit; to forbear. We might have spared this toil and expense; Be pleas’d your politics to spare.

To use tenderly; to treat with pity and forbearance; to forbear to afflict, punish or destroy. Spare us, good Lord. dim sadness did not spare celestial visages. But man alone can whom be conquers spare.
spareness: State of being lean or thin; leanness.
sparerib: The piece of a hog taken from the side, consisting of the ribs with little flesh on them.
sparing: Using frugally; forbearing; omitting to punish or destroy.
Scanty; not plentiful; not abundant; as a sparing diet.
Saving; parsimonious. Virgil being so very sparing of his words, and leaving so much to be imagined by the reader, can never be translated as he ought in amy modern tongue.
sparingly: Frugally; parsimoniously; not lavishly. High titles of honor were in the king’s minority sparingly granted, because dignity then waited on desert. Commend but sparingly whom thou dost love.
Abstinently; moderately. Christians are obliged to taste even the innocent pleasures of life but sparingly. WWV”AD .
Seldom; not frequently. The morality of a grave sentence, affected by Lucan, is more sparingly used by Virgil.
spark: A small particle of fire or ignited substance, which is emitted from bodies in combustion, and which either ascends with the smoke, or is darted in another direction.
Asmall shining body or transient light. We have here and there a little clear light, and some sparks of bright knowledge.
A small portion of any thing active. If any spark of life is yet remaining.
A very small portion. If you have a spark or generosity.
A brisk, showy, gay man. The finest sparks and cleanest beaux.
sparkle: To emit sparks; to send off small ignited particles; as burning fuel.
To glitter; to glisten; as, a brilliant sparkles; sparkling colors.
To twinkle; to glitter; as sparkling stars.
sparkler: He or that which sparkles; one whose eyes sparkle.
sparkling: or a. Emitting sparks; glittering; lively; as sparkling wine; sparkling eyes.
sparrow: A small bird of the genus Fringilla and order of Passers. These birds are frequently seen about houses.
sparrow-hawk: A small species of short winged hawk.
sparhawk: A small species of short winged hawk.
sparry: Resembling spar, or consisting or spar; having a confused cyrstraline structure; spathose.
sparse: Thinly scattered; set or planted here and there; as a sparse population.
In botany, not opposite, not alternate, nor in any apparent regular order; applied to branches, leaves peduncles.
spartan: Pertaining to ancient Sparta; hence, hardly undaunted; as Spartan souls; Spartan bravery.
spasm: A n involuntary contraction of muscles or muscular fibers in animal bodies; irregular motion of the muscles or muscular fibers; convulsion; cramp.
spasmodic: A medicine good for removing spasm; but I believe the word generally employed in anti- spasmodic.
Consisting in spasm; as a spasmodic affection.
spat: A petty combat; a little quarrel or dissension.
spathaceous: Having a calyx like a sheath.
spathe: In botany, the calyx of a spadix opening or bursting longitudinally, in form of a sheath. It is also applied to the calyx of some flowers which have no spadix, as of narcissus, crocus, iris
spathic: Foliated or lamellar. Spathic iron is a mineral of a foliated structure, and a yellowish or brownish color.
spathiform: Resembling spar in form. The ocherous, spathiform and mineralized forms of urinate-
spathous: Having a calyx like a sheath.
spatter: To scatter a liquid substance on; to sprinkle with water or any fluid, or with any moist and dirty matter; as, to spatter a coat; to spatter the floor; to spatter the boots with mud.
To throw out any thing offensive; as, to spatter foul speeches.
To scatter about; as, to spatter water here and there.
To throw out of the mouth in a scattered manner; to sputter.
spatterdashes: Coverings for the legs to keep them clean from water and mud.
spattered: Sprinkling with moist some liquid or dirty substance.
spattling-poppy: A plant; white behen; a species of Campion.
spatulate: In botany, a spatulate leaf is one shaped like a spatula or battledore, being roundish with a long, narrow, linear base; as in cistus incanus.
spavin: A tumor or excrescence that forms on the inside of a horse’s hough, not far from the elbow; at first like gristle, but afterwards hard and bony.
spaw: A mineral water from a place of this name in Germany. The name may perhaps be applied to other similar waters.
spa: A mineral water from a place of this name in Germany. The name may perhaps be applied to other similar waters.
spawl: To throw saliva from the mouth in a scattering form; to disperse spittle in a careless dirty manner. Why must he sputter, spawl and slaver it?
Saliva or spittle thrown out carelessly.
spawling: Throwing spittle carelessly from the mouth.
spawn: The eggs of fish or frogs, when ejected.
Any product or offspring; an expression of contempt.
To produce or deposit, as fishes do their eggs.
spawned: Produced or deposited, as the eggs of fish or frogs.
spawner: the female fish. the spawner and the melter of the barbel cover their spawn with sand.
spay: To castrate the female of a beast by cutting and by taking out the uterus; as, to spay a sow.
speak: To utter words or articulate sounds, as human beings; to express thoughts bywords. Children learn to speak at an early age. The organs may be so obstructed that a man may not be able to speak. Speak, Lord, for thy servant hearth. 7 Samuel 3:9.

To utter a speech, discourse or harangue; to utter thoughts in a public assembly. A man may be well informed on a subject, and yet to diffident to speak in public. Many of the nobility make them selves popular by speaking in parliament against those things which were most grateful to his majesty.
To talk; to express opinions; to dispute. An honest man, sir, is able to speak for himself, when the knave is not.
To discourse; to make mention of. Lucan speaks of a part of Cesar’s army that came to him from the Leman lake. The Scripture speaks only of those to whom it speaks.
speaker: noun
One that speaks, in what ever manner.
One that proclaims or celebrates. -No other speaker of my living actions.
One that utters or pronounces a discourse; usually, one that utters a speech in public. We say, a man is a good speaker, or a bad speaker.
The person who presides in a deliberative assembly, preserving order and regulating the debates; as the speaker of the house of commons; the speaker of a house or representatives.
speaking: The act of uttering words; discourse.
speaking-trumpet: A trumpet by which the sound of the human voice may be propagated to a great distance.
spear: A long pointed weapon, used in war and hunting by thrusting or throwing; a lance.
A sharp pointed instrument with barbs; used for stabbing fish and other animals.
A shoot, as of grass; usually spire.
To pierce with a spear; to kill with a spear; as, to spear a fish.
To shoot into a long stem.
speared: Pierced or killed with a spear.
spear-foot: The far foot behind; used of a horse.
spear-grass: In New England, this name is given to a species of Poa .
spearing: Piercing or killing with a spear.
spearman: One who is armed with a spear. Psalm 68:30.
spearmint: A plant of the genus Mentha; a species of mint.
spear-wort: A plant; the popular name of the Ranunculus flammula.
special: Designating a species of sort. A special idea is called by the schools a species.
Particular; peculiar; noting something more than ordinary. She smiles with a special grace. Our Savior is represented every where in Scripture as the special patron of the poor and afflicted.
Appropriate; designed for a particular purpose. A private grant is made by a special act of parliament or of congress.
Extraordinary; uncommon. Our charities should be universal, but chiefly exercised on special opportunities.
Chief in excellence. The king hath drawn the special head of all the land together.
special administration: in law, is one in which the power of an administrator is limited to the administration of certain specific effects, and not the effects in general of the deceased.
special bail: consists of actual sureties recognized to answer for the appearance of person in court; as distinguished from common boil, which is nominal.
special bailif: is a bailif appointed by the sherif for making arrests and serving processes.
special demurrer: is one in which the cause of demurrer is particularly stated.
special imparlance: is one in which there is a saving of all exceptions to the writ or count, or of all exception whatsoever.
special jury: is one which is called upon motion of either party, when the cause is supposed to require it.
special matter in evidence: the particular facts in the case on which the defendant relies.
special plea: in bar, is a plea which sets forth the particular facts or reasons why the plaintif's demand should be barred as a release, accord
special property: a qualified or limited property, as the property which a man acquires in wild animals by reclaiming them.
special session of a court: an extraordinary session; a session beyond the regular stated sessions; or in corporations and counties in England, a petty session held by a few justices for dispatching small business.
special statue: is a private act of the legislature, such as respects a private act of the legislature, such as respect a private person or individual.
special tail: is where a gift is restrained to certain hears of the donee’s body, and does not descend to the heirs in general.
special verdict: is a verdict in which the jury find the facts and state them as proved, but leave the law arising from the facts to be determined by the court. Another method of finding a special verdict, is when the jury find a verdict generally for the plaintif, but subject to the opinion of the court on a special case stated by the counsel on both sides, with regard to a matter of law.
special warrent: a warrant to take a person and bring him before a particular justice who granted the warrant.
specially: Particularly; in a manner beyond what is common, or out of the ordinary course. Every signal deliverance form danger ought to be specially noticed as a divine interposition.
For a particular purpose. A meeting of the legislature is specially summoned.
specialty: Particularity. specialty of rule hat been neglected.
A particular or peculiar case. Note. This word is now little used in the senses above. Its common acceptation is,
A special contract; an obligation or bond; the evidence of a debt by deed or instrument under seal. Such a debt is called a debt by specialty, in distinction from simple contract.
specie: spe’shy. Coin; copper, silver or gold coined and used as a circulating medium of commerce.
species: In zoology, a collection of organized beings derived from one common parentage by natural generation, characterized by one peculiar from one common parentage by natural generation, characterized by one peculiar form, liable to vary within certain narrow limits. These accidental and limited variations are varieties. Different races from the same parents are called varieties.

In botany, all the plants which spring from the same see, or which resemble each other in certain character or in variable forms. There are aS many species as there are different in variable forms or circumstances only with in certain narrow limits. These accidental and limited variations are varieties. Different races from the same parents are called varieties.
In logic, a special idea, corresponding to the specific distinctions of things in nature.
Sort; kind; in a loose sense; as a species of low cunning in the world; aS a species of generosity; a species of cloth.
specific: That makes a thing of the species of which it is designating the peculiar property of properties of a thing, which constitute its species, and distinguish it from other things. Thus we say, the specific form of an animal or a plant; the specific form of a cube or square; the specific qualities of a plant or a drug; the specific difference between an acid and an alkali; the specific distinction between virtue and vice. Specific difference is that primary attribute which distinguishes each species form one another.

In medicine, appropriate for the cure of a particular disease; that certainly cures or is less fallible than others; as a specific remedy for the gout. The Saratogs waters are found to be a specific remedy, or nearly so, for the cure of bilious complaints, so called.
In medicine, a remedy that certainly cures particular disease.
specifical: That makes a thing of the species of which it is designating the peculiar property of properties of a thing, which constitute its species, and distinguish it from other things. Thus we say, the specific form of an animal or a plant; the specific form of a cube or square; the specific qualities of a plant or a drug; the specific difference between an acid and an alkali; the specific distinction between virtue and vice. Specific difference is that primary attribute which distinguishes each species form one another.

In medicine, appropriate for the cure of a particular disease; that certainly cures or is less fallible than others; as a specific remedy for the gout. The Saratogs waters are found to be a specific remedy, or nearly so, for the cure of bilious complaints, so called.
specific character: in botany, a circumstance or circumstances distinguishing one species from every other species of the same genus.
specific gravity: in philosophy, the weight that belongs to an equal bulk of each body.
specific name: in botany, is the trivial name as distinguished form the generic naSpecific name is now used for the name which, appended to the name of the genus, constitutes the distinctive name of the species; but it was originally applied by Linne to the essential character of the species, or the essential difference. The present specific names he at fist called the trivial names.
