Work done by sewing in a particular manner.
The forming of land into ridges or divisions.
stive: To make hot, sultry and close.
stiver: A Dutch coin of about the value of a half-penny sterling, or the cent of the United States. It is also a money of account in Holland and Flanders.
stoak: To stop; to choke; in seamens language.
stoat: An animal of the weasel king; the ermine. This animal is called stoat when of a reddish color, and ermine when white, as in winter.
stoccade: A stab; a thrust with a rapier.
A fence or barrier made with stakes or posts planted in the earth; a Slight fortification.
stoccado: A stab; a thrust with a rapier.
A fence or barrier made with stakes or posts planted in the earth; a Slight fortification.
stock: The stem or main body of a tree or other plant; the fixed, strong, firm part; the origin and support of the branches. Job 74:8.
The stem in which a graft is inserted, and which is its support. The cion overruleth the stock quite.
A post; something fixed, solid and senseless. When all our fathers worshipd stocks and stones.
A person very stupid, dull and senseless. Lets be no stoics, nor no stocks.
stockade: In fortification, a sharpened post or stake set in the earth.
A line of posts or stakes set in the earth as a fence or barrier.
TO surround or fortify with sharpened posts fixed in the ground.
stockading: Fortifying with sharpened posts or stakes.
stockbroker: A broker who deals in the purchase and sale of stocks or shares in the public funds.
stock-dove: The ring-dove. The stock dove is the wild pigeon of Europe, long considered as the stock of the domestic pigeon, but now regarded as a distinct species. The ring-dove is the Columba palumbus.
stock-fish: Cod dried hard and without salt.
stock-gillyflower: A plant, a species of Cheiranthus; sometimes written stock July flower.
stocking: A garment made to cover the leg.
stock-jobber: One who speculates in the public funds for gain; one whose occupation is to buy and sell stocks.
stock-jobbing: The act of art of dealing in the public funds.
stock-still: Still as a fixed post; perfectly still. Our preachers stand stock-still in the pulpit.
stocky: Thick and firm; stout. A stocky person is one rather thick than tall or corpulent; one whose bones are covered well with flesh, but without a prominent belly.
stoic: A disciple of the philosopher Zeno, who founded a sect. He taught that men should be free from passion, unmoved by joy or grief, and submit without complaint to the unavoidable necessity by which all things are governed.
Pertaining to the Stoics or to their doctrines.
Not affected by passion; unfeeling; manifesting indifference to pleasure or pain.
stoical: Pertaining to the Stoics or to their doctrines.
Not affected by passion; unfeeling; manifesting indifference to pleasure or pain.
stoically: In the manner of the Stoics; without apparent feeling or sensibility; with indifference to pleasure or pain.
stoicalness: The state of being stoical; indifference to pleasure or pain.
stoicism: The opinions and maxims of the Stoics.
A real or pretended indifference to pleasure or pain; insensibility.
stoke: is the same word as stock, differently applied. It is found in many English names of towns.
One who looks after the fire in a brew-house.
stoker: One who looks after the fire in a brew-house.
stole: A long vest or robe; a garment worn by the priests of some denominations when they officiate. It is a broad strip of cloth reaching from the neck to the feet.
A sucker; a shoot from the root of a plant, by which some plants may be propagated; written also stool.
stolen: Stoln. The passive participle of steal. Stolen waters are sweet. Proverbs 9:17.
stoloniferous: Producing suckers; putting forth suckers; as a stoloniferous stem.
stomach: In animal bodies, a membranous receptacle, the organ of digestion, in which food is prepared for entering into the several parts of the body for its nourishment.
Appetite; the desire of food caused by hunger; as a good stomach for roast beef.
Inclination; liking. He which hath no stomach to this fight, let him depart-- .
Anger; violence of temper. Stern was his look, and full of stomach vain.
Sullenness; resentment; willful obstinacy; stubbornness.V\ . This sort of crying proceeding from pride, obstinacy and stomach, the will, where the fault lies, must be bent.
stomacher: An ornament or support to the breast, worn by females. Isaiah 3:24.
stomachful: Willfully obstinate; stubborn; perverse; as a stomachful boy.
stomachic: Pertaining to the stomach; as stomachic vessels.
Strengthening to the stomach; exciting the action of the stomach.
A medicine that excites the action and strengthens the tone of the stomach.
stomachical: Pertaining to the stomach; as stomachic vessels.
Strengthening to the stomach; exciting the action of the stomach.
stond: A stop; a post; a station.
stone: Aconcretion of some species of earth, as lime, silex, clay and the like, usually in combination with some species of air or gas, with sulphur or with a metallic substance; a hard compact body, of any form and size. In popular language, very large masses of concretions are called rocks; and very small concretions are universally called gravel or sand, or grains of sand. Stones are of various degrees of hardness and weight; they are brittle and fusible, but not malleable, ductile, or soluble in water. Stones are of great and extensive use int he construction of buildings of all kinds, for walls, fences, piers, abutments, arches, monuments, sculpture and the like. When we speak of the substance generally, we use stone in the singular; as a house or wall of stone. But when we speak of particular separate masses, we say, a stone, or the stones.
A gem; a precious stone. Inestimable stones, unvalud jewels.
Any thing made of stone; a mirror.
A calculous concretion in the kidneys or bladder; the disease arising from a calculus.
stone-blind: Blind as a stone; perfectly blind.
stone-bow: A cross bow for shooting stones.
stone-crop: A sort of tree. A plant of the genus Sedum; wall-pepper. The stone- crop tree or shrubby glass-wort is of the genus Chenopodium.
stonecutter: One whose occupation is to hew stones.
stonecutting: The business of hewing stones for walls, steps, cornices, monuments
stoned: Pelted or killed with stones; freed from stones; walled with stones.
stone-fruit: Fruit whose seeds are covered with a hard shell enveloped in the pulp, as peaches, cherries, plums; a drupe.
stone-parsley: A plant of the genus Bubon.
stone-pit: A pit or quarry where stones are dug.
stoner: One who beats or kills with stones; one who walls with stones.
stone-squarer: One who forms stones into squares. 1 Kings 5:18.
stone-still: Still as a stone; perfectly still or motionless.
stone-ware: A species of potters ware of a coarse kind, glazed and baked.
stone-work: Work or wall consisting of stone; masons work of stone.
stoniness: The quality of abounding with stones; as, the stoniness of ground renders it difficult to till .
stony: Made of stone; as a stony tower.
Consisting of stone; as a stony cave.
Full of stones; abounding with stones; as stony ground.
Petrifying; as the stony dart of senseless cold.
Hard; cruel; unrelenting; pitiless; as a stony heart.
stook: A small collection of sheaves set up in the field.
stool: A seat without a back; a little form consisting of a board with three or four legs, intended as a set for one person.
The seat used in evacuating the contents of the bowels; hence, an evacuation; a discharge from the bowels.

A sucker; a shoot from the bottom of the stem or the root of a plant. Stool of repentance, in Scotland, an elevated seat in the church, on which persons sit as a punishment for fornication and adultery.
In agriculture, to ramify; to tiller, as grain; to shoot out suckers.
stool-ball: A play in which balls are driven from stool to stool.
stoom: To put bags of herbs or other ingredients into wine, to prevent fermentation.
stoop: To bend the body downward and forward; as, to stoop to pick up a book.
To bend or lean forward; to incline forward in standing or walking. We often see men stoop in standing or walking, either from habit or from age.
To yield; to submit; to bend by compulsion; as, Carthage at length stooped to Rome.

To descend from rank or dignity; to condescend. IN modern days, attention to agriculture is not called stooping in men of property. Where men of great wealth stoop to husbandry, it multiplieth riches exceedingly.
To yield; to be inferior. These are arts, my prince, in which our Zama does not stoop to Rome.
stooper: One that bends the body forward.
stooping: Bending the body forward; yielding; submitting; condescending; inclining.
stoopingly: With a bending of the body forward.
stoor: To rise in clouds, as dust or smoke; from the Welsh ystwr, a stir.
stooter: A small silver coin in Holland, value 2 stivers.
stop: To close, as an aperture, by filling or by obstructing; as, to stop a vent; to stop the ears; to stop wells of water. 2 Kings 3:19.
To obstruct; to render impassable; as, to stop a way, road or passage.
To hinder; to impede; to arrest progress; as, to stop a passenger in the road; to stop the course of a stream.
To restrain; to hinder; to suspend; as to stop the execution of a decree.
To repress; to suppress; to restrain; as, to stop the progress of vice.
stop-cock: A pipe for letting out a fluid, stopped by a turning cock.
stoppage: The act of stopping or arresting progress or motion; or the state of being stopped; as the stoppage of the circulation of the blood; the stoppage of commerce.
stopped: Closed; obstructed; hindered from proceeding; impeded; intercepted.
stopper: One who stops, closes, shuts or hinders; that which stops or obstructs; that which closes or fills a vent or hole in a vessel.

In seamens language, a short piece of rope used for making something fast, as the anchor or cables. Stoppers are also used to prevent the running rigging from coming up, whilst the men are belaying it.
stoppered: Closed with a stopper; as a stoppered retort.
stopping: Closing; shutting; obstructing; hindering from proceeding; ceasing to go or move; putting an end to; regulating the sounds of.
stopple: That which stops or closes the mouth of a vessel; as a glass stopple; a cork stopple.
storage: The act of depositing in a store or warehouse for safe keeping; or the safe keeping of goods in a warehouse.
The price charge or paid for keeping goods in a store.
storax: A plant or tree; also, a resinous and odoriferous drug brought from Turkey, but generally adulterated. It imparts to water a yellow color, and has been deemed a resolvent. Storax is a solid balsam, either in red tears, or in large cakes, brittle, but soft to the touch, and of a reddish brown color. It is obtained from the Styrax officinalis, a tree which grows in the Levant. Liquid storax, or styrax, is a liquid or semifluid balsam, said to be obtained from the Liquidamber styraciflua, a tree which grows in Virginia. It is greenish, of an aromatic taste, and agreeable smell.
store: A large number; as a store of years.
A large quantity; great plenty; abundance; as a store of wheat or provisions.
A stock provided; a large quantity for supply; ample abundance. The troops have great stores of provisions and ammunition. The ships have stores for a long voyage.
Quantity accumulated; fund; abundance; as stores of knowledge.
A storehouse; a magazine; a warehouse. Nothing can be more convenient than the stores on Central wharf in Boston.
store-house: A building for keeping grain or goods of any kind; a magazine; a repository; a warehouse. Joseph opened all the store-houses and sold to the Egyptians. Genesis 41:56.
Arepository. The Scripture of God is a store-house abounding with inestimable treasures of wisdom and knowledge.
store-keeper: A man who has the care of a store.
storer: One who lays op or forms a store.
storied: Furnished with stories; adorned with historical paintings. Some greedy minion or imperious wife, the trophied arches, storied halls.
Related in story; told or recited in history.
storier: A relater of stories; a historian. T
stork: A large fowl of the genus Ardea or Heron kind.
storm: A violent wind; a tempest. Thus a storm of wind, is correct language, as the proper sense of the word is rushingIt has primarily no reference to a fall of rain or snow. But as a violent wind is often attended with rain or snow, the word storm has come to be used, most improperly, for a fall of rain or snow without wind. O beat those storms, and roll the seas in vain.
A violent assault on a fortified place; a furious attempt of troops to enter ad take a fortified place by scaling the walls, forcing the gates and the like.

Violent civil or political commotion; sedition; insurrection; also, clamor; tumult; disturbance of the public peace. I will stir up in England some black storms. Her sister began to scold and raise up such a storm-- .
Affliction; calamity; distress; adversity. A brave man struggling in the storms of fate.
storminess: Tempestuousness; the state of being agitated by violent winds.
stormy: Tempestuous; agitated with furious winds; boisterous; as a stormy season; a stormy day or week.
Proceeding from violent agitation or fury; as a stormy sound; stormy shocks.
story: A verbal narration or recital of a series of facts or incidents. WE observe in children a strong passion for hearing stories.
A written narrative of a series of facts or events. There is probably on record no story more interesting than that of Joseph in Genesis.
History; a written narrative or account of past transactions, whether relating to nations or individuals. The four great monarchies make the subject of ancient story.
Petty tale; relation of a single incident or of trifling incidents.
A trifling tale; a fiction; a fable; as the story of a fairy. In popular usage, story is sometimes a softer term for a lie.
story-teller: One who tells stories; a narrator of a series of incidents; as an amusing story-teller.
stound: To be in pain or sorrow.
stour: A battle or tumult. Stour, signifies a river, as in Sturbridge.
stout: Strong; lusty . A stouter champion never handled sword.
Bold; intrepid; valiant; brave. He lost the character of a bold, stout, magnanimous man.N\ .
Proud; resolute; obstinate. The lords all stand to clear their cause, most resolutely stout.
Strong; firm; as a stout vessel.
stoutly: Lustily; boldly; obstinately. He stoutly defended himself.
stove: A hot house; a house or room artificially warmed.
A small box with an iron pan, used for holding coals to warm the feet. It is a bad practice for young persons to accustom themselves to sit with a warm stove under the feet.
An iron box, with various apartments in it for cooking; a culinary utensil of various forms.
To keep warm in a house or room by artificial heat; as, to stove orange trees and myrtles.
stover: Fodder for cattle; primarily, fodder from threshed grain; but in New England, any kind of fodder from the barn or stack.
stow: To place; to put in a suitable place or position; as, to stow bags, bales or casks in a ships hold; to stow hay in a mow; to stow sheaves. The word has reference to the placing of many thing, or of one thing among many, or of a mass of things.
To lay up; to reposit. Stow in names, signifies place, as in Barstow.
stowage: The act or operation of placing in a suitable position; or the suitable disposition of several things together. The stowage of a ships cargo to advantage requires no little skill. It is of great consequence to make good stowage.
Room for the reception of things to be reposited. In every vessel there is stowage for immense treasures.
The state of being laid up. I am curious to have the plate and jewels in safe stowage.
stowed: Placed in due position or order; reposited.
stowing: Placing in due position; disposing in good order.
strabism: A squinting; the act or habit of looking asquint.
straddle: To part the legs wide; to stand or walk with the legs far apart.
To place one leg on one side and the other on the other of any thing; as, to straddle a fence or a horse.
straddling: Standing or walking with the legs far apart; placing one leg on one side and the other on the other.
straggle: To wander from the direct course or way; to rove. When troops are on the march, let not the men straggle.
To wander at large without an certain direction or object; to ramble. The wolf spied a straggling kid.
To exuberate; to shoot too far in growth. Prune the straggling branches of the hedge.
To be dispersed; to be apart from any main body. They came between Scylla and Charybdis and the straggling rocks.
straggler: A wanderer; a rover; one that departs from the direct or proper course; one that rambles without any settled direction.
A vagabond; a wandering shiftless fellow.
Something that shoots beyond the rest or too far.
straggling: Wandering; roving; rambling; being in a separate position.
straight: Right, in a mathematical sense; direct; passing from one point to another by the nearest course; not deviating or crooked; as a straight line; a straight course; a straight piece of timber.
Narrow; close; tight; as a straight garment.
Upright; according with justice and rectitude; not deviating from truth or fairness.
Immediately; directly; in the shortest time. I know thy generous temper well; fling but the appearance of dishonor on it, it straight takes fire, and mounts into a blaze.
straighten: To make straight; to reduce from a crooked to a straight form.
To make narrow, tense or close; to tighten.
To reduce to difficulties or distress.
straightly: In a right line; not crookedly.
straightness: The quality or state of being straight; rectitude.
straightway: Immediately; without loss of time; without delay. He took the damsel by the hand, and said to her, Talitha cumi--and straightway the damsel arose. Vark 5:42.
straiks: Strong plates of iron on the circumference of a cannon wheel over the joints of the fellies.
strain: To stretch; to draw with force; to extend with great effort; as, to strain a rope; to strain the shrouds of a ship; to strain the chords of an instrument.
To cause to draw with force, or with excess of exertion; to injure by pressing with too much effort. He strained this horses or his oxen by overloading them.
To stretch violently or by violent exertion; as, to strain the arm or the muscles.
To put to the utmost strength. Men in desperate cases will strain themselves for relief.
strainer: That through which any liquid passes for purification; an instrument for filtration. The lacteals of animal bodies are the strainers to separate the pure emulsion from its feces.
straining: Stretching; exerting with violence; making great efforts; filtering.
The act of stretching; the act of filtering; filtration.
strait: Narrow; close; not broad. Strait is the gate, and narrow is the way that leadeth to life, and few there be that find it. Matthew 7:14.
Close; intimate; as a strait degree of favor.
Strict; rigorous. He now, forsooth, takes on him to reform some certain edicts, and some strait decrees.
straiten: To make narrow. In narrow circuit, straitend by a foe.
To contract; to confine; as, to straiten the British commerce.
To make tense or tight; as, to straiten a cord.
To distress; to perplex; to press with poverty or other necessity; as, aman straitened in his circumstances.
To press by want of sufficient room. Waters when straitened, as at the falls of bridges, give a roaring noise.
strait-laced: Griped with stays. We have few well-shaped that are strait-laced.
straitness: Narrowness; as the straitness of a place; straitness of mind; straitness of circumstances.
Strictness; rigor; as the straitness of a man’s proceedings.
Distress; difficulty; pressure from necessity of any kind, particularly from poverty.
Want; scarcity; or rather narrowness; as the straitness of the conveniences of life.
strait-waistcoat: An apparatus to confine the limbs of a distracted person.
strait-jacket: An apparatus to confine the limbs of a distracted person.
strake: The iron band of a wheel.
stram: To spread out the limbs; to sprawl.
stramash: To strike, beat or bang; to break; to destroy.
strand: The shore or beach of the sea or ocean, or of a large lake, and perhaps of a navigable river. It is never used of the bank of a small river or pond. The Dutch on the Hudson apply it to a landing place; as the strand at Kingston.
One of the twists or parts of which a rope is composed.
To drive or run aground on the sea shore, as a ship.
To break one of the strands of a rope.
To drift or be driven on shore; to run aground; as, a ship strands at high water.
strange: Foreign; belonging to anther country. I do not contemn the knowledge of strange and divers tongues.
Not domestic; belonging to others. So she impatient her own faults to see, turns from herself, and in strange things delights.
New; not before known, heard or seen. The former custom was familiar; the latter was new and strange to them. Hence,

Wonderful; causing surprise; exciting curiosity. It is strange that men will not receive improvement, when it is shown to be improvement. Sated at length, ere long I might perceive strange alteration in me.
Odd; unusual; irregular; not according to the common way. Hes strange and peevish.
strangely: Wonderfully; in a manner or degree to excite surprise or wonder. How strangely active are the acts of peace. It would strangely delight you to see with what spirit he converses.
strangeness: Foreignness; the state of belonging to another country. If I will obey the gospel, no distance of place, no strangeness of country can make any man a = stranger to me.
Distance in behavior; reserve; coldness; forbidding manner. Will you not observe the’ strangeness of his alterd countenance?
Remoteness from common manners or notions; uncouthness. Men worthier than himself here tend the savage strangeness he puts on.
Alienation of mind; estrangement; mutual dislike. This might seem a means to continue a strangeness between two nations.

Wonderfulness; the power of exciting surprise and wonder; uncommonness' that raises wonder’ by novelty. This raised greater tumults in the hearts of men than the strangeness and seeming unreasonableness of all the former articles.
stranger: A foreigner; one who belongs to another country. Paris and London are visited by strangers from all the countries of Europe.
One of another town, city, state or province in the same country. The Commencements in American colleges are frequented by multitudes of strangers from the neighboring towns and states.
One unknown. The gentleman is a stranger to me .
One unacquainted. My child is yet a stranger to the world. I was no stranger to the original.
strangle: To choke; to suffocate; to destroy life by stopping respiration. Our Saxon ancestors compelled the adulteress to strangle herself.
To suppress; to hinder from birth or appearance.
strangling: The act of destroying life by stopping respiration.
strangulated: Compressed. A hernia or rupture is said to be strangulated, when it is So compressed as to cause dangerous symptoms.
strangulation: The act of strangling; the act of destroying life by stopping respiration; suffocation.
That kind of suffocation which is common to women in hysterics; also, the straitening or compression of the intestines in hernia.
strangury: Literally, a discharge of urine by drops; a difficulty of discharging urine, attended with pain.
strap: A long narrow slip of cloth or lether, of various forms and for various uses; as the strap of a shoe or boot; straps for fastening trunks or other baggage, for stretching limbs in surgery.
In botany, the flat part of the corollet in ligulate florets; also, an appendage to the leaf in some grasses.
To beat or chastise with a strap.
To fasten or bind with a strap.
To rub on a strap for sharpening, as a razor.
strappado: A military punishment formerly practiced. It consisted in drawing an offender to the top of a beam and letting him fall, by which means a limb was sometimes dislocated.
strapping: Drawing on a strap, as a razor.
a. Tall; lusty; as a strapping fellow.
strata: Beds; layers; as strata of sand, clay or coal.
stratagem: An artifice, particularly in war; a plan or scheme for deceiving an enemy.
An artifice; a trick by which some advantage is intended to be obtained. Those oft are stratagems which’ errors seem.
strath: A vale, bottom or low ground between hills.
stratification: The process by which substances in the earth have been formed into strata or layers.
The state of being formed into layers in the earth.
The act of laying in strata.
stratified: Formed into a layer, as a terrene substance.
stratify: To form into a layer, as substances in the earth. Thus clay, sand and other species of earth are often found stratified.
stratifying: Arranging in a layer, as terrene substances.
stratocracy: A military government; government by military chiefs and an army.
stratography: Description of armies, or what belongs to an army.
stratum: stratums or strata. The latter is most common.

In geology and mineralogy, a layer; any species of earth, sand, coal and the like, arranged in a flat form, distinct from the adjacent matter. The thicker strata are called beds; and these beds are sometimes stratified.
A bed or layer artificially made.
straw: The stalk or stem of certain species of grain, pulseChiefly of wheat, rye, oats, barley, buckwheat and peas. When used of single stalks, it admits of a pluralStraws may show which way the wind blows. We say of grain while growing, the straw is large, or it is rusty.
A mass of the stalks of certain species of grain when cut, and after being thrashed; as a bundle or a load of straw. In this sense, the word admits not the plural number.
Any thing proverbially worthless. I care not a straw for the play. I will not abate a straw.
strawberry: A plant and its fruit, of the genus Fragaria. Strawberries are of various kinds, all delicious fruit.
strawberry-tree: An evergreen tree of the genus Arbutus; the fruit is of a fleshy substance, like a strawberry.
straw-built: Constructed of straw; as the suburbs of a straw-built citadel.
straw-color: The color of dry straw; a beautiful yellowish color.
straw-colored: Of a light yellow, the color of dry straw.
straw-cutter: An instrument to cut straw for fodder.
strawy: Made of straw; consisting of straw.
stray: To wander, as from a direct course; to deviate or go out of the way. We say, to stray from the path or road into the forest or wood.
To wander from company, or from the proper limits; as, a sheep strays from the flock; a horse strays from an inclosure.
To rove; to wander from the path of duty or rectitude; to err; to deviate. We have erred and strayed-- .
To wander; to rove at large; to play free and unconfined. Lo, the glad gales oer all her beauties stray, breathe on her lips and in her bosom play.
To wander; to run a serpentine course. Where Thames among the wanton valley strays.
straying: Wandering; roving; departing from the direct course, from the proper inclosure, or from the path of duty.
streak: A line or long mark, of a different color from the ground; a stripe. What mean those colord streaks in heaven?
In a ship, a uniform range of planks on the side or bottom; sometimes pronounced strake.
To form streaks or stripes in; to stripe; to variegate with lines of a different color or of different colors. A mule admirably streaked and dappled with white and black-
streaked: Marked or variegated with stripes of a different color.
streaky: Having stripes; striped; variegated with lines of a different color.
stream: A current of water or other fluid; a liquid substance flowing in a line or course, either on the earth, as a river or brook, or from a vessel or other reservoir or fountain. Hence,
A current of water in the ocean; as the gulf stream.
Acurrent of melted metal or other substance; as a stream of lead or iron flowing from a furnace; a stream of lava from a volcano.
Any thing issuing from a source and moving with a continued succession of parts; as a stream of words; a stream of sand. A stream of beneficence.
streamer: An ensign or flag; a pennon extended or flowing in the wind; a poetic use of the word. Brave Rupert from afar appears, whose waving streamers the glad general knows.
streaming: Emitting; pouring out in abundance; as streaming eyes.
Flowing; floating loosely; as a flag.
streamlet: A small stream; a rivulet; a rill.
stream-tin: Particles or masses of tin found beneath the surface of alluvial ground.
streamy: Abounding with running water. However streamy now, adust and dry, denied the goddess water.
Flowing with a current or streak. His nodding helm emits a streamy ray.
streek: To lay out, as a dead body.
street: Properly, a paved way or road; but in usage, any way or road ina city, chiefly a main way, in distinction from a lane or alley.
Among the people of New England, any public highway.
Streets, plural, any public way, road or place. That there be no complaining in our streets. Psalm 144:174.
street-walker: A common prostitute that offers herself to sale in the streets.
street-ward: Formerly, an officer who had the care of the streets.
strength: That property or quality of an animal body by which it is enabled to move itself or other bodies. We say, a sick man has not strength to walk, or to raise his head or his arm. We say, a man has strength to lift a weight, or to draw it. This quality is called also power and force. But force is also used to denote the effect of strength exerted, or the quantity of motion. Strength in this sense, is positive, or the power of producing positive motion or action, and is opposed to weakness.

Firmness; solidity or toughness; the quality of bodies by which they sustain the application of force without breaking or yielding. Thus we speak of the strength of a bone, the strength of a beam, the strength of a wall, the strength of a rope. In this sense, strength is a passive quality, and is opposed to weakness or frangibility: .
Power or vigor of any kind. This act shall crush the strength of Satan. Strength there must be either of love or war.
Power of resisting attacks; fastness; as the strength of a castle or fort .
Support; that which supports; that which supplies strength; security; . God is our refuge and strength. Psalm 46:7.
strengthen: To make strong or stronger; to add strength to, either physical, legal or moral; as, to strengthen a limb; to strengthen an obligation.
To confirm; to establish; as, to strengthen authority.
To animate; to encourage; to fix in resolution. Charge Joshua, and encourage him, and strengthen him. Deuteronomy 3:28.
To cause to increase in power or security. Let noble warwick, Cobham and the rest, with powerful policy strengthen themselves.
To grow strong or stronger. The disease that shall destroy at length, grows with his growth, and strengthens with his strength. Grows with his growth, and strengthens with his strength.
strengthener: That which increases strength, physical or moral.
In medicine, something which, taken into the system, increases the action and energy of the’ vital powers.
strengthening: Increasing strength, physical or moral; confirming; animating.
strenuous: Eagerly pressing or urgent; zealous; ardent; as a strenuous advocate for national rights; a strenuous opposer of African slavery.
Bold and active; valiant, intrepid and ardent; as a strenuous defender of his country.
strenuously: With eager and pressing zeal; ardently.
strenuousness: Eagerness; earnestness; active zeal; ardor in pursuit of an object, or in opposition to a measure.
stress: Force; urgency; pressure; importance; that which bears with most weight; as the stress of a legal question. Consider how much stress is laid on the exercise of charity in the New Testament. This, on which the great stress of the business depends-- .
Force or violence; as stress of weather.
Force; violence; strain. Though the faculties of the mind are improved by exercise, yet they must not be put to a stress beyond their strength.
To press; to urge; to distress; to put to difficulties.
stretch: To draw out to greater length; to extend in a line; as, to stretch a cord or a rope.
To extend in breadth; as, to stretch cloth,
To spread; to expand; as, to stretch the wings.
To reach; to extend. Stretch thine hand to the poor.
To spread; to display; as, to stretch forth the heavens.
stretched: Drawn out in length; extended; exerted to the utmost.
stretcher: A piece of timber in building.
A narrow piece of plank placed across a boat for the rowers to set their feet against.
stretching: Drawing out in length; extending; spreading; exerting force.
strew: To scatter; to spread by scattering; always applied to dry substances separable into parts or particles; as, to strew seed in beds; to strew sand on or over a floor; to strew flowers over a grave.
To spread by being scattered over. The snow which does the top of Pindus strew. Is thine alone the seed that strews the plain?
To scatter loosely. And strewd his mangled limbs about the field.
strewed: Scattered; spread by scattering; as sand strewed on paper.
Covered or sprinkled with something scattered; as a floor strewed with sand.
strewing: The act of scattering or spreading over.
Any thing fit to be strewed.
striae: In natural history, small channels in the shells of cockles and in other substances.
striate: In botany, streaked; marked or scored with superficial or very slender lines; marked with fine parallel lines. Striated fracture, in mineralogy, consists of long narrow separable parts laid on or beside each other.
striated: In botany, streaked; marked or scored with superficial or very slender lines; marked with fine parallel lines. Striated fracture, in mineralogy, consists of long narrow separable parts laid on or beside each other.
stricken: Struck; smitten; as the stricken deer.
Advanced; worn; far gone. Abraham was old and well stricken in age. Genesis 24:1.
strickle: A strike; an instrument to strike grain to a level with the measure.
strict: Strained; drawn close; tight; as a strict embrace; a strict ligature.
Tense; not relaxed; as a strict or lax fiber.
Exact; accurate; rigorously nice; as, to keep strict watch. Observe the strictest rules of virtue and decorum.
Severe; rigorous; governed or governing by exact rules; observing exact rules; as, the father is very strict in observing the sabbath. The master is very strict with his apprentices.
Rigorous; not mild or indulgent; as strict laws.
strictly: Exactly; with nice accuracy; as, patriotism strictly so called, is a noble virtue.
Positively. He commanded his son strictly to proceed no further.
Rigorously; severely; without remission or indulgence. Examine thyself strictly whether thou didst not best at first.
strictness: Exactness in the observance of rules, laws, rites and the like; rigorous accuracy; nice regularity or precision. I could not grant too much or distrust too little to men that pretended singular piety and religious strictness.
Rigor; severity! These commissioners proceeded with such strictness and severity as did much obscure the kings mercy.
stricture: A stroke; a glance; a touch.
A touch of criticism; critical remark; censure. / . I have given myself the liberty of these strictures by way of reflection, on every passage.
A drawing; a spasmodic or other morbid contraction of any passage of the body.
stride: A long step. Her voice theatrically loud, and masculine her stride.
To walk with long steps. Mars in the middle of the shining shield is gravd, and strides along the field.
To pass over at a step. See him stride valleys wide.
striding: Walking with long steps; passing over at a step.
stridor: A harsh creaking noise, or a crack.
stridulous: Making a small harsh sound or a creaking.
strife: Exertion or contention for superiority; contest of emulation, either by intellectual or physical efforts. Strife may be carried on between students or between mechanics. Thus Gods contended, noble strife, who most should ease the wants of life.

Contention in anger or enmity; contest; struggle for victory; quarrel or war. I and my people were at great strife with the children of Ammon. Judges 12:2. These vows thus granted, raisd a strife above betwixt the god of war and queen of love.
Opposition; contrariety; contrast. Artificial strife lives in these touches livelier than life.
The agitation produced by different qualities; as the strife of acid and alkali.
strifeful: Contentious; discordant. The ape was strifeful and ambitious and the fox guileful and most covetous.
strigment: Scraping; that which is scraped off.
strigous: In botany, a strigous leaf is one set with stiff lanceolate bristles.
strike: pret. struck; pp. struck and stricken; but struck is in the most common use. Strook is wholly obsolete.

To touch or hit with some force, either with the hand or an instrument; to give a blow to, either with the open hand, the fist, a stick, club or whip, or with a pointed instrument, or with a ball or an arrow discharged. An arrow struck the shield; a ball strikes a ship between wind and water. He at Philippi kept his sword een like a dancer, while I struck the lean and wrinkled Cassius.
To dash; to throw with a quick motion. They shall take of the blood, and strike it on the two side-posts. Exodus 12:7.
To stamp; to impress; to coin; as, to strike coin at the mint; to strike dollars or sovereigns; also, to print; as, to strike five hundred copies of a book.
To thrust in; to cause to enter or penetrate; as, a tree strikes its root deep.
strike-block: A plane shorter than a jointer, used for shooting a short joint.
striker: One that strikes, or that which strikes.
In Scripture, a quarrelsome man. Titus 1:7.
striking: Hitting with a blow; impressing; imprinting; punishing; lowering, as sails or a mast.
a. Affecting with strong emotions; surprising; forcible; impressive; as a striking representation or image.
Strong; exact; adapted to make impression; as a striking resemblance of features.
strikingly: In such a manner as to affect or surprise; forcibly; strongly; impressively.
strikingness: The quality of affecting or surprising.
string: Asmall rope, line or cord, or a slender strip of lether or other like substance, used for fastening or tying things.
Aribin. Round Ormonds knee thou tyst the mystic string.
A thread on which any thing is filed; and hence, a line of things; as a string of shells or beads.
The chord of a musical instrument, as of a harpsichord, harp or violin; as an instrument of ten strings.
A fiber, as of a plant. Duck weed putteth forth a little string into the water, from the bottom.
stringed: Having strings; as a stringed instrument.
Produced by strings; as stringed noise.
stringent: for astringent, binding, is not in use.
stringhalt: A sudden twitching of the hinder leg of a horse, or an involuntary or convulsive motion of the muscles that extend or bend the hough.
stringing: Furnishing with strings; putting in tune; filling; making tense; depriving of strings.
stringless: Having no strings. His tongue is now a stringless instrument.
stringy: Consisting of strings or small threads; fibrous; filamentous; as a stringy root.
Ropy; viscid; gluey; that may be drawn into a thread.
strip: To pull or tear off, as a covering; as, to strip the skin from a beast; to strip the bark from a tree; to strip the clothes from a man’s back.
To deprive of a covering; to skin; to peel; as, to strip a beast of his skin; to strip a tree of its bark; to strip a man of his clothes.
To deprive; to bereave; to make destitute; as, to strip a man of his possessions.
To divest; as, to strip one of his rights and privileges. Let us strip this subject of all its adventitious glare.
To rob; to plunder; as, robbers strip a house.
stripe: A line or long narrow division of any thing, of a different color from the ground as a stripe of red on a green ground; hence, any linear variation of color.
A strip or long narrow piece attached to something of a different color; as a long stripe sewed upon a garment.
The weal or long narrow mark discolored by a lash or rod.
A stroke made with a lash, whip, rod, strap or scourge. Forty stripes may he give him, and not exceed. Deuteronomy 25:3.
Affliction; punishment; sufferings. By his stripes are we healed. Isaiah 53:5.
striped: Formed with lines of different colors.
a. Having stripes of different colors.
stripling: A youth in the state of adolescence, or just passing from boyhood to manhood; a lad. And the king said, inquire thou whose son the stripling is. 7 Samuel 17:56.
stripped: Pulled or torn off; peeled; skinned; deprived; divested; made naked; impoverished; husked, as maiz.
stripping: Pulling off; peeling; skinning; flaying; depriving; divesting; husking.
strippings: The last milk drawn from a cow at a milking.
strive: To make efforts; to use exertions; to endeavor with earnestness; to labor hard; applicable to exertions of body or mind. A workman strives to perform his task before another; a student strives to excel his fellows in improvement. Was it for this that his ambition strove to equal Cesar first, and after Jove? Strive with me in your prayers to God for me. Romans 15:30. Strive to enter in at the strait gate. Luke 13:24.

To contend; to contest; to struggle in opposition to another; to be in contention or dispute; followed by against or with before the person or thing opposed; as, strive against temptation; strive for the truth. My spirit shall not always strive with man. Genesis 6:3.
To oppose by contrariety of qualities. Now private pity strove with public hate, reason with rage, and eloquence with fate.
To vie; to be comparable to; to emulate; to contend in excellence. Not that sweet grove of Daphne by Orontes, and the inspird Castalian spring, might with this paradise of Eden strive.
striver: One that strives or contends; one who makes efforts of body or mind.
striving: Making efforts; exerting the power of body or mind with earnestness; contending.
The act of making efforts; contest; contention. Avoid foolish questions and genealogies and contentions, and strivings about the law. Titus 3:9.
strobil: In botany, a pericarp formed from an ament by the hardening of the scales. It is made up of scales that are imbricate, from an ament contracted or squeezed together in this states of maturity, as the cone of the pine.
strobiliform: Shaped like a strobil, as a spike.
strocal: An instrument used by glass-makers to empty the metal from one pot to another.
strokal: An instrument used by glass-makers to empty the metal from one pot to another.
stroke: A blow; the striking of one body against another; applicable to a club or to any heavy body, or to a rod, whip or lash. A piece of timber falling may kill a man by its stroke; a man when whipped, can hardly fail to flinch or wince at every stroke. Th oars were silver, which to the time of flutes kept stroke-- .
A hostile blow or attack. He entered and won the whole kingdom of Naples without striking a stroke.
A sudden attack of disease or affliction; calamity. At this one stroke the man lookd dead in law.
Fatal attack; as the stroke of death.
stroker: One who strokes; one who pretends to cure by stroking.
strokesman: In rowing, the man who rows the aftmost oar, and whose stroke is to be followed by the rest.
stroll: To rove; to wander on foot; to ramble idly or leisurely. These mothers stroll to beg sustenance for their helpless infants.
A wandering on foot; a walking idly and leisurely.
stroller: One who strolls; a vagabond; a vagrant.
strombite: A petrified shell of the genus Strombus.
strong: Having physical active power, or great physical power; having the power of exerting great bodily force; vigorous. A patient is recovering from sickness, but is not yet strong enough to walk. A strong man will lift twice his own weight. That our oxen may be strong to labor. Psalm 144:14. Orses the strong to greater strength must yield.
Having physical passive power; having ability to bear or endure; firm; solid; as a constitution strong enough to bear the fatigues of a campaign.
Well fortified; able to sustain attacks; not easily subdued or taken; as a strong fortress or town.
Having great military or naval force; powerful; as a strong army or fleet; a strong nation; a nation strong at sea.
Having great wealth, means or resources; as a strong house or company of merchants.
strong-hand: Violence; force; power. It was their meaning to take what they needed by strong-hand.
strong-hold: A fastness; a fort; a fortified place; a place of security.
strongly: With strength; with great force or power; forcibly; a word of extensive application.
Firmly; in a manner to resist attack; as a town strongly fortified.
Vehemently; forcibly; eagerly. The evils of this measure were strongly represented to the government.
strontian: An earth which, when pure and dry, is perfectly white, and resembles baryte in many of its properties. It is a compound of oxygen and a base to which is given the name strontium, in the proportion of 16 per cent. of the former, to 84 per cent. of the latter.
strontianite: Carbonate of strontian, a mineral that occurs massive, fibrous, stellated, and crystalized in the form of a hexahedral prism, modified on the edges, or terminated by a pyramid. Prismatic baryte, a species of heavy spar.:
strop: A strap. This orthography is particularly used for a strip of lether used for sharpening razors and giving them a fine smooth edge; a razor-strop. But strap is preferable.
A piece of rope spliced into a circular wreath, and put round a block for hanging it.
strophe: In Greek poetry, a stanza; the first member of a poem. This is succeeded by a similar stanza called antistrophy.
strophy: In Greek poetry, a stanza; the first member of a poem. This is succeeded by a similar stanza called antistrophy.
strow: is only a different orthography of strew.
strowl: for stroll, is not in use.
stroy: for destroy, is not in use.
strucken: the old pp. of strike, is obsolete.
structure: Act of building; practice of erecting buildings. His son builds on and never is content, till the last farthing is in structure spent.
Manner of building; form; make; construction; as the want of insight into the structure and constitution of the terraqueous globe.
Manner of organization of animals and vegetables.

A building of any kind, but chiefly a building of some size or of magnificence; an edifice. The iron bridge over the Seine in Paris, is a beautiful structure. There stands a structure of majestic frame.
In mineralogy, the particular arrangement of the integrant particles or molecules of a mineral.
struggle: Properly, to strive, or to make efforts with a twisting or with contortions of the body. Hence,
To use great efforts; to labor hard; to strive; to contend; as, to struggle to save life; to struggle with the waves; to struggle against the stream; to struggle with adversity.
To labor in pain or anguish; to be in agony; to labor in any kind of difficulty or distress. Tis wisdom to beware, and better shun the bait than struggle in the snare.
Great labor; forcible effort to obtain an object, or to avoid an evil; properly, a violent effort with contortions of the body.
Contest; contention; strife. An honest man might look upon the struggle’ with indifference.
struggler: One who struggles, strives or contends.
struggling: Making great efforts; using violent exertions; affected with contortions.
The act of striving; vehement or earnest effort.
struma: A glandular swelling; scrofula; the kings evil; a wen.
strumous: Having swelling sin the glands; scrofulous.
strut: To walk with a lofty proud gait and erect head; to walk with affected dignity. Does he not hold up his head and strut in his gait?
To swell; to protuberate. The bellying canvas strutted with the gale.
A lofty proud step or walk with the head erect; affectation of dignity in walking.
struthious: Pertaining to or like the ostrich.
strutting: Walking with a lofty gait and erect head.
The act of walking with a proud gait.
struttingly: With a proud lofty step; boastingly.
strychnia: An alkaline substance obtained from the fruit of the Strychnos nux vomica, and Strychnos ignatia. It is a white substance, crystalized in very small four sided prisms, and intolerably bitter. It acts upon the stomach with violent energy, inducing locked jaw and destroying life.
stub: The stump of a tree; that part of the stem of a tree which remains fixed int he earth when the tree is cut down.
To grub up by the roots; to extirpate; as, to stub up edible roots.
To strike the toes against a stump, stone or other fixed object.
stubbed: Short and thick like something truncated; blunt; obtuse.
stubble: The stumps of wheat, rye, barley, oats or buckwheat, left in the ground; the part of the stalk left by the sythe or sickle. After the first crop is off, they plow in the stubble.
stubble-rake: A rake with long teeth for raking together stubble.
stubborn: Unreasonably obstinate; inflexibly fixed in opinion; not to be moved or persuaded by reasons; inflexible; as a stubborn son; a stubborn mind or soul. The queen is obstinate--stubborn to justice.
Persevering; persisting; steady; constant; as stubborn attention.
Stiff; not flexible; as a stubborn bow. Take a plant of stubborn oak.
Hardy; firm; enduring without complaint; as stubborn Stoics.
stubbornness: Perverse and unreasonable obstinacy; inflexibility; contumacy. Stubbornness and obstinate disobedience must be mastered with blows.
stubby: Short and thick; short and strong; as stubby bristles.
stub-nail: A nail broken off; a short thick nail.
stucco: A fine plaster composed of lime, sand, whiting and pounded marble; used for covering walls.
To plaster; to overlay with fine plaster.
stuck: and pp. of stick. Stuck oer with titles, and hung round with strings.
stuckle: A number of sheaves set together in the field.
stud: In building, a small piece of timber or joist inserted in the sills and beams, between the posts, to support he beams or other main timbers. The boards on the outside and the laths on the inside of a building, are also nailed to the studs.

A nail with a large head, inserted in work chiefly for ornament; an ornamental knob. A belt of straw, and ivy buds, with coral clasps and amber studs. Crystal and myrrhine cups, embossd with gems and studs of pearl.
A collection of breeding horses and mares; or the place where they are kept. In the studs of Ireland, where care is taken, we see horses bred of excellent shape, vigor and fire.
A button for a shirt sleeve.
To adorn with shining studs or knobs. Their horses shall be trappd, their harness studded all with gold and pearl.
studded: Set with detached ornaments. The sloping sides and summits of our hills, and the extensive plains that stretch before our view, are studded with substantial, neat and commodious dwelling of freemen.
studding: Setting or adorning with studs or shining knobs.
studding-sail: In navigation, a sail that is set beyond the skirts of the principal sails. The studding-sails are set only when the wind is light. They appear like wings upon the yard-arms.
student: A person engaged in study; one who is devoted to learning, either in a seminary or in private; a scholar; as the students of an academy, of a college or university; a medical student; a law student.
A man devoted to books; a bookish man; as a hard student; a close student. Keep a gamester from dice, and a good student from his books.
One who studies or examines; as a student of natures works.
stud-horse: A breeding horse; a horse kept for propagating his kind.
studied: Read; closely examined; read with diligence and attention; well considered. The book has been studied. The subject has been well studied. 2.a. Learned; well versed in any branch of learning; qualified by study; as a man well studied in geometry, or in law or medical science.
studier: One who studies; a student. Lipsius was a great studier in the stoical philosophy.
studious: Given to books or to learning; devoted to the acquisition of knowledge from books; as a studious scholar.
Contemplative; given to thought, or to the examination of subjects by contemplation.
Diligent; eager to discover something, or to effect some object; as, be studious to please; studious to find new friends and allies.
Attentive to; careful; with of. Divines must become studious of pious and venerable antiquity: .
Planned with study; deliberate. For the frigid villany of studious lewdness, for the calm malignity of labored impiety, what apology can be invented?
studiously: With study; with close attention to books.
studiousness: The habit or practice of study; addictedness to books. Men of sprightly imagination are not generally the most remarkable for studiousness.
study: Literally, a setting of the mind or thoughts upon a subject; hence, application of mind of books, to arts or science, or to any subject, for the purpose of learning what is not before known. Hammond generally spent thirteen hours of the day in study. Study gives strength to the mind; conversation.
Attention; meditation; contrivance. Just men they seemd, and all their study bent to worship God aright and know his works.
Any particular branch of learning that is studied. Let your studies be directed by some learned and judicious friend.
Subject of attention. The Holy Scriptures, especially the New Testament, are her daily study.
A building or an apartment devoted to study or to literary employment.
stuff: A mass of matter, indefinitely; or a collection of substances; as a heap of dust, of chips or of dross.

The matter of which any thing is formed; materials. The carpenter and joiner speak of the stuff with which they build; mechanics pride themselves on having their wares made of good stuff. Time is the stuff which life is made of. Degrading prose explains his meaning ill, and shows the stuff, and not the workmans skill. Cesar hath wept; ambition should be made of sterner stuff.
Furniture; goods; domestic vessels in general. He took away locks, and gave away the kings stuff.
That which fills any thing. Cleanse the suffd bosom of that perilous stuff that weighs upon the heart.
Essence; elemental part; as the stuff of the conscience.
stuffing: That which is used for filling any thing; as the stuffing of a saddle or cushion.
Seasoning for meat; that which is put into meat to give it a higher relish.
stulm: A shaft to draw water out of a mine.
stultify: To make foolish; to make one a fool.
In law, to alledge or prove to be insane, for avoiding some act.
stum: New wine used to raise fermentation in dead or vapid wines.
Wine revived by a new fermentation.
To renew wine by mixing must with it, and raising a new fermentation. We stum our wines to renew their spirits.
stumble: To trip in walking or moving in any way upon the legs; to strike the foot so as to fall, or to endanger a fall; applied to any animal. A man may stumble, as well as a horse. The way of the wicked is as darkness; they know not at what they stumble. Proverbs 4:19.
To err; to slide into a crime or an error. He that loveth his brother, abideth in the light, and there is none occasion of stumbling in him. 7 John 2:10.
To strike upon without design; to fall on; to light on by chance. Men often stumble upon valuable discoveries. Ovid stumbled by some inadvertence upon Livia in a bath.
To obstruct in progress; to cause to trip or stop.
To confound; to puzzle; to put to a nonplus; to perplex. One thing more stumbles me in the very foundation of this hypothesis.
stumbler: One that stumbles or makes a blunder.
stumbling-block: Any cause of stumbling; that which causes to err. We preach Christ crucified, to the Jews a stumbling-block, and to the Greeks foolishness. 7 Corinthians 1:23. STUMBLING:
stumbling-stone: Any cause of stumbling; that which causes to err. We preach Christ crucified, to the Jews a stumbling-block, and to the Greeks foolishness. 7 Corinthians 1:23. STUMBLING:
stump: The stub of a tree; the part of a tree remaining int he earth after the tree is cut down, or the part of any plant left in the earth by the sythe or sickle.
The part of a limb or other body remaining after a part is amputated or destroyed; as the stump of a leg, of a finger or a tooth.
To strike any thing fixed and hard with the toe.
stun: To make senseless or dizzy with a blow on the head; as, to be stunned by a fall, or by a falling timber. One hung a pole-ax at his saddle bow, and one a heavy mace to stun the foe.
To overpower the sense of hearing; to blunt or stupefy the organs of hearing. To prevent being stunned, cannoneers sometimes fill their ears with wool.
To confound or make dizzy by loud and mingled sound. --An universal hubbub wild of stunning sounds and voices all confusd.
stunned: Having the sense of hearing overpowered; confounded with noise.
stunning: Overpowering the organs of hearing; confounding with noise.
stunt: To hinder from growth; applied to animals and plants; as, to stunt a child; to stunt a plant.
stupe: Cloth or flax dipped in warm medicaments and applied to a hurt or sore; fomentation; sweating bath.
stupefaction: A stupid or senseless state; insensibility; dullness; torpor; stupidity. Resistance of the dictates of conscience brings a hardness and stupefaction upon it.
stupefactive: Causing insensibility; deadening or blunting the sense of felling or understanding; narcotic. Opium hath a stupefactive part.
stupefier: That which causes dullness or stupidity.
stupefy: To make stupid; to make dull; to blunt the faculty of perception or understanding; to deprive of sensibility. It is a great sin to attempt to stupefy the conscience. The fumes of passion intoxicate his discerning faculties, as the fumes of drink stupefy the brain.
To deprive of material motion. It is not malleable nor fluent, but stupefied.
stupefying: Rendering extremely dull or insensible; as the stupefying virtues of opium.
stupendous: Literally, striking dumb by its magnitude; hence, astonishing; wonderful; amazing; particularly, of astonishing magnitude or elevation; as a stupendous pile; a stupendous edifice; a stupendous mountain; a stupendous bridge.
stupendously: In a manner to excite astonishment.
stupendousness: The quality or state of being stupendous or astonishing.
stupid: Very dull; insensible; senseless; wanting in understanding; heavy; sluggish. O that men should be so stupid grown, as to forsake the living God. With wild surprise, a moment stupid, motionless he stood.
Dull; heavy; formed without skill or genius. Observe what loads of stupid rhymes oppress us in corrupted times.
stupidity: Extreme dullness of perception or understanding; insensibility; sluggishness.
stupidly: With extreme dullness; with suspension or inactivity of understanding; sottishly; absurdly; without the exercise of reason or judgment.
stupor: Great diminution or suspension of sensibility; suppression of sense; numbness; as the stupor of a limb.
Intellectual insensibility; moral stupidity; heedlessness or inattention to ones interests.
stupration: Rape; violation of chastity by force.
sturdiness: Stoutness; hardiness; as the sturdiness of a school boy./\ .
sturdy: Hardy; stout; foolishly obstinate; implying coarseness or rudeness. This must be done, and I would fain see mortal so sturdy as to gainsay. A sturdy hardened sinner advances to the utmost pitch of impiety with less reluctance than he took the first step.
Strong; forcible; lusty; as a sturdy lout.
Violent; laid on with strength; as sturdy strokes.
Stiff; stout; strong; as a sturdy oak. He was not of a delicate contexture, his limbs rather sturdy than dainty.
sturgeon: A large fish of the genus Acipenser, caught in large rivers. Its flesh is valued for food.
stutter: To stammer; to hesitate in uttering words.
sty: A place of bestial debauchery. To roll with pleasure in a sensual sty: .
An inflamed tumor on the edge of the eyelid.
To shut up in a sty.
styca: A Saxon copper coin of the lowest value.
stygian: Pertaining to Styx, fabled by the ancients to be a river of hell over which the shades of the dead passed, or the region of the dead; hence, hellish; infernal. At that so sudden blaze, the Stygian throng bent their aspect.
style: Manner of writing with regard to language, or the choice and arrangement of words; as a harsh style; a dry style; a tumid or bombastic style; a loose style; a terse style; a laconic or verbose style; a flowing style; a lofty style; an elegant style; an epistolary style. The character of style depends chiefly on a happy selection and arrangement of words. Proper words in proper places, make the true definition of style. Let some lord but own the happy lines, how the wit brightens and the style refines!

Manner of speaking appropriate to particular characters; or in general, the character of the language used. Not style is held for base, where love well named is. According to the usual style of dedications. So we Say, a person addresses another in a style of haughtiness, in a style or rebuke.
Mode of painting; any manner of painting which is characteristic or peculiar. The ornamental style also possesses its own peculiar merit.
A particular character of music; as a grave style.
Title; appellation; as the style of majesty. Propitious hear our prayr, whether the style of Titan please thee more-- .
styliform: Like a style, pin or pen.
stylite: In ecclesiastical history, the Stylites were a sect of solitaries, who stood motionless on columns or pillars for the exercise of their patience.
styloid: Having some resemblance to a style or pen; as the styloid process of the temporal bone.
styptic: That stops bleeding; having the quality of restraining hemorrhage.

A medicine which has the quality of stopping hemorrhage or discharges of blood. Styptics have the quality of astringents, but the word styptic is used in a sense different from that of astringent, and much more limited. Styptics are usually external applications for restraining discharges of blood; astringents are usually internal applications for stopping bleeding, or for strengthening the solids. Astringent is the general term; styptic a subdivision of it.
styptical: That stops bleeding; having the quality of restraining hemorrhage.
stypticity: The quality of stanching blood, or stopping hemorrhage.
suability: Liability to be sued; the state of being subject by law to civil process.
suable: That may be sued; subject by law to be called to answer in court.
suade: for persuade, is not in use.
suage: for assuage, is not in use.
suant: Even; uniform; spread equally over the surface.
suasible: That may be persuaded or easily persuaded.
suasory: Tending to persuade; having the quality of convincing and drawing by argument or reason.
suavity: Sweetness, in a figurative sense; that which is to the mind what sweetness is to the tongue; agreeableness; softness; pleasantness; as suavity of manners; suavity of language, conversation or address.
sub: a Latin preposition, denoting under or below, used in English as a prefix, to express a subordinate degree. Before f and p it is changed into those letters, as in suffer, and suppose; and before m, into that letter, as in summon.
subacid: Moderately acid or sour; as a subacid juice.
subaction: The act of reducing to any state, as of mixing two bodies completely, or of beating them to a powder.
subah: In India, a province or viceroy ship.
subahdar: In India, a viceroy, or the governor of a province; also, a native of India, who ranks as captain in the European companies.
subaltern: Inferior; subordinate; that in different respects is both superior and inferior; as a subaltern officer. It is used chiefly of military officers.
A subordinate officer in an army or military body. It is applied to officers below the rank of captain.
subaquatic: Being under water, or beneath the surface of water.
subaqueous: Being under water, or beneath the surface of water.
subastral: Beneath the stars or heavens; terrestrial.
subaxillary: Placed under the axil or angle formed by the branch of a plant with the stem, or by a leaf with the branch.
sub-brigadier: An officer in the horse guards, who ranks as cornet.
subcarbureted: Carbureted in an inferior degree; or consisting of one prime of carbon and two of hydrogen.
sub-celestial: Being beneath the heavens; as sub-celestial glories.
sub-chanter: An under chanter; a deputy of the precentor of a cathedral.
subclavian: Situated under the clavicle or collar bone; as the subclavian arteries.
sub-committee: An under committee; a part or division of a committee.
sub-contrary: Contrary in an inferior degree. In geometry, when two similar triangles are so placed as to have a common angle at their vertex, and yet their bases not parallel.
subcordate: 2. In shape somewhat like a heart.
subcostal: 2. The subcostal muscles are the internal intercostal muscles.
subcuticular: Being under the cuticle or scarf-skin.
subdeacon: An under deacon; a deacons servant, in the Romish church.
subdeaconry: The order and office of subdeacon in the catholic church.
subdeaconship: The order and office of subdeacon in the catholic church.
subdean: An under dean; a deans substitute or vicegerent.
subdeanery: The office and rank of subdean.
subdeposit: That which is deposited beneath something else.
subdititious: Put secretly in the place of something else.
subdiversify: To diversify again what is already diversified.
subdivide: To divide a part of a thing into more parts; to part into smaller divisions. In the rise of eight in tones, are two half tones; so as if you divide the tones equally, the eight is but seven whole and equal notes; and if you subdivide that into half notes, as in the stops of a lute, it makes the number thirteen. The progenies of Cham and Japhet swarmed into colonies, and those colonies were subdivided into many others-- .
subdivided: Divided again or into smaller parts.
subdividing: Dividing into smaller parts that which is already divided.
subdivision: The act of subdividing or separating a part into smaller parts.
The part of a thing made by subdividing; the part of a larger part. In the decimal table, the subdivision s of the cubit, as span, palm, and digit, are deduced from the’ shorter cubit.
subdominant: In music, the fourth note above the tonic, being under the dominant.
subduce: To withdraw; to take away. Or from my side subducting, took perhaps more than enough.
To subtract by arithmetical operation. If out of that infinite multitude of antecedent generations we should subduct ten-- .
subduct: To withdraw; to take away. Or from my side subducting, took perhaps more than enough.
To subtract by arithmetical operation. If out of that infinite multitude of antecedent generations we should subduct ten-- .
subduction: The act of taking away or withdrawing.
subdue: To conquer by force or the exertion of superior power, and bring into permanent subjection; to reduce under dominion. Thus Cesar subdued the Gauls; Augustus subdued Egypt; the English subdued Canada. Subduing implies conquest or vanquishing, but it implies also more permanence of subjection to the conquering power, than either of these words. I will subdue all thine enemies. 7 Chronicles 17:10.

To oppress; to crush; to sink; to overpower so as to disable from further resistance. Nothing could have subdud nature to such a lowness, but his unkind daughters. If aught were worthy to subdue the soul of man.
To tame; to break by conquering a refractory temper or evil passions; to render submissive; as, to subdue a stubborn child.
To conquer; to reduce to mildness; as, to subdue the temper or passions.
To overcome by persuasion or other mild means; as, to subdue opposition by argument or intreaties.
subdued: Conquered and reduced to subjection; oppressed; crushed; tamed; softened.
subduer: One who conquers and brings into subjection; a tamer.
That which subdues or destroys the force of.&
subduing: Vanquishing and reducing to subjection; crushing; destroying the power of resistance; softening.
subduplicate: Having the ratio of the square roots.
suberate: A salt formed by the suberic acid in combination with a base.
suberic: Pertaining to cork, or extracted from it; as suberic acid.
suberose: In botany, having the appearance of being gnawed; appearing as if a little eaten or gnawed.
subglobular: Having a form approaching to globular.
subhastation: A public sale or auction, so called form the Roman practice.
subhydrosulphuret: A compound of sulphurated hydrogen with a base, in a less proportion than in hydrosulphuret.
subindication: The act of indication by signs.
subinfeudation: In law, the act of enfeoffing by a tenant or feoffee, who holds lands of the crown; the act of a greater baron, who grants land or a smaller manor to an inferior person. By 34 Edward Il all subinfeudations previous to the reign of king Edward I., were confirmed.
Under tenancy. The widow is immediate tenant to the heir, by a kind of subinfeudation or under tenancy.
subjacent: Being in a lower situation, though not directly beneath. A man placed on a hill, surveys the subjacent plain.
subject: adjective
Placed or situate under. --The eastern tower whose height commands, as subject, all the vale, to see the fight.
Being under the power and dominion of another; as, Jamaica is subject to Great Britain. Esau was never subject to Jacob.
Exposed; liable from extraneous causes; as a country subject to extreme heat or cold.
Liable from inherent causes; prone; disposed. All human things are subject to decay.
Being that on which nay thing operates, whether intellectual or material; as the subject-matter of a discourse.
subjected: Reduced to the dominion of another; enslaved; exposed; submitted; made to undergo.
subjection: The act of subduing; the act of vanquishing and bringing under the dominion of another. The conquest of the kingdom and the subjection of the rebels-
subjective: Relating to the subject, as opposed to the object. Certainty--is distinguished into objective and subjective; objective, is when the proposition is certainly true of itself; and subjective, is when we are certain of the truth of it.
subjoin: To add at the end; to add after something else has been said or written; as, to subjoin an argument or reason.
subjoined: Added after something else said or written.
subjoining: Adding after something else said or written.
subjugate: To subdue and bring under the yoke of power or dominion; to conquer by force and compel to submit to the government or absolute control of another. He subjugated a king, and called him his’ vassal.
subjugated: Reduced to the absolute control of another.
subjugating: Conquering and bringing under the absolute power of another.
subjugation: The act of subduing and bringing under the power or absolute control of another.
subjunction: The act of subjoining, or state of being subjoined.
subjunctive: Subjoined or added to something before said or written.

In grammar, designating a form of verbs which follow other verbs or words expressing condition, hypothesis or contingency; as, veni ut me videas, I came that you may see me; Si fecerint aequum, if they should do what is just.
Subjunctive is often used as a noun, denoting the subjunctive mode.
sublapsarian: Done after the apostasy of Adam.

One who maintains the sublapsarian doctrine, that the sin of Adams apostasy being imputed to all his posterity, God in compassion decreed to send his Son to rescue a great number from their lost state, and to accept of his obedience and death on their account. The decree of reprobation, according to the sublapsarians, is nothing but a preterition or non-election of persons, whom God left as he found, involved in the guilt of Adams transgression without any personal sin, when he withdrew some others as guilty as they. Sublapsarian is opposed to supralapsarian.
sublapsary: Done after the apostasy of Adam.
sublation: The act of taking or carrying away.
sublet: To underlet; to lease, as a lessee to another person.
sublevation: The act of raising on high.
sublieutenant: An officer in the royal regiment of artillery and fusileers, in which are no ensigns, and who is the same as second lieutenant.
sublimable: That may be sublimated; capable of being raised by heat into vapor, and again condensed by cold.
sublimate: To bring a solid substance, as camphor or sulphur, into the state of vapor by heat, which on cooling, returns again to the solid state. [See Sublimation.I .
To refine and exalt; to highthen; to elevate.& And as his actions rose, so raise they still their vein, in words whose weight best suits a sublimated strain.

The product of a sublimation. Corrosive sublimate is the muriate of mercury when it has undergone sublimation. It is one of the most virulent of the mineral poisons. Blue sublimate, is a preparation of mercury with flower of brimstone and sal ammoniac; used in painting.
Brought into a state of vapor by heat and again condensed, as solid substances.
sublimated: Brought into a state of vapor by heat, as a solid substance; refined.
sublimating: Converting into the state of vapor by heat, and condensing; as solid substances.
sublimation: The operation of bringing a solid substance into the state of vapor by heat, and condensing it again into a solid by cold. Sublimation bears the same relation to a solid, that distillation does to a liquid. Both processes purify the substances to which they are severally applied, by separating them from the fixed and grosser matters with which they are connected.
Exaltation; elevation; act of highthening or improving. Religion, the perfection, refinement and sublimation of morality.
sublime: High in place; exalted aloft. Sublime on these a tow’r of steel is rear’d.
High in excellence; exalted by nature; elevated. Can it be that souls sublime . Return to visit our terrestrial clime?
High in style or sentiment; lofty; grand. Easy in style thy work, in sense sublime.
Elevated by joy; as sublime with expectation.
Lofty of mein; elevated in manner. His fair large front and eye sublime declard . Absolute rule.
sublimed: Brought into a state of vapor by heat, and when cooled, changed to a solid state.
sublimely: With elevated conceptions; loftily; as, to express one’s self sublimely. In English lays, and all sublimely great, Thy Homer charms with all his ancient heat.
sublimeness: Loftiness of style or sentiment; sublimity.
sublimity: Highth in excellence; loftiness of nature or character; moral grandeur; as God’s incomprehensible — sublimity. The sublimity of the character of Christ owes nothing to his historians.

In oratory and composition, lofty conceptions, or such conceptions expressed in corresponding language; loftiness of sentiment or style. Milton’s distinguishing excellence lies in the sublimity of his thoughts.
sublingual: Situated under the tongue; as the sublingual glands.
sublunar: Literally, beneath the moon, but sublunary, which is the word chiefly used, denotes merely terrestrial, earthly, pertaining to this world. All things sublunary are subject to change.
sublunary: Literally, beneath the moon, but sublunary, which is the word chiefly used, denotes merely terrestrial, earthly, pertaining to this world. All things sublunary are subject to change.
subluxation: In surgery, a violent sprain; also, an incomplete dislocation.
submarine: Being, acting or growing under water in the sea; as submarine navigators; submarine plants.
submaxillary: Situated under the jaw. The submaxillary glands are two salivatory glands, situated one on either side, immediately within the angle of the lower jaw.
submediant: In music, the sixth note, or middle note between the octave and subdominant.
submerge: To put under water; to plunge.
To cover or overflow with water; to drown. So half my Egypt was submerg’d.
submerj’. To plunge under water, as swallows.
submerse: submers’. Being or growing under water, as the leaves of aquatic plants.
submersed: submers’. Being or growing under water, as the leaves of aquatic plants.
submersion: The act of putting under water or causing to be overflowed; as the submersion of an isle or tract of land.
The act of plunging under water; the act of drowning.
subminister: To subserve; to be useful to. Our passions--subminister to the best and worst of purposes. [See Minister and Administer.:!
subministration: The act of furnishing or supplying.
submission: The act of submitting; the act of yielding to power or authority; surrender of the person and power to the control or government of another. Submission, dauphin! ‘tis a mere French word; . We English warriors wot not what it means.
Acknowledgment of inferiority or dependence; humble or suppliant behavior. In all submission and humility, York doth present himself unto your highness.
Acknowledgment of a fault; confession or error. Be not as extreme in submission, as in offense.
Obedience; compliance with the commands or laws of a superior. Submission of children to their parents is an indispensable duty.
Resignation; a yielding of one’s will to the will or appointment of a superior without murmuring. Entire and cheerful submission to the will of God is a christian duty of prime excellence.
submissive: Yielding to the will or power of another; obedient.
Humble; acknowledging one’s inferiority; testifying one’s submission. Her at his feet submissive in distress, He thus with peaceful words uprais’d.
submissively: With submission; with acknowledgment of inferiority; humbly. The goddess, Soft in her tone, submissively replies.
submissiveness: Confession of fault. Frailty gets pardon by submissiveness.
submit: To let down; to cause to sink or lower. Sometimes the hill submits itself a while.

To yield, resign or surrender to the power, will or authority of another; with the reciprocal pronoun. Return to thy mistress, and submit thyself under her hand. Genesis 16:9. Wives, submit yourselves to your own husbands. Ephesians 5:22. Submit yourselves to every ordinance of man. 7 Peter 2:13.
To refer; to leave or commit to the discretion of judgment of another; as, to submit a controversy to arbitrators; to submit a question to the court.
To surrender; to yield one’s person to the power of another; to give up resistance. The enemy submitted. The revolted provinces presently submitted.
To yield one’s opinion to the opinion or authority of another. On hearing the opinion of the court, the counsel submitted without further argument.
submitting: Surrendering; resigning; yielding; referring to another for decision.
submultiple: A number or quantity which is contained in another a certain number of times, or is an aliquot part of it. Thus 7 is the submultiple of 56, being contained in it eight times. The word is used as an adjective also; as a submultiple number; submultiple ratio.
subnect: To tie, buckle or fasten beneath.
subnormal: A subperpendicular, or a line under the perpendicular to a curve.
subnude: In botany, almost naked or bare of leaves.
suboccipital: Being under the occiput; as the suboccipital nerves.
suborbicular: Almost orbiculate or orbicular; nearly circular.
suborbiculate: Almost orbiculate or orbicular; nearly circular.
subordinacy: The state of being subordinate or subject to control; as, to bring the imagination to act in subordinacy to reason.
subordinate: Inferior in order, in nature, in dignity, in power, importance; as subordinate officers. It was subordinate, not enslaved, to the understanding.
Descending in a regular series. The several kinds and subordinate species of each, are easily distinguished.
To place in order or rank below something else; to make or consider as of less value or importance; as, to subordinate one creature to another; to subordinate temporal to spiritual things.
To make subject; as, to subordinate the passions to reason.
subordinated: Placed in an inferior rank; considered as of inferior importance; subjected.
