subordinately: In a lower rank or of inferior importance.
subordination: The state of being inferior to another; inferiority of rank or dignity: .
Aseries regularly descending. Natural creatures having a local subordination-- .
Place of rank among inferiors. --Persons, who in their several subordinations would be obliged to follow the example of their superiors.

Subjection; state of being under control or government. The most glorious military achievements would be a calamity and a curse, if purchased at the expense of habits of subordination and love of order.
suborn: In law, to procure a person to take such a false oath as constitutes perjury.
To procure privately or by collusion. Or else thou art suborn’d against his honor.
To procure by indirect means. Those who by despair suborn their death.
subornation: In law, the crime of procuring a person to take such a false oath as constitutes perjury.
The crime of procuring one to do a criminal or bad action.
suborned: Procured to take a false oath, or to do a bad action.
suborner: One who procures another to take a false oath, or to do a bad action.
suborning: Procuring one to take a false oath, or to do a criminal action.
subovate: Almost ovate; nearly in the form of an egg.
subpena: A writ commanding the attendance in court of the person on whom it is served; as witnesses.
To serve with a writ of subpena; to command attendance in court by a legal writ.
subpetiolate: In botany, having a very short petiole.
subprior: The vicegerent of a prior; a claustral officer who assists the prior.
subpurchaser: A purchaser who buys of a purchaser.
subquadruple: Containing one part of four; as subquadruple proportion.
subquintuple: Containing one part of five; as subquintuple proportion.
subreption: The act of obtaining a favor by surprise or unfair representation, that is, by suppression or fraudulent concealment of facts.
subrogate: To put in the place of another.
subrogation: In the civil law, the substituting of one person in the place of another and giving him his rights.
subsalt: A salt with less acid than is sufficient to neutralize its radicals; or a salt having an excess of the base.
subscapular: The subscapular artery is the large branch of the axillary artery, which rises near the lowest margin of the scapula.
subscribe: To sign with one’s own hand; to give consent to something written, or to bind one’s self by writing one’s name beneath; as, parties subscribe a covenant or contract; a man subscribes a bond or articles of agreement.
To attest by writing one’s name beneath; as, officers subscribe their official acts; and secretaries and clerks subscribe copies of records.
To promise to give by writing one’s name; as, each man subscribed ten dollars or ten shillings.
To promise to give a certain sum by setting one’s name to a paper. The paper was offered and many subscribed.
subscribed: Having a name or names written underneath. The petition is subscribed by two thousand persons.
Promised by writing the name and sum.' . A large sum is subscribed.
subscriber: One who subscribes; one who contributes to an undertaking by subscribing.
One who enters his name for a paper, book, map and the like.
subscribing: Writing one’s name underneath; assenting to or attesting by writing the name beneath; entering one’s name as a purchaser.
subscription: Any thing, particularly a paper, with names subscribed.
The act of subscribing or writing one’s name underneath; name subscribed; signature.
Consent or attestation given by underwriting the name.W\ .
The act of contributing to any undertaking.
Sum subscribed; amount of sums subscribed. We speak of an individual subscription, or of the whole subscription to a fund.
subsection: The part or division of a section; a subdivision; the section of a section.
subsecutive: Following in a train’ or succession.
subsemitone: In music, the sharp seventh or sensible of any key.
subsequence: A following; a state of coming after something.
subsequent: Following in time; coming or being after something else at any time, indefinitely; as subsequent events; subsequent ages or years; a period long subsequent to the foundation of Rome.
Following in the order of place or succession; succeeding; as a subsequent clause in a treaty. What is obscure in a passage may be illustrated by subsequent words.
subsequently: At a later time; in time after something else. Nothing was done at the first meeting; what was subsequently transacted, I do not know.
After something else in order. These difficulties will be subsequently explained.
subserve: subserv. To serve in subordination; to serve instrumentally. In most engines, we make the laws of matter subserve the purposes of art. Not made to rules . But to subserve where wisdom bears command.
subservience: Instrumental use; use or operation that promotes some purpose. --The body, wherein appears much fitness, use and subserviency to infinite functions. There is a regular subordination and subserviency among all the parts to beneficial ends.
subserviency: Instrumental use; use or operation that promotes some purpose. --The body, wherein appears much fitness, use and subserviency to infinite functions. There is a regular subordination and subserviency among all the parts to beneficial ends.
subservient: Useful as an instrument to promote a purpose; serving to promote some end. Hammond had an incredible dexterity, scarcely ever reading any thing which he did not make subservient in one kind or other.
Subordinate; acting as a subordinate instrument. These are the creatures of God, subordinate to him, and subservient to his will. These ranks of creatures are subservient one to another.
subsessile: In botany, almost sessile; having very short footstalks.
subside: To sink or fall to the bottom; to settle; as lees.
To fall into a state of quiet; to cease to rage; to be calmed; to become tranquil. Let the passions subside. The tumults of war will subside. Christ commanded, and the storm subsided.
To tend downwards; to sink; as a subsiding hill. The land subsides into a plain.
To abate; to be reduced. In cases of danger, pride and envy naturally subside.
subsidence: The act or process of sinking or falling, as the lees of liquors.
The act of sinking or gradually descending, as ground.
subsidency: The act or process of sinking or falling, as the lees of liquors.
The act of sinking or gradually descending, as ground.
subsidiary: Aiding; assistant; furnishing help. Subsidiary troops are troops of one nation hired by another for military service.
Furnishing additional supplies; as a subsidiary stream.
An assistant; an auxiliary; he or that which contributes aid or additional supplies.
subsidize: To furnish with a subsidy; to purchase the assistance of another by the payment of a subsidy to him. Great Britain subsidized some of the German powers in the late war with France.
subsidized: Engaged as an auxiliary by means of a subsidy.
subsidizing: Purchasing the assistance of by subsidies.
subsidy: Aid in money; supply given; a tax; something furnished for aid, as by the people to their prince; as the subsidies granted formerly to the kings of England. Subsidies were a tax, not immediately on property, but on persons in respect of their reputed estates, after the nominal rate of 4s. the pound for lands, and 2s. 8d. for goods.

A sum of money paid by one prince or nation to another, to purchase the service of auxiliary troops, or the aid of such foreign prince in a war against an enemy. Thus Great Britain paid subsidies to Austria and Prussia, to engage them to resist the progress of the French.
subsign: subsi’ne. To sign under; to write beneath.
subsignation: The act of writing the name under something for attestation.
subsist: To be; to have existence; applicable to matter or spirit.
To continue; to retain the present state. Firm we subsist, but possible to swerve.
To live; to be maintained with food and clothing. How many of the human race subsist on the labors of others! How many armies have subsisted on plunder!
To inhere; to have existence by means of something else; as qualities that subsist in substances.
To feed; to maintain; to support with provisions. The king subsisted his troops on provisions plundered from the enemy.
subsistence: Real being; as a chain of differing subsistencies. Not only the things had subsistence, but the very images were of some creatures existing.
Competent provisions; means of supporting life. His viceroy could only propose to himself a comfortable subsistence out of the plunder of his province.
That which supplies the means of living; as money, pay or wages.
Inherence in something else, as the subsistence of qualities in bodies.
subsistency: Real being; as a chain of differing subsistencies. Not only the things had subsistence, but the very images were of some creatures existing.
Competent provisions; means of supporting life. His viceroy could only propose to himself a comfortable subsistence out of the plunder of his province.
That which supplies the means of living; as money, pay or wages.
Inherence in something else, as the subsistence of qualities in bodies.
subsistent: Having real being; as a subsistent spirit,
Inherent; as qualities subsistent in matter. SUBSIISTENT.
subsoil: The bed or stratum of earth which lies between the surface soil and the base on which they rest.
subspecies: A subordinate species; a division of a species.
substance: In a general sense, being; something existing by itself; that which really is or exists; equally applicable to matter or spirit. Thus the soul of man is called an immaterial substance, a cogitative substance, a substance endued with thought. We say, a stone is a hard substance, tallow is a soft substance.
That which supports accidents. That which subsists by itself is called substance; that which subsists in and by another, is called a mode or manner of being.
The essential part; the main or material part. In this epitome, we have the substance of the whole book. This edition is the same in substance with the Latin.
Something real, not imaginary; something solid, not empty. Heroic virtue did his actions guide, And he the substance, not th’ appearance chose.
Body; corporeal nature or matter. The qualities of plants are more various than those of animal substances.
substantial: Belonging to substance; real; actually existing. If this atheist would have his chance to be a real and substantial agent, he is more stupid than the vulgar.
Real; solid; true; not seeming or imaginary. If happiness be a substantial good. The substantial ornaments of virtue.
Corporeal; material. The rainbow appears like a substantial arch in the sky.
Having substance; strong; stout; solid; as substantial cloth; a substantial fence or gate.
Possessed of goods or estate; responsible; moderately wealthy; as a substantial freeholder or farmer; a substantial citizen.
substantiality: Corporeity; materiality. The soul is a stranger to such gross substantiality.
substantially: In the manner of a substance; with reality of existence. In him his Father shone, substantially express’d .
Truly; solidly; really . The laws of this religion would make men, if they would truly observe them substantially religious towards God, chaste and temperate.
In substance; in the main; essentially.€ This answer is substantially the same as that before given.
substantialness: Firmness; strength; power of holding or lasting; as the substantialness of a wall or column.
substantiate: To establish by proof or competent evidence; to verify; to make good, as, to substantiate a charge or allegation; to substantiate a declaration.
substantive: Betokening existence; as the substantive verb.
In grammar, a noun or name; the part of speech which expresses something that exists, either material or immaterial. Thus man, horse, city, goodness, excellence, are substantives.
substantively: In grammar, as a name or noun. An adjective or pronoun may be used substantively.
substile: The line of a dial on which the stile is erected.
substitute: To put in the place of another. Some few verses are inserted or substituted in the room of others.

One person put in the place of another to answer the same purpose. A person may be a substitute with full powers to act for another in an office. Representatives in legislation are the substitutes of their constituents. The orthodox creed of christians is that Christ dies as the substitute of sinners.
One thing put in the place of another. If you have not one medicine, use another as its substitute.
substitution: The act of putting one person or thing in the place of another to supply its place; as the substitution of an agent, attorney or representative to act for one in his absence; the substitution of bank notes for gold and silver, as a circulating medium.
In grammar, syllepsis, or the use of one word for another.
substract: To subtract. Note.--Substract was formerly used in analogy with abstract. But in modern usage, it is written according to the LatinSee this word and its derivatives.
substraction: In law, the withdrawing or withholding of some right. Thus the substraction of conjugal rights, is when either the husband or wife withdraws from the other and lives separate. The substraction of a legacy, is the withholding or detaining of it from the legatee by the executor. In like manner, the withholding of any service, rent, duty or custom, is a substraction, for which the law gives a remedy.
substratum: That which is laid or spread under; a layer of earth lying under another. In agriculture, the subsoil.
In metaphysics, the matter or substance supposed to furnish the basis in which the’ perceptible qualities inhere.
substylar: In dialing, the substylar line, is a right line on which the gnomon or style is erected at right angles with the plane.
substyle: In dialing, the line on which the gnomon stands.
subsulphate: A sulphate with an excess of the base.
subsultive: Bounding; leaping; moving by sudden leaps or starts, or by twitches.
subsultory: Bounding; leaping; moving by sudden leaps or starts, or by twitches.
subsultorily: In a bounding manner; by leaps, starts or twitches.
subsultus: In medicine, a twitching or convulsive motion; as subsultus tendinum.
subsume: To assume as a position by consequence.
subtangent: In geometry, the part of the axis contained between the ordinate and tangent drawn to the same point in a curve.
subtend: To extend under; as the line of a triangle which subtends the right angle; to subtend the chord of an arch. A line from the eye to a planet, subtends an angle of 40 degrees with the horizon.
subtense: subtens’. The chord of an arch or arc.
subterfuge: Literally, that to which a person resorts for escape or concealment; hence, a shift; an evasion; an artifice employed to escape censure or the force of an argument, or to justify opinions or conduct. Affect not little shifts and subterfuges, to avoid the force of an argument.
subterrane: A cave or room under ground.
subterranean: Being or lying under the surface of the earth; situated within the earth or under ground; as subterranean springs; a subterraneous passage.
subterraneous: Being or lying under the surface of the earth; situated within the earth or under ground; as subterranean springs; a subterraneous passage.
subtil: Thin; not dense or gross; as subtil air; subtil vapor; a subtil medium.
Nice; fine; delicate. I do distinguish plain . Each subtil line of her immortal face.
Sly; artful; cunning; crafty; insinuating; as a subtil person; a subtil adversary.
Planned by art; deceitful; as a subtil scheme.
subtiliation: The act of making thin or rare.
subtilization: The act of making subtil, fine or thin. In the laboratory, the operation of making so volatile as to rise in steam or vapor.
subtilize: To make thin or fine; to make less gross or coarse.
To refine; to spin into niceties; as, to subtilize arguments.
To refine in argument; to make very nice distinctions. In whatever manner the papist might subtilize---\ .
subtilly: Finely; not grossly or thickly. The opakest bodies, if subtilly divided--become perfectly transparent.
Artfully; cunningly; craftily; as a scheme subtilly contrived.
subtilness: Thinness; rareness; as the subtilness of air.
Fineness; acuteness; as the subtilness of an argument.
Cunning; artfulness; as the subtilness of a foe.
subtilty: Thinness; fineness; exility; in a physical sense; as the subtilty of air or light; the subtilty of sounds.
Refinement; extreme acuteness. Intelligible discourses are spoiled by too much subtilty in nice divisions.
Slyness in design; cunning; artifice; usually but less properly written subtlety.
subtle: Sly in design; artful; cunning; insinuating; applied to persons; as a subtle foe.
Cunningly devised; as a subtle stratagem.
subtly: Slyly; artfully; cunningly. Thou seest how subtly to detain thee I devise.
Nicely; delicately. In the nice bee, what sense so subtly true.
subtract: To withdraw or take a part from the rest; to deduct. Subtract 5 from 9, and the remainder is 4.
subtracter: The number to be taken from a larger number. J .
subtraction: The act or operation of taking a part from the rest.
In arithmetic, the taking of a lesser number from a greater of the same kind or denomination; an operation by which is found the difference between two sums.
subtractive: Tending or having power to subtract.
subtrahend: In arithmetic, the sum or number to be subtracted or taken from another.
subtriple: Containing a third or one part of three.
subtriplicate: In the ratio of the cubes.
subulate: In botany, shaped like an awl; awl-shaped. A subulate leaf, is linear at the bottom, but gradually tapering towards the end.
suburb: A building without the walls of a city, but near them; or more generally, the parts that lie without the walls, but in the vicinity of a city. The word may signify buildings, streets or territory. We say, a house stands in the suburbs; a garden is situated in the suburbs of London or Paris.
suburbs: A building without the walls of a city, but near them; or more generally, the parts that lie without the walls, but in the vicinity of a city. The word may signify buildings, streets or territory. We say, a house stands in the suburbs; a garden is situated in the suburbs of London or Paris.
suburban: Inhabiting or being in the suburbs of a city.
suburbed: Bordering on a suburb; having a suburb on its out part.
suburbicarian: Being in the suburbs; an epithet applied to the provinces of Italy which composed the ancient diocese of Rome.
suburbicary: Being in the suburbs; an epithet applied to the provinces of Italy which composed the ancient diocese of Rome.
subvariety: A subordinate variety, or division of a variety.
subvention: The act of coming to relief; support; aid.
subversion: Entire overthrow; an overthrow of the foundation; utter ruin; as the subversion of a government or state; the subversion of despotic power; the subversion of the constitution or laws; the subversion of an empire.
subversive: Tending to subvert; having a tendency to overthrow and ruin. Every immorality is subversive of private happiness. Public corruption of morals is subversive of public happiness.
subvert: To overthrow from the foundation; to overturn; to ruin utterly. The northern nations of Europe subverted the Roman empire. He is the worst enemy of man, who endeavors to subvert the christian religion. The elevation of corrupt men to office will slowly, but surely, subvert a republican government. This would subvert the principles of all knowledge.
To corrupt; to confound; to pervert the mind, and turn it from the truth. 2 Timothy 2:14.
succedaneous: Supplying the place of something else; being or employed as a substitute.
succedaneum: That which is used for something else; a substitute.
succede: The first is the more analogical spelling, as in concede

To follow in order; to take the place which another has left; as, the king’s eldest son succeeds his father on the throne. John Adams succeeded Gen. Washington in the presidency of the United States. Lewis XVIII of France has lately deceased, and is succeeded by his brother Charles X.
To follow; to come after; to be subsequent or consequent. Those destructive effects succeeded the curse.&
To prosper; to make successful. Succeed my wish, and second my design.
succeed: The first is the more analogical spelling, as in concede

To follow in order; to take the place which another has left; as, the king’s eldest son succeeds his father on the throne. John Adams succeeded Gen. Washington in the presidency of the United States. Lewis XVIII of France has lately deceased, and is succeeded by his brother Charles X.
To follow; to come after; to be subsequent or consequent. Those destructive effects succeeded the curse.&
To prosper; to make successful. Succeed my wish, and second my design.
To follow in order. Not another comfort like to this, Succeeds in unknown fate.
suceeded: Followed in order; prospered; attended with success.
succeeder: One that follows or comes in the place of another; a successor.
succeeding: Following in order; subsequent; coming after; as in all succeeding ages. He attended to the business in every succeeding stage of its progress.
Taking the place of another who has quitted the place, or is dead; as a son succeeding his father; an officer succeeding his predecessor.
The act or state of prospering or having success. There is a good prospect of his succeeding.
success: The favorable or prosperous termination of any thing attempted; a termination which answers the purpose intended; properly in a good sense, but often in a bad sense. Or teach with more success her son, The vices of the time to shun. Every reasonable man cannot but wish me success in this attempt. Be not discouraged in a laudable undertaking at the ill success of the first attempt. Military successes, above all others, elevate the minds of a people.
Succession. [Note. Success without an epithet, generally means a prosperous issue.; .
successful: Terminating in accomplishing what is wished or intended; having the desired effect; hence, in a good sense, prosperous; fortunate; happy; as a successful application of medicine; a successful experiment in chimistry or in agriculture; a successful enterprise.
In a bad sense; as a successful attempt to subvert the constitution.
successfully: With a favorable termination of what is attempted; prosperously; favorably. A reformation successfully carried on-- .
succession: A following of things in order; consecution; series of things following one another, either in time or place. Thus we speak of a succession of events in chronology, a succession of kings or bishops, and a succession of words or sentences.

The act of succeeding or coming in the place of another; as, this happened after the succession of that prince to the throne. So we speak of the succession of heirs to the estates of their ancestors, or collateral succession.
Lineage; an order or series of descendants. A long succession must ensue.

The power or right of coming to the inheritance of ancestors. He holds the property by the title of succession. What people is so void of common sense, To vote succession from a native prince? Succession of crops, in agriculture, is more generally called rotation.
successive: Following in order or uninterrupted course, as a series of persons or things, and either in time or place; as the successive revolutions of years or ages; the successive kings of Egypt. The author holds this strain of declamation through seven successive pages or chapters. Send the successive ills through ages down.
Inherited by succession; as a successive title; a successive empire.
successively: In a series or order, one following another. He left three sons, who all reigned successively. The whiteness at length changed successively into blue, indigo and violet .
successless: Having no success; unprosperous; unfortunate; failing to accomplish what was intended. Successless all her soft caresses prove. Best temper’d steel successless prov’d in field .
successor: One that succeeds or follows; one that takes the place which another has left, and sustains the like part or character; correlative to predecessor; as the successor of a deceased king; the successor of a president or governor; a man’s son and successor. A gift to a corporation, either of lands or of chattels, without naming their successors, vests an absolute property in them so long as the corporation subsists.
succinate: A salt formed by the succinic acid and a base.
succinated: Impregnated with the acid of amber.
succinct: Tucked up; girded up; drawn up to permit the legs to be free. Hib habit fit for speed succinct.
Compressed into a narrow compass; short; brief; concise; as a succinct account of the proceedings of the council. Let all your precepts be succinct and clear.
succinctly: Briefly; concisely. The facts were succinctly stated.
succinctness: Brevity; conciseness; as the succinctness of a narration.
succinic: Pertaining to amber; drawn from amber; as the succinic acid.
succinite: A mineral of an amber color, considered as a variety of garnet. It frequently occurs in globular or granular masses, about the size of a pea.
succor: Literally, to run to, or run to support; hence, to help or relieve when in difficulty, want or distress; to assist and deliver from suffering; as, to succor a besieged city; to succor prisoners. He is able to succor them that are tempted. Hebrews 2:18.
Aid; help; assistance; particularly, assistance that relieves and delivers from difficulty, want or distress. My father . Flying for succor to his servant Banister-- .
The person or thing that brings relief. The city when pressed received succors from an unexpected quarter. The mighty succor which made glad the foe.
succorer: He that affords relief; a helper; a deliverer.
succory: Wild endive, a plant of the genus Cichorium.
succotash: In America, a mixture of green maiz and beans boiled. The dish, as well as the name, is borrowed from the native Indians.
succulence: Juiciness; as the succulence of a peach.
succulency: Juiciness; as the succulence of a peach.
succulent: Full of juice; juicy. Succulent plants are such as have a juicy and soft stem, as distinguished from such as are ligneous, hard and dry. Thus the grasses are succulent herbs, as are peas, beans and the like.
succumb: To yield; to submit; as, to succumb to a foreign power.
To yield; to sink unresistingly; as, to succumb under calamities.
succussion: The act of shaking; a shake.
In medicine, a shaking of the nervous parts by powerful stimulants.
such: Of that kind; of the like kind. We never saw such a day; we have never had such a time as the present. It has as before the thing to which it relates. Give your children such precepts as tend to make them wiser and better. It is to be noted that the definitive adjective a, never precedes such, but is placed between it and the noun to which it refers; as such a man; such an honor.
The same that. This was the state of the kingdom at such time as the enemy landed.
The same as what has been mentioned. That thou art happy, owe to God; . That thou continu’st such, owe to thyself.
Referring to what has been specified. I have commanded my servant to be at such a place.
Such and such, is used in reference to a person or place of a certain kind. The sovereign authority may enact a law, commanding such and such an action.
suck: To draw with the mouth; to draw out, as a liquid from a cask, or milk from the breast; to draw into the mouth. To suck is to exhaust the air of the mouth or of a tube; the fluid then rushes into the mouth or tube by means of the pressure of the surrounding air.
To draw milk from with the mouth; as, the young of an animal sucks the mother or dam, or the breast.
To draw into the mouth; to imbibe; as, to suck in air; to suck the juice of plants.
To draw or drain. Old ocean suck’d through the porous globe.
To draw in, as a whirlpool; to absorb.
sucked: Drawn with the mouth, or with an instrument that exhausts the air; imbibed; absorbed.
sucker: He or that which draws with the mouth.
The embolus or piston of a pump.
A pipe through which any thing is drawn.
The shoot of a plant from the roots or lower part of the stem; so called perhaps from its drawing its nourishment from the root or stem.
A fish, called also remora; also, a name of the Cyclopterus or lump-fish.
sucking: Drawing with the mouth or with an instrument; imbibing; absorbing.
sucking-bottle: A bottle to be filled with milk for infants to suck instead of the pap.
suckle: To give suck to; to nurse at the breast. Romulus and Remus are fabled to have been suckled by a wolf.
suckling: A young child or animal nursed at the breast. Psalm 8:2.
suction: The act of sucking or drawing into the mouth, as fluids.
The act of drawing, as fluids into a pipe or other thing.
sudak: A fish, a species of Perca.
sudatory: A hot house; a sweating bath.
sudden: Happening without previous notice; coming unexpectedly, or without the common preparatives. And sudden fear troubleth thee. Job 22:10. For when they shall say, peace and safety, then sudden destruction cometh upon them. 7 Thessalonians 5:3.
An unexpected occurrence; surprise. On a sudden, sooner than was expected; without the usual preparatives. How art thou lost, how on a sudden lost!
suddenly: In an unexpected manner; unexpectedly; hastily; without preparation. Therefore his calamity shall come suddenly. Proverbs 6:15.
suddenness: State of being sudden; a coming or happening without previous notice. The suddenness of the event precluded preparation.
sudorific: sweat; exciting perspiration; as sudorific herbs.
A medicine that produces sweat or sensible perspiration.
suds: Water impregnated with soap. To be in the suds, to be in turmoil or difficulty; a familiar phrase.
sue: To seek justice or right from one by legal process; to institute process in law against one; to prosecute in a civil action for the recovery of a real or supposed right; as, to sue one for debt; to sue one for damages in trespass. Matthew 5:40.
To clean the beak, as a hawk; a term of falconry. To sue out, to petition for and take out; or to apply for and obtain; as, to sue out a writ in chancery; to sue out a pardon for a criminal.
To prosecute; to make legal claim; to seek for in law; as, to sue for damages.
suet: The fat of an animal, particularly that about the kidneys; lard.
suety: Consisting of suet, or resembling it; as a suety substance.
suffer: To feel or bear what is painful, disagreeable or distressing, either to the body or mind; to undergo. We suffer pain of body; we suffer grief of mind. The criminal suffers punishment; the sinner suffers the pangs of conscience in this life, and is condemned to suffer the wrath of an offended God. We often suffer wrong; we suffer abuse; we suffer injustice.
To endure; to support; to sustain; not to sink under. Our spirit and strength entire, Strongly to suffer and support our pains.

To allow; to permit; not to forbid or hinder. Will you suffer yourself to be insulted? I suffer them to enter and possess. Thou shalt in any wise rebuke thy neighbor, and not suffer sin upon him. Leviticus 19:17.
To undergo; to be affected by. Substances suffer an entire change by the action of fire, or by entering into new combinations.
To sustain; to be affected by; as, to suffer loss or damage.
sufferable: That may be tolerated or permitted; allowable.
That may be endured or borne.
sufferably: Tolerably; so as to be endured.
sufferance: The bearing of pain; endurance; pain endured; misery. He must not only die, But thy unkindness’ shall the death draw out . To ling’ring sufferance.
Patience; moderation; a bearing with patience. But hasty heat temp’ring with sufferance wise.

Toleration; permission; allowance; negative consent by not forbidding or hindering. In process of time, sometimes by sufferance, sometimes by special leave and favor, they erected to themselves oratories. In their beginning, they are weak and wan, But soon through sufferance grow to fearful end. An estate at sufferance, in law, is where a person comes into possession of land by lawful title, but keeps it after the title ceases, without positive leave of the owner.
sufferer: One who endures or undergoes pain, either of body or mind; one who sustains inconvenience or loss; as suffers by poverty or sickness. Men are sufferers by fire or losses at sea; they are sufferers by the ravages of an enemy; still more are they sufferers by their own vices and follies.
suffering: Bearing; undergoing pain, inconvenience or damage; permitting; allowing.
The bearing of pain, inconvenience or loss; pain endured; distress, loss or injury incurred; as sufferings by pain or sorrow; sufferings by want or by wrongs.
suffice: suffi'ze. To satisfy; to content; to be equal to the wants or demands of.
suffi’ze. To be enough or sufficient; to be equal to the end proposed. To recount Almighty works . What words or tongue of seraph can suffice?

suffi’ze. To satisfy; to content; to be equal to the wants or demands of. Let it suffice thee; speak no more to me of this matter. Deuteronomy 3:26. Lord, show us the Father, and it sufficeth us. John 14:8 Ruth 2:14.
To afford; to supply. The pow’r appeas’d, with wind suffic’d the sail.
sufficiency: The state of being adequate to the end proposed. His sufficiency is such, that he bestows and possesses, his plenty being unexhausted.
Qualification for any purpose. I am not so confident of my own sufficiency as not willingly to admit the counsel of others.
Competence; adequate substance or means. An elegant sufficiency.
Supply equal to wants; ample stock or fund.
Ability; adequate power. Our sufficiency is of God.2 Corinthians 3:5.
sufficient: Enough; equal to the end proposed; adequate to wants; competent; as provision sufficient for the family; water sufficient for the voyage; an army sufficient to defend the country. My grace is sufficient for thee.2 Corinthians 12:9.
Qualified; competent; possessing adequate talents or accomplishments; as a man sufficient for an office.
Fit; able; of competent power or ability. Who is sufficient for these things? 2 Corinthians 2:16.
sufficiently: To a sufficient degree; enough; to a degree that answers the purpose, or gives content; as, we are sufficiently supplied with food and clothing; a man sufficiently qualified for the discharge of his official duties.
sufficing: suffi’zing. Supplying what is needed; satisfying.
suffix: A letter or syllable added or annexed to the end of a word.
To add or annex a letter or syllable to a word.
suffixed: Added to the end of a word.
suffixing: Adding to the end of a word.
sufflation: The act of blowing up or inflating.
suffocate: To choke or kill by stopping respiration. Respiration may be stopped by the interception of air, as in hanging and strangling, or by the introduction of smoke, dust or mephitic air into the lungs. Men may be suffocated by the halter; or men may be suffocated in smoke or in carbonic acid gas, as in mines and wells. And let not hemp his windpipe suffocate.
To stifle; to destroy; to extinguish; as, to suffocate fire or live coals. A swelling discontent is apt to suffocate and strangle without passage.
suffocatingly: So as to suffocate; as suffocatingly hot.
suffocation: The act of choking or stifling; a stopping of respiration, either by intercepting the passage of air to and from the lungs, or by inhaling smoke, dust or air that is not respirable.
The act of stifling, destroying or extinguishing.
suffocative: Tending or able to choke or stifle; as suffocative catarrhs.
suffragan: A bishop, considered as an assistant to his metropolitan; or rather, an assistant bishop. By Hen. VIII. suffragans are to be denominated from some principal place in the diocese of the prelate whom they are to assist.
suffragant: An assistant; a favorer; one who concurs with.
suffragator: One who assists or favors by his vote.
suffrage: A vote; a voice given in deciding a controverted question, or in the choice of a man for an office or trust. Nothing can be more grateful to a good man than to be elevated to office by the unbiased suffrages of free enlightened citizens. Lactantius and St. Austin confirm by their suffrages the observation made by heathen writers.
United voice of persons in public prayer.
suffraginous: Pertaining to the knee joint of a beast.
suffruticous: In botany, under-shrubby, or part shrubby; permanent or woody at the base, but the yearly branches decaying; as sage, thyme, hyssop.
suffumigate: To apply fumes or smoke to the internal parts of the body, as in medicine.
suffumigation: Fumigation; the operation of smoking any thing, or rather of applying fumes to the internal parts of the body.
A term applied to all medicines that are received into the body in the form of fumes.
suffuse: suffi’ze. To overspread, as with a fluid or tincture; as eyes suffused with tears; cheeks suffused with blushes. When purple light shall next suffuse the skies.
suffused: Overspread, as with a fluid or with color.
suffusion: The act or operation of overspreading, as with a fluid or with a color.
The state of being suffused or spread over. To those that have the jaundice or like suffusion of eyes, objects appear of that color.
That which is suffused or spread over.
sugar: A well known substance manufactured chiefly from the sugar cane, arundo saccharifera; but in the United States, great quantities of this article are made from the sugar maple; and in France, a few years since, it was extensively manufactured from the beet. The saccharine liquor is concentrated by boiling, which expels the water; lime is added to neutralize the acid that is usually present; the gresser impurities rise to the surface, and are separated in the form of scum; and finally as the liquor cools, the sugar separates from the melasses in grains. The sirup or melasses is drained off, leaving the sugar in the state known in commerce by the name of raw or muscovado sugar. This is farther purified by means of clay, or more extensively by bullocks’ blood, which forming a coagulum, envelops the impurities. Thus clarified, it takes the names of lump, loaf, refinedaccording to the differen
A chimical term; as the sugar of lead.
SHUGAR. To impregnate, season, cover, sprinkle or mix with sugar.
To sweeten. But flattery still in sugar’d words betrays. Sugar of lead, acetate of lead.
sugar-candy: Sugar clarified and concreted or crystallized, in which state it becomes transparent.
sugar-cane: The cane or plant from whose juice sugar is obtained.
sugar-house: A building in which sugar is refined.
sugar-loaf: A conical mass of refined sugar.
sugar-mill: A machine for pressing out the juice of the sugar cane.
sugar-mite: A winged insect; lepisma. The lepisma saccharina, is an apterous or wingless insect, covered with silvery scales.
sugar-plum: A species of sweetmeat in small balls.
sugary: Tinctured or sweetened with sugar; sweet; tasting like sugar.
Fond of sugar, or of sweet things.
suggest: To hint; to intimate or mention in the first instance; as, to suggest a new mode of cultivation; to suggest a different scheme or measure; to suggest a new idea.
To offer to the mind or thoughts. Some ideas are suggested to the mind by all the ways of sensation and reflection.
To seduce; to draw to ill by insinuation. Knowing that tender youth is soon suggested.
To inform secretly. We must suggest the people.
suggestion: A hint; a first intimation, proposal or mention. The measure was adopted at the suggestion of an eminent philosopher.
Presentation of an idea to the mind; as the suggestions of fancy or imagination; the suggestions of conscience.
suggilation: A black and blue mark; a blow; a bruise.
suicidal: Partaking of the crime of suicide.
suicide: Self-murder; the act of designedly destroying one’s own life. To constitute suicide, the person must be of years of discretion and of sound mind.
One guilty of self-murder; a felo de se.
suicism: for suicide, is not in use.
suing: The process of soaking through any thing.
suit: Literally, a following; and so used in the old English statutes.
Consecution; succession; series; regular order; as the same kind and suit of weather.

A set; a number of things used together, and in a degree necessary to be united, in order to answer the purpose; as a suit of curtains; a suit of armor; sometimes with less dependence of the particular parts on each other, but still united in use; as a suit of clothes; a suit of apartments.
A set of the same kind or stamp, as a suit of cards.
Retinue; a company or number of attendants or followers; attendance; train; as a nobleman and his suit.
suitable: Fitting; according with; agreeable to; proper; becoming; as ornaments suitable to one’s character and station; language suitable to the subject.
Adequate. We cannot make suitable returns for divine mercies.
suitableness: Fitness; propriety; agreeableness; a state of being adapted or accommodated. Consider the laws, and their suitableness to our moral state.
suitably: Fitly; agreeably; with propriety. Let words be suitably applied.
suitor: One that sues or prosecutes a demand of right in law, as a plaintiff, petitioner or appellant.
One who attends a court, whether plaintiff, defendant, petitioner, appellant, witness, juror and the like. These, in legal phraseology, are all included in the word suitors.
A petitioner; an applicant. She hath been a suitor to me for her brother.
One who solicits a woman in marriage; a wooer; a lover.
sulcate: In botany, furrowed; grooved; scored with deep broad channels longitudinally; as a sulcated stem.
sulcated: In botany, furrowed; grooved; scored with deep broad channels longitudinally; as a sulcated stem.
sulky: Sullen; sour; heavy; obstinate; morose. While these animals remain in their inclosures, they are sulky.
A carriage for a single person.
sullage: A drain of filth, or filth collected from the street or highway.
sullen: Gloomily angry and silent; cross; sour; affected with ill humor. And sullen I forsook th’ imperfect feast.
Mischievous; malignant. Such sullen planets at my birth did shine.
Obstinate; intractable. Things are as sullen as we are.
Gloomy; dark; dismal. Why are thine eyes fix’d to the sullen earth? Night with her sullen wings. No cheerful breeze this sullen region knows.
Heavy; dull; sorrowful. Be thou the trumpet of our wrath, And sullen presage of your own decay.
sullenness: Ill nature with silence; silent moroseness; gloominess; malignity; intractableness.
sully: To soil; to dirt; to spot; to tarnish. And statues sullied yet with sacrilegious smoke.
To tarnish; to darken. Let there be no spots to sully the brightness of this solemnity.
To stain; to tarnish; as the purity of reputation; as virtues sullied by slanders; character sullied by infamous vices.
To be soiled or tarnished. Silvering will sully and canker more than gilding.
Soil, tarnish; spot. A noble and triumphant merit breaks through little spots and sullies on his reputation.
sulphate: A neutral salt formed by sulphuric acid in combination with any base; as sulphate of lime.
sulphite: A salt or definite compound formed by a combination of sulphurous acid with a base.
sulphur: A simple combustible mineral substance, of a yellow color, brittle, insoluble in water, but fusible by heat. It is called also brimstone, that is, burn-stone, from its great combustibility. It burns with a blue flame and a peculiar suffocating odor. Sulphur native or prismatic is of two kinds, common and volcanic.
sulphurate: Belonging to sulphur; of the color of sulphur.
sulphurated: Combined or impregnated with sulphur; as sulphurated hydrogen gas.
sulphuration: Act of addressing or anointing with sulphur.
sulphore: A combination of sulphur with a metallic, earthy or alkaline base; as a sulphuret of potash.
sulphuret: A combination of sulphur with a metallic, earthy or alkaline base; as a sulphuret of potash.
sulphureous: Consisting of sulphur; having the qualities of sulphur or brimstone; impregnated with sulphur. Her snakes untied, sulphureous waters drink.
sulphureted: Applied to gaseous bodies holding sulphur in solution; as sulphureted hydrogen.
sulphuric: Pertaining to sulphur; more strictly, designating an acid formed by sulphur saturated with oxygen; as sulphuric acid, formerly called vitriolic acid, or oil of vitrol.
sulphurous: Like sulphur; containing sulphur; also, designating an acid formed by sulphur subsaturated with oxygen. This is called sulphurous acid.
sulphur-wort: A plant, hog’s fennel, of the genus Peucedanum.
sulphury: Partaking of sulphur; having the qualities of sulphur.
sultan: An appellation given to the emperor of the Turks, denoting ruler or commander.
sultana: The queen of a sultan; the empress of the Turks.
sultaness: The queen of a sultan; the empress of the Turks.
sultan-flower: A plant, a species of Centaurea.
sultanry: An eastern empire; the dominions of a sultan.
sultriness: The state of being sultry; heat with a moist or close air.
sultry: Very hot, burning and oppressive; as Libya’s sultry deserts.

Very hot and moist, or hot, close, stagnant and unelastic; as air or the atmosphere. A sultry air is usually enfeebling and oppressive to the human body. Such as born beneath the burning sky . And sultry sun, betwixt the tropics lie.
sum: The aggregate of two or more numbers, magnitudes, quantities or particulars; the amount or whole of any number of individuals or particulars added. The sum of 5 and 7 is 12. How precious are thy thoughts to me, O God! how great is the sum of them! Psalm 139:17. Take the sum of all the congregation. Numbers 1:2.
A quantity of money or currency; any amount indefinitely. I sent him a sum of money, a small sum, or a large sum. I received a large sum in bank notes.

Compendium; abridgment; the amount; the substance. This is the sum of all the evidence in the case. This is the sum and substance of all his objections. The sum of all I have said is this. The phrase, in sum, is obsolete or nearly so. In sum, the gospel considered as a law, prescribes every virtue to our conduct, and forbids every sin.
Highth; completion. Thus have I told thee all my state, and brought . My story to the sum of earthly bliss.

To add particulars into one whole; to collect two or more particular numbers into one number; to cast up; usually followed by up, but it is superfluous. Custom enables a man to sum up a long column of figures with surprising facility and correctness. The hour doth rather sum up the moments, than divide the day.
sumac: shu’mak. A plant or shrub of the genus Rhus, of many species, some of which are used in tanning and dyeing, and in medicine.
sumach: shu’mak. A plant or shrub of the genus Rhus, of many species, some of which are used in tanning and dyeing, and in medicine.
sumless: Not to be computed; of which the amount cannot be ascertained. The sumless treasure of exhausted mines.
summarily: In a summary manner; briefly; concisely; in a narrow compass or in few words. The Lord’s prayer teaches us summarily the things we are to ask for.
In a short way or method. When the parties proceed summarily, and they choose the ordinary way of proceeding, the cause is made plenary.
summary: Reduced into a narrow compass, or into few words; short; brief; concise; compendious; as a summary statement of arguments or objections; a summary proceeding or process.
An abridged account; an abstract, abridgment or compendium, containing the sum or substance of a fuller account; as the comprehensive summary of our duty to God in the first table of the law.
summed: Collected into a total amount; fully grown, as feathers.
summer: One who casts up an account.

With us, the season of the year comprehended in the months June, July and August; during which time, the sun being north of the equator, shines more directly upon this part of the earth, which, together with the increased length of the days, renders this the hottest period of the year. In latitudes south of the equator, just the opposite takes place, or it is summer there when it is winter here. The entire year is also sometimes divided into summer and winter, the former signifying the warmer and the latter the colder part of the year.
To pass the summer or warm season. The fowls shall summer upon them. Isaiah 18:6.
A large stone, the first that is laid over columns and pilasters, beginning to make a cross vault; or a stone laid over a column, and hollowed to receive the first haunce of a platband.
summer-colt: The undulating state of the air near the surface of the ground when heated.
summer-cypress: A plant, a species of Chenopodium.
summer-fallow: Naked fallow; land lying bare of crops in summer.
To plow and work repeatedly in summer, to prepare for wheat or other crop.
summer-house: A house or apartment in a garden to be used in summer.
summerset: A high leap in which the heels are thrown over the head.
summit: The top; the highest point; as the summit of a mountain.
The highest point or degree; utmost elevation. The general arrived to the summit of human fame.
summon: To call, cite or notify by authority to appear at a place specified, or to attend in person to some public duty, or both; as, to summon a jury; to summon witnesses. The parliament is summoned by the king’s writ or letter. Nor trumpets summon him to war.
To give notice to a person to appear in court and defend.
To call or command. Love, duty, safety summon us away.
To call up; to excite into action or exertion; with up. Summon up all your strength or courage. Stiffen the sinews, summon up the blood.
summoned: Admonished or warned by authority to appear or attend to something; called or cited by authority.
summoner: One who summons or cites by authority. In England, the sheriff's messenger, employed to warn persons to appear in court.
summoning: Citing by authority to appear or attend to something.
summons: with a plural termination, but used in the singular number; as a summons is prepared. A call by authority or the command of a superior to appear at a place named, or to attend to some public duty.z This summons he resolved not to disobey. He sent to summon the seditious and to offer pardon; but neither summons nor pardon was regarded.

In law, a warning or citation to appear in court; or a written notification signed by the proper officer, to be served on a person, warning him to appear in court at a day specified, to answer to the demand of the plaintiff.
sump: In metallurgy, a round pit of stone, lined with clay, for receiving the metal on its first fusion.
A pond of water reserved for salt-works.
In mining, a pit sunk below the bottom of the mine.
sumpter: A horse that carries clothes or furniture; a baggage-horse; usually called a pack-horse.
sumptuary: Relating to expense. Sumptuary laws or regulations are such as restrain or limit the expenses of citizens in apparel, food, furnitureSumptuary laws are abridgments of liberty, and of very difficult execution. They can be justified only on the ground of extreme necessity.
sumptuous: Costly; expensive; hence, splendid; magnificent; as a sumptuous house or table; sumptuous apparel. We are too magnificent and sumptuous in our tables and attendance.
sumptuousness: Costliness; expensiveness. I will not fall out with those who can reconcile sumptuousness and charity.
sun: The splendid orb or luminary which, being in or near the center of our system of worlds, gives light and heat to all the planets. The light of the sun constitutes the day, and the darkness which proceeds form its absence, or the shade of the earth, constitutes the night. Psalm 136:8.
In popular usage, a sunny place; a place where the beams of the sun fall; as, to stand in the sun, that is, to stand where the direct rays of the sun fall.

Any thing eminently splendid or luminous; that which is the chief source of light or honor. The natives of America complain that the sun of their glory is set. I will never consent to put out the sun of sovereignty to posterity.
In Scripture, Christ is called the sun of righteousness, as the source of light, animation and comfort to his disciples.

The luminary or orb which constitutes the center of any system of worlds. The fixed stars are supposed to be suns in their respective systems. Under the sun, in the world; on earth; a proverbial expression. There is no new thing under the sun. Ecclesiastes 1:9.
sunbeam: A ray of the sun. Truth written with a sunbeam, is truth made obviously plain. Gliding through the even on a sunbeam.
sun-beat: Struck by the sun’s rays; shone brightly on.
sun-bright: Bright as the sun; like the sun in brightness; as a sun- bright shield; a sun-bright chariot.
Bright as the sun; like the sun in brightness; as a sun-bright shield; a sun-bright chariot. How and which way I may bestow myself . To be regarded in her sun-bright eye.
sun-burning: The burning or tan occasioned by the rays of the sun on the skin.
sun-burnt: Discolored by the heat or rays of the sun; tanned; darkened in hue; as a sunburnt skin. Sunburnt and swarthy though she be.
Scorched by the sun’s rays; as a sunburnt soil.
sunday: The christian sabbath; the first day of the week, a day consecrated to rest from secular employments, and to religious worship. It is called also the Lord’s day. Many pious persons however discard the use of Sunday, and call the day the sabbath.
sunder: To part; to separate; to divide; to disunite in almost any manner, either by rending, cutting, or breaking; as, to sunder a rope or cord; to sunder a limb or joint; to sunder friends, or the ties of friendship. The executioner sunders the head from the body at a stroke. A mountain may be sundered by an earthquake. Bring me lightning, give me thunder; . --Jove may kill, but ne’er shall sunder.
In sunder, in tow. He cutteth the spear in sunder. Psalm 46:9.
sun-dew: A plant of the genus Drosera.
sun-dial: An instrument to show the time of day, by means of the shadow of a gnomon or style on a plate.
sun-dried: Dried in the rays of the sun.
sundry: Several; divers; more than one or two. I have composed sundry collects. Sundry foes the rural realm surround.
sunfish: A name of the diodon, a genus of fishes of a very singular form, appearing like the fore part of the body of a very deep fish amputated in the middle. The sunfish is the Tetraodon mola of Linne.
sunflower: A plant of the genus Helianthus; so called from the form and color of its flower, or from its habit of turning to the sun. The bastard sunflower is of the genus Helenium; the dwarf sunflower is of the genus Rudbeckia, and another of the genus Tetragonotheca; the little sunflower is of the genus Cistus.
sung: and pp. of sing. While to his harp divine Amphion sung.
sunk: and pp. of sink. Or toss’d by hope, or sunk by care.
sunless: Destitute of the sun or its rays; shaded.
sunny: Proceeding from the sun; as sunny beams.
Exposed to the rays of the sun; warmed by the direct rays of the sun; as the sunny side of a hill or building. SUNN Y. Her blooming mountains and her sunny shores.
Colored by the sun. Her sunny locks . Hang on her temples like a golden fleece.
sunproof: Impervious to the rays of the sun.
sunrise: The first appearance of the sun above the horizon in the morning; or more generally, the time of such appearance, whether in fair or cloudy weather.
sunrising: The first appearance of the sun above the horizon in the morning; or more generally, the time of such appearance, whether in fair or cloudy weather.
sunset: The descent of the sun below the horizon; or the time when the sun sets; evening.
sunsetting: The descent of the sun below the horizon; or the time when the sun sets; evening.
sunshine: The light of the sun, or the place where it shines; the direct rays of the sun, or the place where they fall. But all sunshine, as when his beams at noon . Culminate from th’ equator.
A place warmed and illuminated; warmth; illumination. The man that sits within a monarch’s heart, And ripens in the sunshine of his favor.
Bright with the rays of the sun; clear, warm or pleasant; as a sunshiny day; sunshiny weather.
Bright like the sun. --Flashing beams of that sunshiny shield.
sunshiny: Bright with the rays of the sun; clear, warm or pleasant; as a sunshiny day; sunshiny weather.
Bright like the sun. --Flashing beams of that sunshiny shield.
sup: To take into the mouth with the lips, as a liquid; to take or drink by a little ata time; to sip.
To take into the mouth with the lips, as a liquid; to take or drink by a little at a time; to sip. There I'll sup . Balm and nectar in my cup.
To eat the evening meal. When they had supped, they brought Tobias in.
To treat with supper. Sup them well.
A small mouthful, as of liquor or broth; a little taken with the lips; a sip. Tom Thumb got a little sup.
super: a Latin preposition, Gr. signifies above, overIt is much used in composition.
superable: That may be overcome or conquered. These are superable difficulties.
superableness: The quality of being conquerable or surmountable.
superabound: To be very abundant or exuberant; to be more than sufficient. The country super-abounds with corn.
superabounding: A bounding beyond want or necessity; abundant to excess or a great degree.
superabundance: More than enough; excessive abundance; as a superabundance of the productions of the earth.
superabundant: Abounding to excess; being more than is sufficient; as superabundant zeal.
superadd: To add over and above; to add to what has been added.
To add or annex something extrinsic. The strength of a living creature, in those external motions, is something distinct from and superadded to its natural gravity.
superadding: Adding over and above; adding something extrinsic.
superaddition: The act of adding to something, or of adding something extraneous.
That which is added. This superaddition is nothing but fat.
superadvenient: Coming upon; coming to the increase or assistance of something. When a man has done bravely by the superadvenient assistance of his God-- .
superangelic: Superior in nature or rank to the angels. One class of Unitarians believe Christ to be a superangelic being.
superannuate: t To impair or disqualify by old age and infirmity; as a superannuated magistrate.
superannuated: Impaired or disqualified by old age.
superannuation: The state of being too old for office of business, or of being disqualified by old age.
superb: Grand; magnificent; as a superb’ edifice; a superb colonnade.
Rich; elegant; as superb furniture or decorations.
Showy; pompous; as a superb exhibition.
Rich; splendid; as a superb entertainment.
superbly: In a magnificent or splendid manner; richly; elegantly.
supercargo: An officer or person in a merchant's ship, whose business is to manage the sales and superintend all the commercial concerns of the voyage.
supercelestial: Situated above the firmament or great vault of heaven.
superciliary: Situated or being above the eyebrow. The superciliary arch, is the bony superior arch of the orbit.
supercilious: Lofty with pride; haughty; dictatorial; overbearing; as a supercilious officer.
Manifesting haughtiness, or proceeding from it; overbearing; as a supercilious air; supercilious behavior.
superciliously: Haughtily; dogmatically; with an air of contempt.
superciliousness: Haughtiness; an overbearing temper or manner.
superconception: A conception after a former conception.
supercrescence: That which grows upon another growing thing.
supercrescent: Growing on some other growing thing.
supereminence: Eminence superior to what is common; distinguished eminence; as the supereminence of Cicero as an orator; the supereminence of Dr. Johnson as a writer, or of lord Chatham as a statesman.
supereminency: Eminence superior to what is common; distinguished eminence; as the supereminence of Cicero as an orator; the supereminence of Dr. Johnson as a writer, or of lord Chatham as a statesman.
supereminent: Eminent in a superior degree; surpassing others in excellence; as a supereminent divine; the supereminent glory of Christ.
supereminently: In a superior degree of excellence; with unusual distinction.
supererogate: To do more than duty requires. Aristotle’s followers have supererogated in observance.
supererogation: Performance of more than duty requires. There is no such thing as works of supererogation.
supererogatory: Performed to an extent not enjoined or not required by duty; as supererogatory services.
superessential: Essential above others, or above the constitution of a thing.
superexalt: To exalt to a superior degree.
superexcellent: Excellent in an uncommon degree; very excellent.
superfecundity: Superabundant fecundity or multiplication of the species.
superfetate: To conceive after a prior conception. The female is said to superfetate.
superfetation: A second conception after a prior one, and before the birth of the first, by which two fetuses are growing at once in the same matrix.
superfete: To conceive after a former conception.
superficial: Being on the surface; not penetrating the substance of a thing; as a superficial color; a superficial covering.
Composing the surface or exterior part; as, soil constitutes the superficial part of the earth.
Shallow; contrived to cover something. This superficial tale . Is but a preface to her worthy praise.
Shallow; not deep or profound; reaching or comprehending only what is obvious or apparent; as a superficial scholar; superficial knowledge.
superficially: On the surface only; as a substance superficially tinged with a color.
On the surface or exterior part only; without penetrating the substance or essence; as, to survey things superficially.
Without going deep or searching things to the bottom; slightly. He reasons superficially. I have laid down superficially my present thoughts.
superficialness: Slight knowledge; shallowness of observation or learning; show without substance.
superficies: The surface; the exterior part of a thing. A superficies consists of length and breadth; as the superficies of a plate or of a sphere. Superficies is rectilinear, curvilinear, plane, convex or concave.
superfine: Very fine or most fine; surpassing others in fineness; as superfine cloth. The word is chiefly used of cloth, but sometimes of liquors; as superfine wine or cider; and of other things, as superfine wire; superfine flour.
superfluitance: The act of floating above or on the surface.
superfluitant: Floating above or on the surface.
superfluity: Superabundance; a greater quantity than is wanted; as a superfluity of water or provisions.
Something that is beyond what is wanted; something rendered unnecessary by its abundance. Among the superfluities of life we seldom number the abundance of money.
superfluous: More than is wanted; rendered unnecessary by superabundance; as a superfluous supply of corn.

More than sufficient; unnecessary; useless; as a composition abounding with superfluous words. Superfluous epithets rather enfeeble than strengthen description. If wnat has been said will not convince, it would be superfluous to say more. Superfluous interval, in music, is one that exceeds a true diatonic interval by a semitone minor. Superfluous polygamy, (Polygamia superflua,) a kind of inflorescence or compound flower, in which the florets of the disk are hermaphrodite and fertile, and those of the ray, though female or pistiliferous only, are also fertile; designating the second order of the class Syngenesia of Linne. Superfluous sound or tone, is one which contains a semitone minor more than a tone.
superfluously: With excess; in a degree beyond what is necessary.
superfluousness: The state of being superfluous or beyond what is wanted. SUPERFLUxThat which is more than is wanted.
superhuman: Above or beyond what is human; divine.
superimpose: superimpo’ze. To lay or impose on something else; as a stratum of earth superimposed on a different stratum.
superimposition: The act of laying or the state of being placed on something else.
superimpregnation: The act of impregnating upon a prior impregnation; impregnation when previously impregnated.
superincumbent: Lying or resting on something else.
superinduce: To bring in or upon as an addition to something; as, to superinduce a virtue or quality upon a person not before possessing it. Long custom of sinning superinduces upon the soul new and absurd desires.
superinduction: The act of superinducing. The superinduction of ill habits quickly defaces the first rude draught of virtue.
superinspect: To oversee; to superintend by inspection.
superinstitution: One institution upon another; as when A is instituted and admitted to a benefice upon a title, and B is instituted and admitted upon the presentation of another.
superintend: To have or exercise the charge and oversight of; to oversee with the power of direction; to take care of with authority; as, an officer superintends the building of a ship or the construction of a fort. God exercises a superintending care over all his creatures.
superintendence: The act of superintending; care and oversight for the purpose of direction and with authority to direct.
superintendency: The act of superintending; care and oversight for the purpose of direction and with authority to direct.
superintendent: One who has the oversight and charge of something, with the power of direction; as the superintendent of an alms-house or work-house; the superintendent of public works; the superintendent of customs or finance.
An ecclesiastical superior in some reformed churches.
superintending: Overseeing with the authority to direct what shall be done and how it shall be done.
superior: Higher; upper; more elevated in place; as the superior limb of the sun; the superior part of an image.
Higher in rank or office; more exalted in dignity; as a superior officer; a superior degree of nobility: .

Higher or greater in excellence; surpassing others in the greatness, goodness or value of any quality; as a man of superior merit, of superior bravery, of superior talents or understanding, of superior accomplishments.

Being beyond the power or influence of; too great or firm to be subdued or affected by; as a man superior to revenge. There is not on earth a spectacle more worthy than a great man superior to his sufferings.
In botany, a superior flower has the receptacle of the flower above the germ; a superior germ is included within the corol.
superiority: Pre-eminence; the quality of being more advanced or higher, greater or more excellent than another in any respect; as superiority of age, of rank or dignity, of attainments or excellence. The superiority of others in fortune and rank, is more readily acknowledged than superiority of understanding.
superlation: Exaltation of any thing beyond truth or propriety.
superlative: Highest in degree; most eminent; surpassing all other; as a man of superlative wisdom or prudence, of superlative worth; a woman of superlative beauty.
Supreme; as the superlative glory of the divine character.
In grammar, expressing the highest or utmost degree; as the superlative degree of comparison.
In grammar, the superlative degree of adjectives, which is formed by the termination est, as meanest, highest, bravest; or by the use of most, as most high, most brave; or by least, as least amiable.
superlatively: In a manner expressing the utmost degree. I shall not speak superlatively of them.
In the highest or utmost degree. Tiberius was superlatively wicked; Clodius was superlatively profligate.
superlativeness: The state of being in the highest degree.
superlunar: Being above the moon; not sublunary or of this world. The head that turns at superlunar things.
superlunary: Being above the moon; not sublunary or of this world. The head that turns at superlunar things.
supernal: Being in a higher place or region; locally higher; as the supernal orbs; supernal regions.
Relating to things above; celestial; heavenly; as supernal grace. Not by the sufferings of supernal pow’r.
supernatant: Swimming above; floating on the surface; as oil supernatant on water.
supernatation: The act of floating on the surface of a fluid.
supernatural: Being beyond or exceeding the powers or laws of nature; miraculous. A supernatural event is one which is not produced according to the ordinary or established laws of natural things. Thus if iron has more specific gravity than water, it will sink in that fluid; and the floating of iron on water must be a supernatural event. Now no human being can alter a law of nature; the floating of iron on water therefore must be caused by divine power specially exerted to suspend, in this instance, a law of nature. Hence supernatural events or miracles can be produced only by the immediate agency of divine power.
supernaturally: In a manner exceeding the established course or laws of nature. The prophets must have been supernaturally taught or enlightened, for their predictions were beyond human fore-knowledge.
supernaturalness: The state or quality of being beyond the power or ordinary laws of nature.
supernumerary: Exceeding the number stated or prescribed; as a supernumerary officer in a regiment; a supernumerary canon in the church.
Exceeding a necessary, a usual or a round number; as supernumerary addresses; supernumerary expense.
A person or thing beyond the number stated, or beyond what is necessary or usual. On the reduction of the regiments, several supernumeraries were to be provided for.
superparticular: Noting a ratio when the excess of the greater term is a unit; as the ratio of 1 to 2, or of 3 to 4.
superpartient: Noting a ratio when the excess of the greater term is more than a unit; as that of 3 to 5, or of 7 to 10.
superplant: A plant growing on another plant; as the mistletoe.
superplusage: That which is more than enough; excess.
superpose: superpo’ze. To law upon, as one kind of rock on another.
superposition: A placing above; a lying or being situated above or upon something; as the superposition of rocks.
That which is situated above or upon something else.
superpurgation: More purgation than is sufficient. I
superreflection: The reflection of an image reflected.
superroyal: Larger than royal; denoting the largest species of printing paper.
supersaliency: The act of leaping on any thing.
supersalt: In chimistry, a salt with an excess of acid, as supertartrate of potash.
supersaturation: The operation of saturating to excess; or the state of being thus saturated.
superscribe: To write or engrave on the top, outside or surface; or to write the name or address of one on the outside or cover; as, to superscribe a letter.
superscribing: Inscribing, writing or engraving on the outside, or on the top.
superscription: That which is written or engraved on the outside, or above something else. The superscription of his accusation was written over, THE KING
An impression of letters on coins. Matthew 22:20.
supersecular: Being above the world or secular things.
supersede: Literally, to set above; hence, to make void, inefficacious or useless by superior power, or by coming in the place of; to set aside; to render unnecessary; to suspend. The use of artillery in making breaches in walls, has superseded the use of the battering ram. The effect of passion is to supersede the workings of reason. Nothing is supposed that can supersede the known laws of natural motion.
To come or be placed in the room of; hence, to displace or render unnecessary; as, an officer is superseded by the appointment of another person.
supersedeas: In law, a writ of supersedeas, is a writ or command to suspend the powers of an officer in certain cases, or to stay proceedings. This writ does not destroy the power of an officer, for it may be revived by another writ called a procedendo.
superseded: Made void; rendered unnecessary or inefficacious; displaced; suspended.
superseding: Coming in the place of; setting aside; rendering useless; displacing; suspending.
supersedure: The act of superseding; as the supersedure of trial by jury.
superserviceable: Over officious; doing more than is required or desired.
superstition: Excessive exactness or rigor in religious opinions or practice; extreme and unnecessary scruples in the observance of religious rites not commanded, or of points of minor importance; excess or extravagance in religion; the doing of things not required by God, or abstaining from things not forbidden; or the belief of what is absurd, or belief without evidence. Superstition has reference to god, to religion, or to beings superior to man.
Rite or practice proceeding from excess of scruples in religion. In this sense, it admits of a plural. They the truth . With superstitions and traditions taint.
Belief in the direct agency of superior powers in certain extraordinary or singular events, or in omens’ and prognostics.
superstitious: Over scrupulous and rigid in religious observances; addicted to superstition; full of idle fancies and scruples in regard to religion; as superstitious people.
Proceeding from superstition; manifesting superstition; as superstitious rites; superstitious observances.
Over exact; scrupulous beyond need. Superstitious use, in law, the use of land for a religious purpose, or by a religious corporation.
superstitiously: In a superstitious manner; with excessive regard to uncommanded rites or unessential opinions and forms in religion.
With too much care; with excessive exactness or scruple.
With extreme credulity in regard to the agency of superior beings in extraordinary events.
superstratum: A stratum or layer above another, or resting on something else.
superstruct: To build upon; to erect. This is the only proper basis on which to superstruct first innocence and then virtue.
superstruction: An edifice erected on something. My own profession hath taught me not to erect new super structions on an old ruin.
superstructive: Built or erected on something else.
superstucture: Any structure or edifice built on something else; particularly, the building raised on a foundation. This word is used to distinguish what is erected on a wall or foundation from the foundation itself.
Any thing erected on a foundation or basis. In education, we begin with teaching languages as the foundation, and proceed to erect on that foundation the superstructure of science.
supersubstantial: More than substantial; being more than substance.
supersulphate: Sulphate with an excess of acid.
supersulphureted: Combined with an excess of sulphur.
superterrene: Being above ground, or above the earth.
superterrestrial: Being above the earth, or above what belongs to the earth.
supertonic: In music, the note next above the key-note.
supervacaneous: Superfluous; unnecessary; needless; serving no purpose.
supervene: To come upon as something extraneous. Such a mutual gravitation can never supervene to matter, unless impressed by divine power.
To come upon; to happen to.
supervenient: Coming upon as something additional or extraneous. That branch of belief was in him supervenient to christian practice. Divorces can be granted, a mensa et toro, only for supervenient causes.
supervision: supervizh’on. The act of overseeing; inspection; superintendence.
supervise: To oversee; to superintend; to inspect; as, to supervise the press for correction.
supervisor: An overseer; an inspector; a superintendent; as the supervisor of a pamphlet.
supervive: To live beyond; to outlive. The soul will supervive all the revolutions of nature.
supination: The act of lying or state of being laid with the face upward.
The act of turning the palm of the hand upwards.
supinator: In anatomy, a muscle that turns the palm of the hand upward.
supine: Lying on the back, or with the face upward; opposed to prone.
Leaning backward; or inclining with exposure to the sun. If the vine . On rising ground be plac’d on hills supine-- .
Negligent; heedless; indolent; thoughtless; inattentive. He became pusillanimous and supine, and openly exposed to any temptation. These men suffer by their supine credulity.
In grammar, a word formed from a verb, or a modification of a verb.
supinely: Carelessly; indolently; drowsily; in a heedless, thoughtless state. Who on beds of sin supinely lie.
supineness: A lying with the face upward.
Indolence; drowsiness; heedlessness. Many of the evils of life are owing to our own supineness.
suppalpation: The act of enticing by soft words.
supparasitation: The act of flattering merely to gain favor.
supper: The evening meal. People who dine late, eat no supper. The dinner of fashionable people would be the supper of rustics.
supperless: Wanting supper; being without supper; as, to go supperless to bed.
supplant: To trip up the heels. Supplanted down he fell.

To remove or displace by stratagem; or to displace and take the place of; as, a rival supplants another in the affections of his mistress, or in the favor of his prince. Suspecting that the courtier had supplanted the friend.
supplanting: Tripping up the heels; displacing by artifice.
supple: Pliant; flexible; easily bent; as supple joints; supple fingers.
Yielding; compliant; not obstinate. If punishment--makes not the will supple, it hardens the offender.
Bending to the humor of others; flattering; fawning.
That makes plaint; as supple government.
To make soft and pliant; to render flexible; as, to supple leather.
suppled: Made soft and plaint; made compliant.
supplement: Literally, a supply; hence, an addition to any thing by which its defects are supplied, and it is made more full and complete. The word is particularly used of an addition to a book or paper.
In trigonometry, the quantity by which an arc or an angle falls short of 180 degrees or a semicircle.
supplemental: Additional; added to supply what is wanted; as a supplemental law or bill.
supplementary: Additional; added to supply what is wanted; as a supplemental law or bill.
suppleness: Pliancy; pliableness; flexibility; the quality of being easily bent; as the suppleness of the joints.
Readiness of compliance; the quality of easily yielding; facility; as the suppleness of the will.
suppletory: Supplying deficiencies; as a suppletory oath.
That which is to supply what is wanted.
suppliant: Entreating; beseeching; supplicating; asking earnestly and submissively. The rich grow suppliant, and the poor grow proud.
Manifesting entreaty; expressive of humble supplication. To bow and sue for grace with suppliant knee.
A humble petitioner; one who entreats submissively. Spare this life, and hear thy suppliant’s pray’r.
suppliantly: In a suppliant or submissive manner.
supplicant: One that entreats; a petitioner who asks earnestly and submissively. The wise supplicant--left the event to God.
supplicate: To entreat for; to seek by earnest prayer; as, to supplicate blessings on christian efforts to spread the gospel.
To address in prayer; as, to supplicate the throne of grace.
To entreat; to beseech; to implore; to petition with earnestness and submission. A man cannot brook to supplicate or beg.
supplication: Entreaty; humble and earnest prayer in worship. In all our supplications to the Father of mercies, let us remember a world lying in ignorance and wickedness.
In Roman antiquity, a religious solemnity observed in consequence of some military success. It consisted in sacrifices, feasting, offering thanks, and praying for a continuance of success.
supply: To fill up, as any deficiency happens; to furnish what is wanted; to afford or furnish a sufficiency; as, to supply the poor with bread and clothing; to supply the daily wants of nature; to supply the navy with masts and spars; to supply the treasury with money. The city is well supplied with water. I wanted nothing fortune could supply.
To serve instead of. Burning ships the banish’d sun supply.
To give; to bring or furnish. Nearer care supplies . Signs to my breast, and sorrow to my eyes.
